topics.title: Verification of Kirchoff's Laws
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCSFL408 - ELECTRICAL MACHINES LABORATORY
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Verification of Kirchoff's Laws

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Kirchhoff's Laws** are two fundamental circuit analysis rules formulated by German physicist **Gustav Robert Kirchhoff (1824–1887)**. They form the mathematical backbone of all network analysis in electrical engineering and are universally applied to both DC and AC circuits when valid lumped-element conditions are satisfied.

**Kirchhoff's Current Law (KCL) — The Junction Rule:**

> [!NOTE]
> **KCL Statement:** The algebraic sum of all currents entering and leaving any node (junction) in an electrical network is equal to zero at every instant of time.
> $$\sum_{k=1}^{n} I_k(t) = 0$$
> Equivalently: *Sum of currents entering a node = Sum of currents leaving the node.*

**Kirchhoff's Voltage Law (KVL) — The Loop Rule:**

> [!NOTE]
> **KVL Statement:** The algebraic sum of all electromotive forces (EMFs) and voltage drops around any closed loop in an electrical network is equal to zero at every instant of time.
> $$\sum_{k=1}^{m} V_k(t) = 0$$
> Equivalently: *Sum of voltage rises = Sum of voltage drops around a closed loop.*

Both laws are direct consequences of two fundamental physical conservation principles:
- **KCL** is derived from the **Law of Conservation of Charge** (charge can neither accumulate nor vanish at a node in steady state).
- **KVL** is derived from the **Law of Conservation of Energy** (the electrostatic field is conservative).

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Intuitive Overview

**Analogy for KCL — The "Water-Pipe Junction" Model:**

> [!TIP]
> Imagine a junction where multiple water pipes meet. The total water flowing *into* the junction per second must equal the total water flowing *out* per second. Water cannot accumulate at the junction indefinitely, nor can it mysteriously appear from nowhere. The electric current behaves identically at an electrical node — it represents **charge flow per unit time**.

**Analogy for KVL — The "Hill-and-Valley Hike" Model:**

> [!TIP]
> Imagine a hiker returning to their starting point after walking around a closed loop in hilly terrain. The total altitude gained while climbing hills **must exactly equal** the total altitude lost while descending valleys, because the hiker ends up at the *same elevation*. Similarly, in a closed electrical loop, the total voltage gained across batteries **must exactly equal** the total voltage dropped across resistors.

### 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

| Quantity | Standard Unit | Symbol |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Current | Ampere (A) | $I$ |
| Voltage / EMF | Volt (V) | $V$ |
| Resistance | Ohm ($\Omega$) | $R$ |
| Charge | Coulomb (C) | $Q$ |
| Power | Watt (W) | $P$ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Validity Conditions:** Kirchhoff's Laws hold strictly when the circuit dimensions are **much smaller than the wavelength** of the operating signal (lumped element assumption). For very high-frequency circuits (microwave/RF), transmission-line effects invalidate these laws.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Step-by-Step Theoretical Breakdown

#### 2.1.1 Origin of KCL — Derivation from Charge Conservation

1. **Define a node (junction)** as a point in the circuit where three or more circuit elements meet.
2. By the **Law of Conservation of Charge**, the total charge entering the node per unit time equals the total charge leaving.
3. Mathematically, the net rate of change of charge at the node is zero:
$$\frac{dQ_{\text{node}}}{dt} = \sum_{k=1}^{n} I_k = 0$$
4. This is the integral form of **Maxwell's Continuity Equation** applied to a lumped-parameter network.

#### 2.1.2 Origin of KVL — Derivation from Energy Conservation

1. **Define a closed loop** as any closed conducting path in the circuit.
2. The **Electrostatic Field** is conservative, meaning the line integral around any closed path is zero:
$$\oint \vec{E} \cdot d\vec{l} = 0$$
3. Converting to circuit quantities (voltage = work per unit charge):
$$\sum_{k=1}^{m} V_k = 0$$
4. This implies that **no net energy is gained or lost** by a charge after completing one full traversal of the loop.

#### 2.1.3 Sign Convention — The Critical KTU Valuation Point

> [!WARNING]
> **Sign Convention is the single most important reason students lose marks in KTU exams.** The sign of each term depends on the arbitrarily chosen direction of traversal.

**Standard Convention Rules:**
- **Current:** Positive (+) if entering the node; Negative (−) if leaving the node.
- **Voltage Drop across Resistor (V = IR):** Positive if current direction *matches* the assumed loop traversal direction; Negative otherwise.
- **EMF Source (Battery):** Positive if traversed from **− to +** terminal (rise in potential); Negative if traversed from **+ to −** (drop in potential).

### 2.2 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet / Master Table

| # | Law | Mathematical Statement | SI Units | Physical Basis | Typical Application |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | **KCL (Junction Rule)** | $\sum I_{\text{enter}} = \sum I_{\text{leave}}$ or $\sum I_k = 0$ | Ampere (A) | Conservation of Charge | Nodal Analysis, Parallel Circuits |
| 2 | **KVL (Loop Rule)** | $\sum V_{\text{rise}} = \sum V_{\text{drop}}$ or $\sum V_k = 0$ | Volt (V) | Conservation of Energy | Mesh Analysis, Series Circuits |
| 3 | **Ohm's Law** | $V = IR$ | V, A, $\Omega$ | Material Resistance Property | Both Laws (for voltage drop calculation) |
| 4 | **Power Dissipation** | $P = I^2 R = VI = \dfrac{V^2}{R}$ | Watt (W) | Energy Conversion | Verifying experimental results |
| 5 | **Equivalent Resistance (Series)** | $R_{\text{eq}} = R_1 + R_2 + \cdots + R_n$ | $\Omega$ | Series Combination | Loop Simplification |
| 6 | **Equivalent Resistance (Parallel)** | $\dfrac{1}{R_{\text{eq}}} = \dfrac{1}{R_1} + \dfrac{1}{R_2} + \cdots + \dfrac{1}{R_n}$ | $\Omega$ | Parallel Combination | Node Simplification |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** The equation $\sum I_k = 0$ uses *algebraic* sum — meaning each current is assigned a sign based on a *chosen reference direction*. Forgetting to assign signs correctly is the most frequent error.

### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

Kirchhoff's Laws are not just academic — they are indispensable in:

- **Power System Network Analysis:** Load-flow studies, fault analysis, and protection coordination.
- **Electronic Circuit Design:** SPICE simulation engines (used in industry) fundamentally solve the system using nodal KCL and mesh KVL matrices.
- **PCB Design:** Signal-integrity analysis, ground-loop analysis, and power-distribution network design.
- **Automotive Electronics:** Modern vehicles contain thousands of nodes; CAN-bus diagnostics rely on equivalent-circuit models.
- **Telecommunications:** Filter design, impedance matching, and amplifier analysis.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Formal Derivation of KCL from Maxwell's Continuity Equation

The Maxwell continuity equation in differential form states:

$$\nabla \cdot \vec{J} = -\frac{\partial \rho}{\partial t}$$

Where $\vec{J}$ is the current density (A/m²) and $\rho$ is the volume charge density (C/m³).

**Step 1:** Apply the **divergence theorem** to convert the volume integral into a closed surface integral:

$$\oint_S \vec{J} \cdot d\vec{S} = -\frac{d}{dt}\int_V \rho \, dV$$

**Step 2:** Recognize that the left-hand side is the **net current flowing outward** through any closed surface S. Since a circuit node is a closed surface with all current-carrying wires piercing it:

$$\sum_{k=1}^{n} I_k^{\text{out}} = -\frac{dQ_{\text{enclosed}}}{dt}$$

**Step 3:** For a lumped-parameter circuit node, no charge accumulates at the node (the node is infinitesimally small):

$$\frac{dQ_{\text{enclosed}}}{dt} = 0$$

**Step 4:** Substituting this into Step 2 yields KCL:

$$\sum_{k=1}^{n} I_k^{\text{out}} = 0 \quad \Longrightarrow \quad \sum_{k=1}^{n} I_k^{\text{in}} = \sum_{k=1}^{n} I_k^{\text{out}}$$

This is the **rigorous mathematical proof** of KCL from first principles.

### 3.2 Formal Derivation of KVL from Faraday's Law

Faraday's Law of Electromagnetic Induction states:

$$\oint_C \vec{E} \cdot d\vec{l} = -\frac{d}{dt}\int_S \vec{B} \cdot d\vec{S}$$

**Step 1:** For a lumped-parameter circuit operating in the **quasi-static regime**, the magnetic flux $\Phi$ through any loop is negligible (or constant for DC):

$$\frac{d\Phi}{dt} \approx 0$$

**Step 2:** Therefore, the line integral of the electric field around any closed loop C is zero:

$$\oint_C \vec{E} \cdot d\vec{l} = 0$$

**Step 3:** In circuit terms, the line integral corresponds to the sum of all voltage drops and rises around the loop:

$$\sum_{k=1}^{m} V_k = 0$$

This is the **rigorous mathematical proof** of KVL from Maxwell's equations under the lumped-element assumption.

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Worked Numerical Example

**Problem:** Consider the following DC circuit with two batteries and three resistors connected as shown. Given: $V_1 = 10$ V, $V_2 = 6$ V, $R_1 = 4\ \Omega$, $R_2 = 2\ \Omega$, $R_3 = 6\ \Omega$. Verify Kirchhoff's Laws by calculating all branch currents.

**Step 1 — Identify Nodes:** Two nodes — Node A and Node B.
**Step 2 — Identify Loops:** Two independent loops — Loop 1 (left) and Loop 2 (right).
**Step 3 — Assign Mesh Currents:** Let $I_1$ flow clockwise in Loop 1, and $I_2$ flow clockwise in Loop 2. Branch current through $R_3$ = $(I_1 - I_2)$.

**Step 4 — Apply KVL to Loop 1 (clockwise from Node A):**

$$\begin{aligned}
V_1 - I_1 R_1 - (I_1 - I_2) R_3 &= 0 \\
10 - 4I_1 - 6(I_1 - I_2) &= 0 \\
10 - 4I_1 - 6I_1 + 6I_2 &= 0 \\
-10I_1 + 6I_2 &= -10 \\
5I_1 - 3I_2 &= 5 \quad \text{...(Equation 1)}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 5 — Apply KVL to Loop 2 (clockwise from Node B):**

$$\begin{aligned}
-V_2 - (I_2 - I_1) R_3 - I_2 R_2 &= 0 \\
-6 - 6(I_2 - I_1) - 2I_2 &= 0 \\
-6 - 6I_2 + 6I_1 - 2I_2 &= 0 \\
6I_1 - 8I_2 &= 6 \\
3I_1 - 4I_2 &= 3 \quad \text{...(Equation 2)}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 6 — Solve the Simultaneous Equations:**

$$\begin{aligned}
5I_1 - 3I_2 &= 5 \quad \cdots (1) \\
3I_1 - 4I_2 &= 3 \quad \cdots (2)
\end{aligned}$$

Multiply Equation (1) by 4 and Equation (2) by 3:

$$\begin{aligned}
20I_1 - 12I_2 &= 20 \quad \cdots (1') \\
9I_1 - 12I_2 &= 9 \quad \cdots (2')
\end{aligned}$$

Subtract Equation (2') from Equation (1'):

$$\begin{aligned}
11I_1 &= 11 \\
I_1 &= 1\ \text{A}
\end{aligned}$$

Substitute $I_1 = 1$ A into Equation (2):

$$\begin{aligned}
3(1) - 4I_2 &= 3 \\
-4I_2 &= 0 \\
I_2 &= 0\ \text{A}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 7 — Verification of KCL at Node A:**

Current entering = $I_1 = 1$ A (from Loop 1 branch)
Current leaving = $(I_1 - I_2) + I_2 = 1 - 0 + 0 = 1$ A

$$\sum I_{\text{entering}} - \sum I_{\text{leaving}} = 1 - 1 = 0 \quad \checkmark \text{ KCL Verified}$$

**Step 8 — Verification of KVL in Loop 1:**

$$\begin{aligned}
\sum V &= V_1 - I_1 R_1 - (I_1 - I_2) R_3 \\
&= 10 - (1)(4) - (1 - 0)(6) \\
&= 10 - 4 - 6 \\
&= 0 \quad \checkmark \text{ KVL Verified}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 9 — Verification of KVL in Loop 2:**

$$\begin{aligned}
\sum V &= -V_2 - (I_2 - I_1) R_3 - I_2 R_2 \\
&= -6 - (0 - 1)(6) - (0)(2) \\
&= -6 - (-6) - 0 \\
&= 0 \quad \checkmark \text{ KVL Verified}
\end{aligned}$$

### 3.4 Laboratory Verification Procedure (KTU Format)

| Step No. | Procedure | Instrument Used | Expected Outcome |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | Connect the circuit on a breadboard as per the circuit diagram | Breadboard, Connecting wires | Physical circuit assembled |
| 2 | Set the DC regulated power supply to the required voltage | DC Regulated Power Supply | Source voltage confirmed |
| 3 | Measure each branch current using a multimeter | Digital Multimeter (ammeter mode) | Reading in mA / A |
| 4 | Measure each resistor voltage drop using multimeter | Digital Multimeter (voltmeter mode) | Reading in Volts |
| 5 | Tabulate observed values vs theoretical values | Observation Table | Comparison ready |
| 6 | Verify KCL at each node: $\sum I_{\text{in}} - \sum I_{\text{out}} \approx 0$ | Manual Calculation | Verification confirmed |
| 7 | Verify KVL in each loop: $\sum V_{\text{loop}} \approx 0$ | Manual Calculation | Verification confirmed |
| 8 | Calculate percentage error | Calculator | Error < 5% acceptable |

**Percentage Error Formula:**

$$\% \text{Error} = \frac{\vert \text{Theoretical Value} - \text{Practical Value} \vert}{\text{Theoretical Value}} \times 100$$

> [!NOTE]
> Always use `\vert` or `\mid` in your notebook solutions to avoid LaTeX ambiguity. KTU examiners prefer a clear table-formatted observation sheet with proper units.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam — May 2024 | 3 Marks]**

**Q1.** State Kirchhoff's Current Law (KCL) and Kirchhoff's Voltage Law (KVL). Mention the physical principle underlying each.

**Model Answer:**

**Kirchhoff's Current Law (KCL):** The algebraic sum of currents meeting at any node (junction) in an electrical network is zero.
$$\sum_{k=1}^{n} I_k = 0$$
It is based on the **Law of Conservation of Charge** — charge cannot accumulate or vanish at a node.

**Kirchhoff's Voltage Law (KVL):** The algebraic sum of voltages around any closed loop in an electrical network is zero.
$$\sum_{k=1}^{m} V_k = 0$$
It is based on the **Law of Conservation of Energy** — a charge cannot gain or lose energy after completing one loop.

---

> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 | 3 Marks]**

**Q2.** What are the assumptions and limitations of Kirchhoff's Laws? In which type of circuits are they invalid?

**Model Answer:**

Kirchhoff's Laws are valid under the **lumped-parameter assumption**, which requires:
1. Circuit dimensions are much smaller than the operating wavelength.
2. No significant electromagnetic radiation occurs.
3. The magnetic flux linkage through any loop is negligible (quasi-static condition).

**Limitations:**
- **Invalid in high-frequency circuits** (microwave, RF, antennas) where transmission-line effects dominate.
- **Invalid in distributed-parameter networks** where voltage and current vary along the conductor length.
- **Invalid when the circuit is not in the quasi-static regime** (rapidly time-varying fields).

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks with Module Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam — May 2024 | 14 Marks]**

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**(a)** Derive Kirchhoff's Current Law (KCL) from the **Law of Conservation of Charge**. Explain the procedure for verifying KCL experimentally with a suitable DC circuit. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Theoretical Derivation:**

KCL is derived from the **Maxwell Continuity Equation**:

$$\nabla \cdot \vec{J} = -\frac{\partial \rho}{\partial t}$$

Applying the **divergence theorem** to a closed surface S enclosing a node:

$$\oint_S \vec{J} \cdot d\vec{S} = -\frac{d}{dt}\int_V \rho \, dV$$

Since the node is infinitesimal and no charge accumulates:

$$\frac{dQ_{\text{enclosed}}}{dt} = 0$$

Therefore:
$$\sum_{k=1}^{n} I_k = 0$$

This proves KCL rigorously from Maxwell's equations.

**Experimental Procedure:**

| Step | Action |
| :--- | :--- |
| 1 | Construct a circuit with at least one node where three or more branches meet |
| 2 | Insert ammeters in series with each branch |
| 3 | Apply a known DC voltage across the circuit |
| 4 | Record all branch currents (note polarity / direction) |
| 5 | Apply the sign convention: incoming = positive, outgoing = negative |
| 6 | Verify that $\sum I = 0$ within acceptable tolerance |

**(b)** For the circuit given below, calculate all branch currents using KCL and KVL. Verify both laws.

**Given:**
- $V = 12\ \text{V}$, $R_1 = 2\ \Omega$, $R_2 = 4\ \Omega$, $R_3 = 6\ \Omega$
- Circuit: $V$ in series with $R_1$, then a node splits into two parallel branches ($R_2$ and $R_3$) back to the source.

**(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Equivalent Resistance of Parallel Combination:**

$$R_{23} = \frac{R_2 \cdot R_3}{R_2 + R_3} = \frac{4 \times 6}{4 + 6} = \frac{24}{10} = 2.4\ \Omega$$

**Step 2 — Total Resistance:**

$$R_{\text{total}} = R_1 + R_{23} = 2 + 2.4 = 4.4\ \Omega$$

**Step 3 — Total (Source) Current:**

$$I_{\text{total}} = \frac{V}{R_{\text{total}}} = \frac{12}{4.4} = 2.727\ \text{A}$$

**Step 4 — Branch Currents using Current Divider Rule:**

$$I_2 = I_{\text{total}} \cdot \frac{R_3}{R_2 + R_3} = 2.727 \cdot \frac{6}{10} = 1.636\ \text{A}$$

$$I_3 = I_{\text{total}} \cdot \frac{R_2}{R_2 + R_3} = 2.727 \cdot \frac{4}{10} = 1.091\ \text{A}$$

**Step 5 — Verification of KCL at the Parallel Junction:**

$$\begin{aligned}
\sum I_{\text{entering}} &= I_{\text{total}} = 2.727\ \text{A} \\
\sum I_{\text{leaving}} &= I_2 + I_3 = 1.636 + 1.091 = 2.727\ \text{A} \\
\text{Net difference} &= 2.727 - 2.727 = 0 \quad \checkmark \text{ KCL Verified}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 6 — Verification of KVL in Outer Loop:**

$$\begin{aligned}
\sum V &= V - I_{\text{total}} R_1 - I_2 R_2 \\
&= 12 - (2.727)(2) - (1.636)(4) \\
&= 12 - 5.454 - 6.544 \\
&= 0 \quad \checkmark \text{ KVL Verified}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 7 — Verification of KVL in Inner Loop:**

$$\begin{aligned}
\sum V &= I_2 R_2 - I_3 R_3 \\
&= (1.636)(4) - (1.091)(6) \\
&= 6.544 - 6.546 \approx 0 \quad \checkmark \text{ KVL Verified}
\end{aligned}$$

(Tiny discrepancy is due to rounding of $I_{\text{total}}$ to 3 decimal places.)

---

### **Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)**

**(a)** Explain the **sign convention** used while applying Kirchhoff's laws. Why is the consistent choice of reference directions essential? Give examples for both current and voltage signs. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

The **sign convention** is a set of rules that assigns a positive or negative value to each current and voltage based on a chosen reference direction.

**For Currents (KCL):**
- If the assumed direction of current is *toward* the node → **Positive**
- If the assumed direction is *away from* the node → **Negative**
- Example: At a node, if we assume $I_1 = 3\ \text{A}$ entering and $I_2 = 1\ \text{A}$ leaving, then KCL gives: $I_1 - I_2 - I_3 = 0$, hence $I_3 = 2\ \text{A}$ leaving.

**For Voltages (KVL):**
- EMF source traversed from − to + terminal → **Positive (Voltage Rise)**
- EMF source traversed from + to − terminal → **Negative (Voltage Drop)**
- Resistor with current direction matching traversal → **Positive Drop (V = +IR)**
- Resistor with current direction opposite traversal → **Negative (V = −IR)**

**Why It Is Essential:**
- A consistent sign convention ensures that the resulting equations are **mathematically self-consistent**.
- Inconsistent sign choices lead to **incorrect solutions** even when the underlying physics is correctly understood.
- KTU examiners specifically check for proper sign conventions — incorrect signs are a **3-mark penalty** in 14-mark problems.

**(b)** A DC network has three loops. Write the **mesh equations** using KVL and verify using KCL at every node. Take any standard 2-mesh numerical example and solve. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

Consider the standard two-mesh circuit (already worked out in Section 3.3). Repeating for completeness:

**Given:** $V_1 = 10$ V, $V_2 = 6$ V, $R_1 = 4\ \Omega$, $R_2 = 2\ \Omega$, $R_3 = 6\ \Omega$.

**Mesh Equations (Mesh Currents $I_1$ and $I_2$, both clockwise):**

$$\text{Loop 1:} \quad 5I_1 - 3I_2 = 5$$
$$\text{Loop 2:} \quad 3I_1 - 4I_2 = 3$$

**Solving:** $I_1 = 1\ \text{A}$, $I_2 = 0\ \text{A}$, $I_{R_3} = I_1 - I_2 = 1\ \text{A}$.

**KCL at Node A:**
- Entering: $I_1 = 1$ A
- Leaving: $I_{R_3} + I_2 = 1 + 0 = 1$ A
- Balance: $1 = 1$ ✓

**KCL at Node B:**
- Entering: $I_{R_3} + I_2 = 1 + 0 = 1$ A
- Leaving: $I_1 = 1$ A
- Balance: $1 = 1$ ✓

**KVL Verified** (sums to 0 in each loop — already shown in Section 3.3).

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Pitfalls in KTU Lab Viva and Theory Exams:**
>
> 1. **Incorrect Sign Convention (–3 marks):** Forgetting to assign proper +/− signs while writing KCL/KVL equations. *Always* draw arrows showing assumed reference directions on your circuit diagram before writing equations.
>
> 2. **Unit Inconsistency (–2 marks):** Mixing milliamps (mA) and Amperes (A) in the same observation table. Convert all values to a *single consistent unit* (preferably Amperes) before tabulation.
>
> 3. **Missing the Verification Step (–2 marks):** Simply calculating branch currents without explicitly stating "KCL verified at Node X" or "KVL verified in Loop Y". KTU expects an *explicit verification statement*.
>
> 4. **Percentage Error Calculation Error (–2 marks):** Computing error as `Practical − Theoretical` instead of `|Theoretical − Practical|`. The modulus (absolute value) is mandatory.
>
> 5. **Not Drawing Circuit Diagram (–3 marks):** In viva-voce, examiners will *immediately* ask you to draw the circuit diagram. Practice drawing it before writing any equation.
>
> 6. **Forgetting to Mention Physical Principle (–1 mark):** KTU values theory — always state *why* KCL comes from charge conservation and KVL from energy conservation.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **HIGH-YIELD REVISION POINTS — Read This Before Every KTU Exam**

- ⭐ **KCL** is derived from **Conservation of Charge**; applies at *nodes*.
- ⭐ **KVL** is derived from **Conservation of Energy**; applies in *closed loops*.
- ⭐ Always assume a **reference direction** for each current and voltage *before* writing equations.
- ⭐ The standard formula $\sum I = 0$ uses **algebraic sum** — every current must have a sign.
- ⭐ The standard formula $\sum V = 0$ requires that **voltage rises equal voltage drops** around the loop.
- ⭐ For a circuit with **n nodes**, there are exactly **(n − 1) independent KCL equations**.
- ⭐ For a planar circuit with **m meshes (independent loops)**, there are exactly **m independent KVL equations**.
- ⭐ Kirchhoff's Laws are **invalid** for high-frequency circuits (microwave, RF) where the wavelength is comparable to circuit dimensions.
- ⭐ **Ohm's Law** ($V = IR$) is *always* combined with KVL to express voltage drops across resistors.
- ⭐ In the lab, **percentage error should be < 5%** for verification to be considered successful.
- ⭐ Always present the observation in a **proper table** with columns: *Quantity | Theoretical Value | Practical Value | % Error*.
- ⭐ Real-world applications include **SPICE simulation**, **power-system load flow**, and **PCB design**.
- ⭐ Memorize the **matrix form** of mesh analysis:
$$\begin{bmatrix} R_{11} & -R_{12} \\ -R_{21} & R_{22} \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} I_1 \\ I_2 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} V_1 \\ V_2 \end{bmatrix}$$
- ⭐ Practice converting between **branch currents** and **mesh currents** — they are *not* always equal (shared branch current = algebraic sum/difference of adjacent mesh currents).
- ⭐ **Final KTU Answer-Writing Mantra:** *Diagram → Reference Directions → Equations → Solve → Verify both laws → Tabulate → Percentage Error → Conclusion.*

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Verification of Superposition Theorem
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCSFL408 - ELECTRICAL MACHINES LABORATORY
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Verification of Superposition Theorem

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Superposition Theorem** — In any linear bilateral bilateral network containing more than one independent source, the response (voltage across or current through any element) is equal to the **algebraic sum** of the responses produced by each independent source acting **alone**, with all other independent sources replaced by their **internal resistances** (i.e., voltage sources replaced by short circuits and current sources replaced by open circuits).

The theorem is rigorously applicable **only** to:
- **Linear networks** (elements obeying Ohm's law; parameters do not vary with voltage/current).
- **Bilateral networks** (elements behave identically in both directions).
- Networks containing **independent sources**; dependent sources are *not* deactivated.

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy (Plain English)

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — Multiple Forces on a Single Object:**
> Imagine a stationary boat being pushed by two tugboats from opposite banks of a river. The final position of the boat is the **resultant effect** of both pushes combined. Superposition says you don't have to solve the combined tug-of-war in one shot. Instead, ask:
> *“What if Tugboat A pushed alone?”* → measure the displacement.
> *“What if Tugboat B pushed alone?”* → measure the displacement.
> The **actual displacement** equals the **algebraic sum** of the two individual displacements.

In an electrical circuit:
- **Tugboats** → independent voltage/current sources.
- **Boat's displacement** → voltage across (or current through) a particular branch.
- The river's drag → resistors dissipating energy.

This analogy maps perfectly because **Ohm's law is linear**, just like the linear force-displacement relationship in classical mechanics (Hooke's law).

## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Lab Metrics

- Source voltage ratings used: **+5 V, +10 V, +12 V, +15 V DC** (standard regulated DC power supplies).
- Standard decade resistance boxes: **1 Ω to 1 MΩ**, tolerance ±1 %.
- Standard ammeter ranges: **0–10 mA / 0–100 mA / 0–1 A DC**.
- Standard voltmeter ranges: **0–3 V / 0–15 V / 0–30 V DC**.
- Internal resistance of an **ideal voltmeter → ∞**; ideal ammeter → **0**.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Mathematical Statement

For a linear network with **n** independent sources $S_1, S_2, \ldots, S_n$, the response in any branch (voltage $V_k$ or current $I_k$) is:

$$
V_k = \sum_{i=1}^{n} V_{k,i} \quad \text{or} \quad I_k = \sum_{i=1}^{n} I_{k,i}
$$

where $V_{k,i}$ (or $I_{k,i}$) is the response due to source $S_i$ acting alone, with all other sources deactivated.

## 2.2 Algorithmic Procedure to Apply Superposition

1. **Identify** all independent voltage sources ($V_{S_i}$) and independent current sources ($I_{S_i}$).
2. **Keep** source $S_1$ active; **replace** all other voltage sources by **short circuits** and all other current sources by **open circuits**.
3. **Solve** the simplified (now single-source) circuit using mesh analysis, nodal analysis, or source transformation.
4. **Record** the partial response $V_{k,1}$ (or $I_{k,1}$) — **note the polarity/direction**.
5. **Repeat** steps 2–4 for every remaining independent source.
6. **Algebraically sum** all partial responses (taking polarity signs into account) to obtain the total response.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner's Rule:** The sign convention is the single most common reason for losing marks. If a partial current enters a node from one source and exits the node due to another source, you **must subtract**, not add.

## 2.3 Boundary Conditions & Validity

| Condition | Status | Reason |
|---|---|---|
| Linear resistors only | Valid | Ohm's law is linear ($V = IR$). |
| Non-linear elements (diodes, transistors) | Invalid | $I$-$V$ curve is non-linear. |
| Bilateral elements (resistors, inductors) | Valid | Response is direction-independent. |
| Unilateral elements (diodes) | Invalid | Different behavior in forward/reverse bias. |
| Power calculation | Invalid directly | Power is **quadratic** ($I^2R$, $V^2/R$); cannot be superposed. |
| Dependent sources | Never deactivated | Controlled by circuit variables, kept intact. |

## 2.4 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> All formulas below are universally applicable for any linear DC network used in the verification experiment.

| # | Formula | Description / Use | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $V_k = \sum V_{k,i}$ | Superposition of voltages | Volts (V) |
| 2 | $I_k = \sum I_{k,i}$ | Superposition of currents | Amperes (A) |
| 3 | $V_{S_i} \rightarrow \text{short circuit}$ | Deactivation of an ideal voltage source | — |
| 4 | $I_{S_i} \rightarrow \text{open circuit}$ | Deactivation of an ideal current source | — |
| 5 | $V = IR$ | Ohm's law (linear element) | V, A, Ω |
| 6 | $P = I^2R$ | Power dissipated (cannot superpose) | Watts (W) |
| 7 | $R_{eq} = \dfrac{R_a R_b}{R_a + R_b}$ | Two parallel resistors | Ω |
| 8 | $R_{eq} = \sum R_i$ | Resistors in series | Ω |
| 9 | $I_L = \dfrac{V_k}{R_L}$ | Load current from load voltage | A |
| 10 | $\% \text{ Error} = \dfrac{\vert V_{theoretical} - V_{measured} \vert}{V_{theoretical}} \times 100$ | Experimental error | % |

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

The Superposition Theorem is foundational in:

- **Small-signal AC analysis of amplifiers** (linearizing the circuit around the DC operating point).
- **Power systems analysis** — fault analysis using Thevenin equivalents derived from superposition.
- **Filter design** — analyzing the response to multiple frequency components.
- **Noise analysis in electronic circuits** — separating the contribution of each noise source.
- **Network synthesis and circuit simplification** in CAD tools like SPICE.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Laboratory Example (Reference Circuit for the Experiment)

### 3.1.1 Circuit Description

A two-loop DC resistive network is constructed with:

- **Independent Source 1:** $V_1 = 10 \text{ V}$ DC
- **Independent Source 2:** $V_2 = 15 \text{ V}$ DC
- **Resistor** $R_1 = 1 \text{ k}\Omega$ in series with $V_1$ (left branch)
- **Resistor** $R_2 = 2 \text{ k}\Omega$ in series with $V_2$ (right branch)
- **Load Resistor** $R_3 = 1 \text{ k}\Omega$ (middle branch, common element)
- **Measurement:** Voltage $V_{R_3}$ across $R_3$ and current $I_{R_3}$ through $R_3$.

**Topology:**

$$
\text{Top Node } (A) \;\;\; \xleftarrow{R_1=1\text{k}\Omega} \;\; V_1=10\text{V} \;\; (\text{Reference/Ground Node } B)
$$

$$
\text{Top Node } (A) \;\;\; \xrightarrow{R_2=2\text{k}\Omega} \;\; V_2=15\text{V} \;\; (\text{Reference/Ground Node } B)
$$

$$
\text{Top Node } (A) \;\;\; \xrightarrow{R_3=1\text{k}\Omega} \;\; \text{Reference/Ground Node } (B)
$$

### 3.1.2 Step A — Both Sources Active (Direct Nodal Analysis)

Let $V_A$ be the voltage at the top node A with respect to the reference (ground) node B. Apply **Kirchhoff's Current Law (KCL)** at node A:

$$
\frac{V_1 - V_A}{R_1} + \frac{V_2 - V_A}{R_2} = \frac{V_A}{R_3}
$$

Substitute the values $V_1=10$, $V_2=15$, $R_1=1$, $R_2=2$, $R_3=1$ (all in volts and kΩ):

$$
\frac{10 - V_A}{1} + \frac{15 - V_A}{2} = \frac{V_A}{1}
$$

Multiply through by 2 to clear the denominator:

$$
2(10 - V_A) + (15 - V_A) = 2V_A
$$

$$
20 - 2V_A + 15 - V_A = 2V_A
$$

$$
35 - 3V_A = 2V_A
$$

$$
35 = 5V_A
$$

$$
V_A = 7 \text{ V}
$$

Therefore, the voltage across $R_3$ when **both sources act simultaneously** is:

$$
V_{R_3}^{\text{total}} = V_A = 7 \text{ V}
$$

The corresponding current through $R_3$ is:

$$
I_{R_3}^{\text{total}} = \frac{V_A}{R_3} = \frac{7 \text{ V}}{1 \text{ k}\Omega} = 7 \text{ mA}
$$

### 3.1.3 Step B — Activate $V_1$ Alone ($V_2$ Deactivated → Short Circuit)

**Deactivation rule:** $V_2$ (ideal voltage source) is replaced by a **short circuit** (a plain wire).

The simplified circuit now has:
- $V_1 = 10 \text{ V}$ in series with $R_1 = 1 \text{ k}\Omega$ (left branch — unchanged).
- $R_2 = 2 \text{ k}\Omega$ from node A to ground (right branch — $V_2$ removed).
- $R_3 = 1 \text{ k}\Omega$ from node A to ground (middle branch — unchanged).

Apply KCL at node A:

$$
\frac{V_1 - V_A'}{R_1} = \frac{V_A'}{R_2} + \frac{V_A'}{R_3}
$$

Substitute values:

$$
\frac{10 - V_A'}{1} = \frac{V_A'}{2} + \frac{V_A'}{1}
$$

Multiply by 2:

$$
2(10 - V_A') = V_A' + 2V_A'
$$

$$
20 - 2V_A' = 3V_A'
$$

$$
20 = 5V_A'
$$

$$
V_A' = 4 \text{ V}
$$

Thus, the **contribution of $V_1$ alone** is:

$$
V_{R_3}^{(V_1)} = V_A' = 4 \text{ V}, \qquad I_{R_3}^{(V_1)} = \frac{4 \text{ V}}{1 \text{ k}\Omega} = 4 \text{ mA}
$$

### 3.1.4 Step C — Activate $V_2$ Alone ($V_1$ Deactivated → Short Circuit)

**Deactivation rule:** $V_1$ is replaced by a **short circuit**.

The simplified circuit now has:
- $R_1 = 1 \text{ k}\Omega$ from node A to ground (left branch — $V_1$ removed).
- $V_2 = 15 \text{ V}$ in series with $R_2 = 2 \text{ k}\Omega$ (right branch — unchanged).
- $R_3 = 1 \text{ k}\Omega$ from node A to ground (middle branch — unchanged).

Apply KCL at node A:

$$
\frac{V_A''}{R_1} + \frac{V_2 - V_A''}{R_2} = \frac{V_A''}{R_3}
$$

Substitute values:

$$
\frac{V_A''}{1} + \frac{15 - V_A''}{2} = \frac{V_A''}{1}
$$

Multiply by 2:

$$
2V_A'' + (15 - V_A'') = 2V_A''
$$

$$
V_A'' + 15 = 2V_A''
$$

$$
V_A'' = 15 \text{ V}
$$

Wait — verify by re-arranging carefully:

$$
\frac{V_A''}{1} + \frac{15 - V_A''}{2} = \frac{V_A''}{1}
$$

Move $\frac{V_A''}{1}$ from the right side to the left:

$$
\frac{V_A''}{1} - \frac{V_A''}{1} + \frac{15 - V_A''}{2} = 0
$$

$$
\frac{15 - V_A''}{2} = 0
$$

$$
15 - V_A'' = 0
$$

$$
V_A'' = 15 \text{ V}
$$

This means $V_2$'s voltage appears entirely at node A because $V_1$ is shorted, making $R_1$ and $R_3$ form a parallel path from A to ground. Recompute more rigorously with all three branches contributing current into A:

**Correct KCL (sum of currents leaving node A = 0):**

$$
\frac{V_A'' - 0}{R_1} + \frac{V_A'' - V_2}{R_2} + \frac{V_A'' - 0}{R_3} = 0
$$

$$
\frac{V_A''}{1} + \frac{V_A'' - 15}{2} + \frac{V_A''}{1} = 0
$$

Multiply by 2:

$$
2V_A'' + (V_A'' - 15) + 2V_A'' = 0
$$

$$
5V_A'' - 15 = 0
$$

$$
V_A'' = 3 \text{ V}
$$

Thus, the **contribution of $V_2$ alone** is:

$$
V_{R_3}^{(V_2)} = V_A'' = 3 \text{ V}, \qquad I_{R_3}^{(V_2)} = \frac{3 \text{ V}}{1 \text{ k}\Omega} = 3 \text{ mA}
$$

### 3.1.5 Step D — Algebraic Summation (Verification of Theorem)

$$
V_{R_3}^{\text{superposed}} = V_{R_3}^{(V_1)} + V_{R_3}^{(V_2)} = 4 \text{ V} + 3 \text{ V} = 7 \text{ V}
$$

$$
I_{R_3}^{\text{superposed}} = I_{R_3}^{(V_1)} + I_{R_3}^{(V_2)} = 4 \text{ mA} + 3 \text{ mA} = 7 \text{ mA}
$$

### 3.1.6 Step E — Comparison and Verification

| Quantity | Direct Measurement (Both Sources) | Superposition Sum | Match? |
|---|---|---|---|
| $V_{R_3}$ | **7 V** | $4 + 3 = 7 \text{ V}$ | ✓ |
| $I_{R_3}$ | **7 mA** | $4 + 3 = 7 \text{ mA}$ | ✓ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Since both partial currents flow in the **same direction** (top-to-bottom through $R_3$, i.e., from node A to ground), they **add directly**. If their directions were opposite, the **algebraic** (signed) sum would be required.

### 3.1.7 Percentage Error Calculation

For each measured quantity, the experimental error is:

$$
\% \text{ Error} = \frac{\lvert V_{\text{theoretical}} - V_{\text{measured}} \rvert}{V_{\text{theoretical}}} \times 100
$$

A typical KTU lab acceptance threshold is **error ≤ 5 %**.

## 3.2 Observation Table Format (Record During the Experiment)

| Sl. No. | Circuit Condition | $V_{R_3}$ (V) | $I_{R_3}$ (mA) | Polarity |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Both sources active | | | |
| 2 | $V_1$ alone ($V_2$ shorted) | | | |
| 3 | $V_2$ alone ($V_1$ shorted) | | | |
| 4 | Sum (Row 2 + Row 3) | | | |

## 3.3 Apparatus List (Standard Lab Setup)

| Sl. No. | Apparatus | Specification | Quantity |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | DC Regulated Power Supply | 0–30 V, 1 A | 2 |
| 2 | Decade Resistance Box | 1 Ω–1 MΩ | 1 |
| 3 | Carbon Resistors | 1 kΩ, 2 kΩ, 1 kΩ (1/4 W) | 3 |
| 4 | Digital Multimeter (DMM) | DC V & DC I modes | 2 |
| 5 | Breadboard / Circuit Panel | 840 tie-points | 1 |
| 6 | Connecting Wires (single-strand) | 22 AWG, red & black | As required |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Superposition Theorem — Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A0([START: Linear Network with n Independent Sources]) --> B0[Step 1: Identify all Independent Sources V1, V2, ... Vn]
    B0 --> C0[Step 2: Deactivate all sources EXCEPT V1. Replace each Vi by internal resistance]
    C0 --> D0[Step 3: Solve simplified circuit. Record Partial Response R1 across element X]
    D0 --> E0{All sources considered?}
    E0 -->|No| F0[Step 4: Deactivate V1. Activate next source V2]
    F0 --> G0[Step 5: Solve circuit. Record Partial Response R2]
    G0 --> E0
    E0 -->|Yes| H0[Step 6: Algebraic Sum of all partial responses with correct signs]
    H0 --> I0[Total Response across element X]
    I0 --> J0([END: Compare with direct measurement. Theorem VERIFIED if match within tolerance])

    style A0 fill:#1e3a5f,color:#ffffff,stroke:#ffffff,stroke-width:2px
    style J0 fill:#2e7d32,color:#ffffff,stroke:#ffffff,stroke-width:2px
    style H0 fill:#f57c00,color:#ffffff,stroke:#ffffff,stroke-width:2px
    style E0 fill:#6a1b9a,color:#ffffff,stroke:#ffffff,stroke-width:2px
    style D0 fill:#37474f,color:#ffffff,stroke:#ffffff,stroke-width:2px
    style G0 fill:#37474f,color:#ffffff,stroke:#ffffff,stroke-width:2px
```

## 4.2 Reference Circuit — Two-Loop Network with $V_1$, $V_2$, $R_3$ as Load

```mermaid

    src1((V1 = 10 V DC)) --- r1[R1 = 1 kOhm]
    r1 --- nodeA((Node A))
    nodeA --- r3[R3 = 1 kOhm LOAD]

    nodeB --- src1
    nodeA --- r2[R2 = 2 kOhm]
    r2 --- src2((V2 = 15 V DC))
    src2 --- nodeB

    meterV[Voltmeter across R3] -.-> r3
    meterI[Ammeter in series with R3] -.-> r3

    style src1 fill:#0277bd,color:#ffffff,stroke:#ffffff,stroke-width:2px
    style src2 fill:#c62828,color:#ffffff,stroke:#ffffff,stroke-width:2px
    style nodeA fill:#fbc02d,color:#000000,stroke:#000000,stroke-width:2px
    style nodeB fill:#424242,color:#ffffff,stroke:#ffffff,stroke-width:2px
```
