```mermaid
    style meterV fill:#6a1b9a,color:#ffffff,stroke:#ffffff,stroke-width:2px
    style meterI fill:#6a1b9a,color:#ffffff,stroke:#ffffff,stroke-width:2px
```

## 4.3 Deactivation of Sources — Visual Summary

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Independent Voltage Source Vi] -->|Deactivate| B[Replace by Short Circuit]
    C[Independent Current Source Ij] -->|Deactivate| D[Replace by Open Circuit]
    E[Dependent Source] -->|NEVER Deactivate| F[Keep intact in circuit]

    style A fill:#0277bd,color:#ffffff,stroke:#ffffff,stroke-width:2px
    style B fill:#2e7d32,color:#ffffff,stroke:#ffffff,stroke-width:2px
    style C fill:#c62828,color:#ffffff,stroke:#ffffff,stroke-width:2px
    style D fill:#2e7d32,color:#ffffff,stroke:#ffffff,stroke-width:2px
    style E fill:#f57c00,color:#ffffff,stroke:#ffffff,stroke-width:2px
    style F fill:#f57c00,color:#ffffff,stroke:#ffffff,stroke-width:2px
```

## 4.4 Precautions Infographic

```mermaid
mindmap
  root((Precautions for Superposition Verification))
    Connections
      Tight connections on breadboard
      Verify polarity of meters
      Use shortest possible leads
    Source Handling
      Deactivate source fully before reconnection
      Confirm zero current through shorted source
    Measurements
      Note DMM range before measuring
      Avoid parallax in analog meters
      Record readings at stable steady state
    Safety
      Do not exceed power supply ratings
      Verify resistor wattage ratings
      Switch off supplies when reconfiguring
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam — May 2023]
**State the Superposition Theorem. Mention two conditions under which it is applicable.**

**Model Answer:**
The Superposition Theorem states that in any linear bilateral network containing multiple independent sources, the response (voltage or current) in any element is equal to the **algebraic sum of the responses produced by each independent source acting alone**, while all other independent sources are deactivated (voltage sources replaced by short circuits and current sources by open circuits).

**Applicability conditions:**
1. The network must be **linear** (elements obey Ohm's law; parameters are constant).
2. The network must be **bilateral** (element behavior is identical irrespective of current direction).

> [!WARNING]
> **Valuation Pitfall:** Many students forget to explicitly mention "**independent**" sources. Dependent (controlled) sources are **not deactivated**. Examiners deduct ½ mark for this omission.

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]
**While applying the Superposition Theorem, how would you deactivate (a) an ideal voltage source and (b) an ideal current source? Justify your answer.**

**Model Answer:**

(a) **Ideal Voltage Source:** Replaced by a **short circuit** (zero resistance), because an ideal voltage source maintains a constant voltage regardless of current. When set to zero, it behaves like a wire.

(b) **Ideal Current Source:** Replaced by an **open circuit** (infinite resistance), because an ideal current source delivers a constant current regardless of the voltage across it. When the current is set to zero, no current can flow, equivalent to an open.

> [!WARNING]
> **Valuation Pitfall:** Some students mistakenly replace a voltage source by an open circuit and a current source by a short. This is the **exact opposite** and results in full marks deduction. Memorize: "**V → short, I → open.**"

---

## Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]

**(a) [7 Marks]** For the circuit shown below, verify the Superposition Theorem by computing the current through the 1 kΩ load resistor using mesh analysis. Use $V_1 = 10$ V, $V_2 = 15$ V, $R_1 = 1$ kΩ, $R_2 = 2$ kΩ.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Tabulate the measured and computed values. Calculate the percentage error and state whether the theorem is verified within a 5 % tolerance.

#### Model Solution for Question A(a):

**Step 1 — Direct Analysis (Both Sources Active)**

Using nodal analysis at the top node A (with bottom node as reference):

$$
\frac{V_1 - V_A}{R_1} + \frac{V_2 - V_A}{R_2} = \frac{V_A}{R_3}
$$

Substituting numerical values:

$$
\frac{10 - V_A}{1} + \frac{15 - V_A}{2} = \frac{V_A}{1}
$$

Multiplying through by 2:

$$
2(10 - V_A) + (15 - V_A) = 2V_A
$$

$$
20 - 2V_A + 15 - V_A = 2V_A
$$

$$
35 = 5V_A \quad \Rightarrow \quad V_A = 7 \text{ V}
$$

Therefore:

$$
I_{R_3}^{\text{total}} = \frac{V_A}{R_3} = \frac{7 \text{ V}}{1 \text{ k}\Omega} = 7 \text{ mA}
$$

**Step 2 — Superposition with $V_1$ Only ($V_2$ Shorted)**

With $V_2$ replaced by a short, $R_2$ now connects node A directly to ground.

$$
\frac{V_1 - V_A'}{R_1} = \frac{V_A'}{R_2} + \frac{V_A'}{R_3}
$$

$$
\frac{10 - V_A'}{1} = \frac{V_A'}{2} + \frac{V_A'}{1}
$$

Multiplying by 2:

$$
20 - 2V_A' = V_A' + 2V_A'
$$

$$
20 = 5V_A' \quad \Rightarrow \quad V_A' = 4 \text{ V}
$$

$$
I_{R_3}^{(V_1)} = \frac{4 \text{ V}}{1 \text{ k}\Omega} = 4 \text{ mA}
$$

**Step 3 — Superposition with $V_2$ Only ($V_1$ Shorted)**

With $V_1$ replaced by a short, $R_1$ now connects node A directly to ground.

$$
\frac{V_A''}{R_1} + \frac{V_2 - V_A''}{R_2} = \frac{V_A''}{R_3}
$$

$$
\frac{V_A''}{1} + \frac{15 - V_A''}{2} = \frac{V_A''}{1}
$$

Rearranging (transfer $\frac{V_A''}{1}$ from RHS to LHS):

$$
\frac{V_A''}{1} - \frac{V_A''}{1} + \frac{15 - V_A''}{2} = 0
$$

The first two terms cancel:

$$
\frac{15 - V_A''}{2} = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad V_A'' = 15 \text{ V}
$$

Re-doing the KCL correctly (sum of currents **leaving** node A = 0):

$$
\frac{V_A''}{R_1} + \frac{V_A'' - V_2}{R_2} + \frac{V_A''}{R_3} = 0
$$

$$
\frac{V_A''}{1} + \frac{V_A'' - 15}{2} + \frac{V_A''}{1} = 0
$$

Multiplying by 2:

$$
2V_A'' + V_A'' - 15 + 2V_A'' = 0
$$

$$
5V_A'' = 15 \quad \Rightarrow \quad V_A'' = 3 \text{ V}
$$

$$
I_{R_3}^{(V_2)} = \frac{3 \text{ V}}{1 \text{ k}\Omega} = 3 \text{ mA}
$$

**Step 4 — Algebraic Sum**

$$
I_{R_3}^{\text{superposed}} = I_{R_3}^{(V_1)} + I_{R_3}^{(V_2)} = 4 \text{ mA} + 3 \text{ mA} = 7 \text{ mA}
$$

**Step 5 — Verification**

$$
I_{R_3}^{\text{direct}} = I_{R_3}^{\text{superposed}} = 7 \text{ mA} \quad \checkmark
$$

**The Superposition Theorem is verified.**

#### Model Solution for Question A(b):

**Observation Table (Recorded in Lab):**

| Sl. No. | Circuit Condition | Theoretical $I_{R_3}$ | Measured $I_{R_3}$ | % Error |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Both sources active | 7.00 mA | 6.93 mA | 1.00 % |
| 2 | $V_1$ only ($V_2$ shorted) | 4.00 mA | 3.96 mA | 1.00 % |
| 3 | $V_2$ only ($V_1$ shorted) | 3.00 mA | 2.98 mA | 0.67 % |
| 4 | Superposition sum (Row 2 + Row 3) | 7.00 mA | 6.94 mA | 0.86 % |

**Sample Error Calculation for Row 1:**

$$
\% \text{ Error} = \frac{\lvert 7.00 - 6.93 \rvert}{7.00} \times 100 = \frac{0.07}{7.00} \times 100 = 1.00 \%
$$

**Result:** All percentage errors are well within the 5 % tolerance. The Superposition Theorem is **verified experimentally**.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — ALTERNATIVE [KTU University Exam — May 2024]

**(a) [7 Marks]** Two ideal voltage sources $V_1 = 12$ V and $V_2 = 6$ V are connected in a network along with three resistors $R_1 = 4$ Ω, $R_2 = 2$ Ω, $R_3 = 6$ Ω as shown. Apply the Superposition Theorem to find the current through $R_3$.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Explain with a circuit diagram why the Superposition Theorem **cannot** be directly used to compute the power dissipated in $R_3$. Compute the total power using each method and compare.

#### Model Solution for Question B(a):

**Circuit:** $V_1 = 12$ V in series with $R_1 = 4$ Ω and $R_2 = 2$ Ω (forming left loop), $V_2 = 6$ V in series with $R_3 = 6$ Ω (forming right loop). $R_2$ is shared between the two loops.

**Mesh 1 (left):**

$$
V_1 = I_1 R_1 + (I_1 - I_2) R_2 \quad \Rightarrow \quad 12 = 4I_1 + 2(I_1 - I_2)
$$

$$
12 = 6I_1 - 2I_2 \quad \cdots (1)
$$

**Mesh 2 (right):**

$$
V_2 = (I_2 - I_1) R_2 + I_2 R_3 \quad \Rightarrow \quad 6 = 2(I_2 - I_1) + 6I_2
$$

$$
6 = -2I_1 + 8I_2 \quad \cdots (2)
$$

**Solving equations (1) and (2):**

From (1): $I_1 = \dfrac{12 + 2I_2}{6} = \dfrac{6 + I_2}{3}$

Substitute into (2):

$$
6 = -2 \cdot \frac{6 + I_2}{3} + 8I_2
$$

Multiply by 3:

$$
18 = -2(6 + I_2) + 24I_2
$$

$$
18 = -12 - 2I_2 + 24I_2
$$

$$
30 = 22I_2
$$

$$
I_2 = \frac{30}{22} = \frac{15}{11} \approx 1.364 \text{ A}
$$

$$
I_1 = \frac{6 + 15/11}{3} = \frac{66/11 + 15/11}{3} = \frac{81/11}{3} = \frac{27}{11} \approx 2.455 \text{ A}
$$

Current through $R_3$ (mesh 2 current) = $I_2 \approx 1.364$ A.

**Now applying Superposition:**

**Case 1: $V_1$ Alone (Short $V_2$)**

Mesh 1: $12 = 4I_1' + 2(I_1' - I_2') = 6I_1' - 2I_2'$
Mesh 2: $0 = 2(I_2' - I_1') + 6I_2' = -2I_1' + 8I_2'$

From Mesh 2: $I_1' = 4I_2'$

Substitute: $12 = 6(4I_2') - 2I_2' = 22I_2'$

$$
I_2' = \frac{12}{22} = \frac{6}{11} \approx 0.545 \text{ A}
$$

**Case 2: $V_2$ Alone (Short $V_1$)**

Mesh 1: $0 = 6I_1'' - 2I_2''$
Mesh 2: $6 = -2I_1'' + 8I_2''$

From Mesh 1: $I_1'' = \dfrac{I_2''}{3}$

Substitute: $6 = -\dfrac{2I_2''}{3} + 8I_2'' = \dfrac{22 I_2''}{3}$

$$
I_2'' = \frac{18}{22} = \frac{9}{11} \approx 0.818 \text{ A}
$$

**Algebraic Sum:**

$$
I_{R_3}^{\text{superposed}} = I_2' + I_2'' = \frac{6}{11} + \frac{9}{11} = \frac{15}{11} \approx 1.364 \text{ A}
$$

This **matches** the direct mesh analysis result. **Superposition is verified.**

#### Model Solution for Question B(b):

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Concept:** Power is a **quadratic function** of current ($P = I^2 R$) or voltage ($P = V^2 / R$). Superposition applies only to **linear** operations (addition, subtraction, multiplication by a constant). Squaring a sum is **not** a linear operation:
> $$(I_1 + I_2)^2 \neq I_1^2 + I_2^2$$

**Direct Total Power Calculation (Correct Method):**

Using $I_{R_3}^{\text{total}} = 1.364$ A:

$$
P_{R_3}^{\text{direct}} = (I_{R_3}^{\text{total}})^2 \cdot R_3 = (1.364)^2 \times 6 = 1.860 \times 6 = 11.16 \text{ W}
$$

**Incorrect Superposition of Powers:**

Using partial currents $I_2' = 0.545$ A and $I_2'' = 0.818$ A:

$$
P_{R_3}^{\text{wrong}} = (I_2')^2 R_3 + (I_2'')^2 R_3
$$

$$
P_{R_3}^{\text{wrong}} = (0.545)^2 \times 6 + (0.818)^2 \times 6
$$

$$
P_{R_3}^{\text{wrong}} = 0.297 \times 6 + 0.669 \times 6
$$

$$
P_{R_3}^{\text{wrong}} = 1.783 + 4.015 = 5.798 \text{ W}
$$

**Comparison:**

| Method | Power in $R_3$ |
|---|---|
| Direct (Correct) | **11.16 W** |
| Superposition of powers (Incorrect) | **5.80 W** |
| Difference | 5.36 W (error ≈ 48 %) |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning:** In questions that ask for power, **always** compute power from the **total voltage or total current**, never from superposed values. If a student writes $P = P_1 + P_2$, they will lose a minimum of 3 marks for conceptual error, even if arithmetic is correct.

---

## ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls on Superposition Problems

> [!WARNING]
> **Top 5 ways students lose marks on this topic:**
> 1. **Deactivating dependent sources** — Controlled sources must remain in the circuit. Only independent sources are deactivated.
> 2. **Replacing voltage source by open circuit** — This is the **wrong** deactivation. Always use a short circuit for voltage sources.
> 3. **Adding powers directly** — Superposition does NOT apply to power. Always compute power from total current/voltage.
> 4. **Ignoring sign conventions** — If partial currents flow in opposite directions through a resistor, **subtract** (algebraic sum), do not add.
> 5. **Missing the polarity of voltmeter readings** — A voltmeter reads the same magnitude irrespective of lead orientation, but the **sign** in superposition must be carried algebraically.

---

## 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Definition:** Superposition Theorem states that the response in any branch of a linear bilateral network equals the algebraic sum of responses due to each independent source acting alone.
- **Deactivation rules:** Voltage source → **short circuit**; Current source → **open circuit**; Dependent sources → **kept intact**.
- **Applicability:** Only for **linear, bilateral** networks with **independent** sources. Valid for **voltages** and **currents** — **never for power**.
- **Sign convention:** Adopt a reference direction for each current and reference polarity for each voltage **before** starting superposition. Carry these signs consistently through each partial calculation.
- **Power Pitfall:** $P = I^2 R$ is nonlinear — compute total current first, then power.
- **Experimental Rule of Thumb:** If measured and theoretical values agree within **±5 %**, the theorem is considered verified.
- **Practical Importance:** Superposition simplifies complex multi-source problems; it is the mathematical foundation of **small-signal AC analysis**, **noise analysis**, and **linear systems theory**.
- **Lab Procedure Order:** (1) Connect both sources & measure; (2) Short $V_2$, keep $V_1$, measure; (3) Short $V_1$, keep $V_2$, measure; (4) Algebraically sum partial results; (5) Compare with Step 1 measurement.
- **Units to remember:** Voltage in **V (Volts)**, current in **mA (milli-Amperes)** for kΩ resistor networks, resistance in **kΩ**.
- **Common Resistor Color Codes (1 kΩ, 2 kΩ):**
  - 1 kΩ → Brown-Black-Red-Gold
  - 2 kΩ → Red-Black-Red-Gold
- **KTU Quick Recall:** *“V-short, I-open, dependent-leave-alone, power-never-superpose.”*

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Study of B.H. Curve on C.R.O
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCSFL408 - ELECTRICAL MACHINES LABORATORY
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Study of B.H. Curve on C.R.O

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

The **B–H Curve** (also called the **Magnetization Curve** or **Hysteresis Loop**) is the graphical relationship between the **magnetic flux density (B)** in a ferromagnetic specimen and the **magnetizing field intensity (H)** applied to it, traced dynamically as a closed loop on a **Cathode Ray Oscilloscope (CRO)**. The CRO method allows the hysteresis loop to be displayed in real time for a specimen (usually a toroidal iron core) under alternating magnetisation, without requiring a point-by-point ballistic galvanometer measurement.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (PCSFL408 – Module 2):** Students are expected to *study*, *trace*, and *interpret* the B–H loop on a CRO, and compute the magnetic parameters **Coercivity (H_c)**, **Retentivity (B_r)**, **Hysteresis Loss (W_h)** per cycle, and **Permeability (μ)** from the displayed waveform.

### 1.2 Physical Quantities Involved

| Symbol | Quantity | SI Unit | Meaning in Plain English |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| $B$ | Magnetic Flux Density | Tesla (T) | How dense the magnetic field lines are inside the material |
| $H$ | Magnetizing Field Intensity | Ampere/metre (A/m) | The "push" applied to magnetise the material |
| $\mu$ | Permeability of medium | H/m | Ease with which the material allows magnetic lines to pass through |
| $\mu_0$ | Permeability of free space | $4\pi \times 10^{-7}$ H/m | Permeability of vacuum |
| $\mu_r$ | Relative permeability | Dimensionless | Ratio of material's permeability to vacuum |

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — "The Sponge and Water Bucket"

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Analogy:** Imagine a sponge inside a bucket of water.
> - **H (Magnetising Field)** is the effort you apply to squeeze water (magnetic energy) into the sponge.
> - **B (Flux Density)** is the actual amount of water held inside the sponge.
> - When you squeeze a little (**low H**), the sponge absorbs a small amount (low B). When you squeeze hard (**high H**), it absorbs more.
> - The "B–H curve" is the *signature* of how this particular sponge behaves — some sponges (like iron) hold a lot, some (like air) hold almost nothing.
> - The fact that the sponge **doesn't release all the water** when you stop squeezing is called **Retentivity**, and the **extra squeeze needed to fully wring it out** is called **Coercivity**. This "memory effect" of the sponge is what creates the *closed loop* (hysteresis) on the CRO.

### 1.4 Why a CRO is Used Instead of a Ballistic Galvanometer

- The traditional **ballistic galvanometer** method is slow, manual, and gives only *discrete* points.
- The **CRO method** is **real-time, dynamic, and continuous** — it displays the entire loop in one AC cycle.
- It allows visual observation of the loop's shape, area, and saturation behaviour under varying applied voltages and frequencies.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Underlying Physics — What Creates the Hysteresis Loop?

When a ferromagnetic material (e.g., silicon steel, cast iron) is subjected to a cyclic magnetising field $H$, the magnetic domains inside it **align, grow, and resist realignment**. This domain-wall friction causes the flux density $B$ to **lag behind** $H$ — a phenomenon called **Hysteresis** (from Greek *hysterein* — "to lag behind").

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Domain Theory Intuition:** Each tiny region (domain) acts like a miniature magnet. As $H$ increases, domains aligned with $H$ grow at the expense of others. When $H$ is reduced, the domains do **not return to their original orientation** — energy is lost as heat due to internal friction. This lost energy per cycle is the **Hysteresis Loss** and is **proportional to the area enclosed by the B–H loop** on the CRO.

### 2.2 The CRO Display Logic — Why B Goes to Y-Axis and H to X-Axis

In the standard test circuit, two voltages are derived from the specimen:

- A voltage **proportional to B** is fed to the **Y-input** (vertical deflection).
- A voltage **proportional to H** is fed to the **X-input** (horizontal deflection).

When the CRO sweeps, the spot traces the **locus of (H, B)** values — i.e., the **B–H loop**.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **CRITICAL FORMULA TABLE — Memorise for KTU University Exam**

| # | Quantity | Formula | Description | Unit |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | Magnetising Field | $H = \dfrac{N_1 \cdot I_m}{l} \cdot \sin(\omega t)$ | $N_1$ = primary turns, $I_m$ = peak primary current, $l$ = mean magnetic path length | A/m |
| 2 | Peak Magnetising Field | $H_{\max} = \dfrac{N_1 \cdot I_m}{l}$ | Maximum value of H during a cycle | A/m |
| 3 | Induced EMF in Secondary | $e = N_2 \cdot A \cdot \dfrac{dB}{dt}$ | Faraday's law for the secondary winding | V |
| 4 | Flux Density (after integration) | $B = -\dfrac{1}{N_2 A} \int e \, dt$ | $A$ = cross-sectional area of core | T |
| 5 | Output of Op-Amp Integrator | $V_o = -\dfrac{1}{RC} \int V_{in} \, dt$ | Operational amplifier integrator | V |
| 6 | Voltage across Series Resistor | $V_R = I_p \cdot R_s$ | $R_s$ = series sampling resistor | V |
| 7 | Hysteresis Loss per Unit Volume | $W_h = \dfrac{\text{Area of B-H loop}}{4\pi}$ | Steinmetz formula (CGS); SI: $W_h = \eta \cdot B_{\max}^{1.6} \cdot f \cdot V$ | J/m³/cycle |
| 8 | Permeability (at any point) | $\mu = \dfrac{B}{H}$ | Slope of the line joining origin to a point on B–H curve | H/m |
| 9 | Relative Permeability | $\mu_r = \dfrac{\mu}{\mu_0}$ | Dimensionless ratio | – |
| 10 | Retentivity (Remanence) | $B_r$ | $B$ value when $H = 0$ on the loop | T |
| 11 | Coercivity | $H_c$ | $H$ value when $B = 0$ on the loop | A/m |

### 2.4 Engineering Utility of B–H Curve Study

- **Transformer Core Design:** Low hysteresis loss cores (CRGO steel, amorphous metal) are selected based on the **area** of the loop.
- **Electric Motor Laminations:** Materials with narrow loops (low $H_c$) reduce heating.
- **Permanent Magnet Selection:** Hard magnetic materials have **wide, tall loops** (high $B_r$ and high $H_c$) — e.g., AlNiCo, NdFeB.
- **Magnetic Recording Media (Tapes/Disks):** Their B–H characteristics determine storage density and erase behaviour.
- **Switched-Mode Power Supplies (SMPS):** Core selection uses the B–H loop to avoid saturation at high frequencies.

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Insight:** Modern electric vehicle motors use **soft magnetic composites** whose B–H loop is *engineered* through powder metallurgy — a direct industrial application of this lab experiment.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Magnetising Field H (X-axis of CRO)

**Given:**
- A toroidal specimen with $N_1$ primary turns wound uniformly.
- Mean magnetic path length $= l$ (metres).
- AC current through primary: $i_p = I_m \sin(\omega t)$.

**Step-by-step deduction:**

By **Ampere's Circuital Law**, the magnetomotive force (MMF) around the closed magnetic path equals the line integral of $H$:

$$\oint H \, dl = N_1 \cdot i_p$$

Assuming $H$ is uniform along the mean path (valid for a toroid with uniform winding):

$$H \cdot l = N_1 \cdot I_m \sin(\omega t)$$

Rearranging for $H$:

$$H(t) = \frac{N_1 \cdot I_m \sin(\omega t)}{l}$$

Peak value:

$$H_{\max} = \frac{N_1 \cdot I_m}{l}$$

**Reading H from CRO (X-axis):**
The voltage across a small series resistor $R_s$ in the primary circuit is:

$$v_R(t) = i_p(t) \cdot R_s = I_m R_s \sin(\omega t)$$

Since $v_R \propto i_p \propto H$, the **CRO X-deflection** in volts is directly proportional to $H$:

$$H(t) = \frac{N_1}{l \cdot R_s} \cdot v_R(t)$$

Therefore, the **X-axis calibration constant** is:

$$k_x = \frac{N_1}{l \cdot R_s} \quad \left[ \frac{\text{A/m}}{\text{V}} \right]$$

### 3.2 Derivation of the Flux Density B (Y-axis of CRO)

**Given:**
- A secondary winding with $N_2$ turns wound on the same toroid.
- Cross-sectional area of the core $= A$ (m²).
- Magnetic flux $\Phi = B \cdot A$.

**Step-by-step deduction:**

By **Faraday's Law of Electromagnetic Induction**, the instantaneous EMF induced in the secondary is:

$$e(t) = -N_2 \frac{d\Phi}{dt} = -N_2 A \frac{dB}{dt}$$

To recover $B(t)$ from $e(t)$, we must **integrate** with respect to time:

$$B(t) = -\frac{1}{N_2 A} \int e(t) \, dt$$

This integration is performed electronically by an **Op-Amp Integrator circuit** built around a 741 IC (or equivalent).

**Output of ideal Op-Amp Integrator:**

$$V_o(t) = -\frac{1}{R_i C_f} \int V_{in}(t) \, dt$$

Substituting $V_{in} = e(t)$ and $V_o$ as the CRO Y-input:

$$V_o(t) = \frac{1}{R_i C_f \cdot N_2 A} \int e(t) \, dt = \frac{B(t)}{R_i C_f \cdot N_2 A}$$

Hence, the **Y-axis calibration constant** is:

$$k_y = \frac{1}{R_i \cdot C_f \cdot N_2 \cdot A} \quad \left[ \frac{\text{T}}{\text{V}} \right]$$

### 3.3 Combined CRO Display Equation

Combining the X and Y deflection calibration factors, the CRO trace coordinates $(x, y)$ map to the actual $(H, B)$ values as:

$$\begin{aligned}
H &= k_x \cdot x \quad \text{(X-deflection in cm or divisions)} \\
B &= k_y \cdot y \quad \text{(Y-deflection in cm or divisions)}
\end{aligned}$$

### 3.4 Hysteresis Loss Calculation from the Loop Area

The hysteresis loss **per unit volume per cycle** is given by the **area enclosed by the loop** in the B–H plane. In SI units:

$$W_h = \oint H \, dB \quad \left[ \frac{\text{J}}{\text{m}^3 \cdot \text{cycle}} \right]$$

In the **CGS system** (often used in KTU problems):

$$W_h = \frac{\text{Area of B-H loop (in Gauss-Oersted)}}{4\pi} \quad \left[ \frac{\text{ergs}}{\text{cm}^3 \cdot \text{cycle}} \right]$$

**Steinmetz Empirical Formula** (faster, often used in exam numericals):

$$W_h = \eta \cdot B_{\max}^{1.6} \cdot f \cdot V \quad \text{(watts)}$$

where:
- $\eta$ = Steinmetz hysteresis coefficient (material-dependent, e.g., $\eta \approx 0.002$ for cast iron, $\eta \approx 0.0005$ for silicon steel).
- $B_{\max}$ = peak flux density in Tesla.
- $f$ = supply frequency in Hz.
- $V$ = volume of the core in m³.

### 3.5 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Exam Style)

**Problem:** A toroidal specimen has $N_1 = 200$ turns, $N_2 = 200$ turns, mean path length $l = 0.4$ m, cross-sectional area $A = 4 \times 10^{-4}$ m², primary current $I_m = 2$ A, supply frequency $f = 50$ Hz, and the integrator uses $R_i = 100$ k$\Omega$, $C_f = 1$ $\mu$F. The CRO shows a horizontal deflection of $3$ cm and vertical deflection of $4$ cm for the peak values. Find $H_{\max}$ and $B_{\max}$.

**Solution:**

Step 1 — Calculate $H_{\max}$:

$$H_{\max} = \frac{N_1 \cdot I_m}{l} = \frac{200 \times 2}{0.4} = 1000 \; \text{A/m}$$

Step 2 — Calculate $B_{\max}$ using calibration:

$$k_y = \frac{1}{R_i C_f N_2 A} = \frac{1}{(100 \times 10^3)(1 \times 10^{-6})(200)(4 \times 10^{-4})}$$

$$\begin{aligned}
k_y &= \frac{1}{(10^5)(10^{-6})(200)(4 \times 10^{-4})} \\
&= \frac{1}{(0.1)(0.08)} = \frac{1}{0.008} = 125 \; \text{T/V}
\end{aligned}$$

Step 3 — Find CRO Y-sensitivity (assume CRO Y-sensitivity = 0.5 V/cm):

$$B_{\max} = k_y \times (V_{y,\text{peak}}) = 125 \times (4 \times 0.5) = 125 \times 2 = 250 \; \text{T}$$

*(Note: this illustrative value is huge — in practice, a CRO sensitivity of ~10 mV/cm is used, giving realistic $B_{\max}$ values of ~1 T.)*

> [!NOTE]
> **Tip for KTU exams:** Always write units at every step. Even if the value is exaggerated for pedagogy, the **methodology** and **dimensional consistency** are what examiners reward.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture — CRO-Based B–H Tracer

The actual physical circuit uses a toroidal core, two windings, a series resistor, an op-amp integrator, and a CRO. Since a literal hand-drawn schematic is hard to render in text, the following **Mermaid block diagram** shows the complete **signal flow architecture**:

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    AC[AC Supply 50Hz Mains] -->|Variable Voltage| TF[Variac Autotransformer]
    TF -->|Adjustable AC| Pri[Primary Winding N1 on Toroidal Core]
    Pri -->|Magnetising Current Ip| Rs[Series Sampling Resistor Rs]
    Rs -->|Voltage VR proportional to H| CX[CRO X-Input Horizontal]
    Sec[Secondary Winding N2 on Toroidal Core] -->|Induced EMF e t| Int[Op-Amp Integrator 741 RC]
    Int -->|Integrated Voltage Vo proportional to B| CY[CRO Y-Input Vertical]
    Pri -.->|Flux Phi t| Sec
    CX <-->|Deflection| CRT[(CRO Screen Displays B H Hysteresis Loop)]
    CY <-->|Deflection| CRT
```

### 4.2 Detailed Operational Sequence (Signal-Flow Walkthrough)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    step1[Step 1: Apply 50 Hz AC via Variac to primary N1] --> step2[Step 2: Current Ip flows producing magnetising field H proportional to VR]
    step2 --> step3[Step 3: Flux Phi t links secondary N2 inducing EMF e t]
    step3 --> step4[Step 4: Op-Amp Integrator integrates e t to give voltage Vo proportional to B t]
    step4 --> step5[Step 5: VR fed to X-axis and Vo to Y-axis of CRO]
    step5 --> step6[Step 6: CRO spot traces B H hysteresis loop on screen]
    step6 --> step7[Step 7: Calibrate X and Y using CRO sensitivity and kx ky]
    step7 --> step8[Step 8: Read Bmax Hmax Br Hc from the loop edges]
    step8 --> step9[Step 9: Calculate hysteresis loss from loop area]
```

### 4.3 Hysteresis Loop Anatomy — Reading the CRO Trace

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Quadrant I: H positive increasing, B rises to Bmax] -->|Saturation Region| B[Tip of loop: Smax Hmax Bmax]
    B -->|H decreases to 0| C[Retentivity Br on Y-axis where H=0]
    C -->|H reversed to negative Hc| D[Coercivity Hc on X-axis where B=0]
    D -->|Saturation in reverse| E[Quadrant III: negative saturation]
    E -->|H returns to 0| F[Negative Retentivity minus Br]
    F -->|H increases back to Hc| G[Positive Coercivity plus Hc]
    G -->|Back to start| A
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Reading on CRO:** The loop has **four intercept points** — two on the **Y-axis** (Retentivity $B_r$ at top and bottom) and two on the **X-axis** (Coercivity $H_c$ at left and right). The **width** along the X-axis measures coercivity; the **height** along the Y-axis measures retentivity. The **enclosed area** is proportional to hysteresis loss.

### 4.4 Typical Circuit Schematic Description (Text-Based for Lab Record)

The actual lab circuit consists of the following interconnected modules:

- **Specimen:** Toroidal core of soft iron / cast steel / silicon steel, with primary winding $N_1 = 200$ turns (thick wire) and secondary winding $N_2 = 200$ turns (thin wire).
- **Primary Side:** A variac feeds AC mains to the primary in series with a small non-inductive resistor $R_s$ (~10 $\Omega$, 10 W).
- **Secondary Side:** Connected to the input of an op-amp integrator (e.g., $\mu$A741 with $R_i = 100$ k$\Omega$ and $C_f = 1$ $\mu$F).
- **CRO Connections:** X-input probes $V_R$ across the series resistor; Y-input probes integrator output $V_o$.
- **CRO Settings:** Internal timebase switched **OFF** (use **X-Y mode**); X-sensitivity ~ 0.1 V/div; Y-sensitivity ~ 1 V/div.
- **Auxiliary:** A 30 MHz dual-trace CRO is preferred; a low-distortion sine wave source is essential.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]** Define **Magnetic Flux Density (B)** and **Magnetising Field Intensity (H)**. State their SI units and the relationship between them in a magnetic medium.

> **Model Answer (3 marks):**
> - **Magnetic Flux Density (B):** The total magnetic flux passing perpendicularly through a unit area of a material. $B = \Phi / A$. SI unit: **Tesla (T)** or **Weber/m²**.
> - **Magnetising Field Intensity (H):** The magnetising force produced by the current in a coil per unit length of the magnetic path. $H = (N \cdot I) / l$. SI unit: **Ampere/metre (A/m)**.
> - **Relationship:** In a magnetic medium, $B = \mu H = \mu_0 \mu_r H$, where $\mu$ is the permeability of the medium, $\mu_0 = 4\pi \times 10^{-7}$ H/m is the permeability of free space, and $\mu_r$ is the relative permeability.
> *(Award 1 mark for each correct definition + unit, 1 mark for the relation.)*

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]** What is **Hysteresis**? Why is the B–H loop of a ferromagnetic material not a single curve but a closed loop?

> **Model Answer (3 marks):**
> - **Hysteresis** is the phenomenon of **lagging of magnetic flux density (B) behind the magnetising field (H)** in a ferromagnetic material when it is subjected to a cyclic magnetisation process.
> - The closed loop arises because the **magnetic domains do not return to their original orientations** when H is reduced. There is an irreversible energy loss per cycle (manifested as heat) due to **domain wall friction** and **rotational hysteresis**.
> - When H is increased from zero, the B–H curve follows the **initial magnetisation curve**; when H is decreased, B does not retrace the same path — instead, it follows a **lower path** and **retains a value $B_r$ (Retentivity)** when H = 0. To bring B to zero, a reverse field $H_c$ (Coercivity) is needed, giving the loop its characteristic closed shape.
> *(Award 1 mark for definition, 1 mark for lag concept, 1 mark for retentivity-coercivity explanation.)*

---

### 5.2 Part B — Full-Length 14-Mark Questions (Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting the calibration constants** $k_x$ and $k_y$ — students often read CRO divisions directly as B and H, losing **3–4 marks**.
> 2. **Confusing the role of $N_1$ and $N_2$** — $N_1$ is on the primary (produces H); $N_2$ is on the secondary (senses B). Mixing them is a **fatal error**.
> 3. **Forgetting the negative sign** of the integrator output — the CRO's Y-inversion matters for the loop's quadrant orientation.
> 4. **Using wrong Steinmetz exponent** — the law uses $B^{1.6}$, not $B^2$. Marks are deducted for this.
> 5. **Not drawing the labelled B–H loop** in the answer — KTU requires a *neat diagram with B_r, H_c, B_max, H_max marked* (2 marks reserved for the figure).

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024, Module 2]**

**(a)** With the help of a neat **block diagram**, explain the method of obtaining the **B–H curve (Hysteresis Loop) of a ferromagnetic specimen on a CRO**. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Derive the expressions for **H (magnetising field)** and **B (flux density)** in terms of the voltages applied to the **X- and Y-plates** of the CRO. **(7 marks)**

> **Model Solution (a):**
>
> **Block Diagram:** *(Draw the same Mermaid signal flow converted to a neat labelled rectangular box diagram: AC Source → Variac → Primary $N_1$ of toroid + Series $R_s$ → CRO X-input; Secondary $N_2$ of toroid → Op-Amp Integrator → CRO Y-input. Mark all four signal paths with arrows.)*
>
> **Procedure Explanation:**
> 1. The toroidal specimen carries two windings: primary $N_1$ (magnetising) and secondary $N_2$ (sensing).
> 2. The primary is energised from an AC source (50 Hz) through a variac (for amplitude control) and a series sampling resistor $R_s$.
> 3. The voltage $V_R$ across $R_s$ is proportional to the primary current $I_p$, which in turn is proportional to the magnetising field $H$. This voltage is fed to the **X-plate** of the CRO.
> 4. The secondary winding picks up the rate of change of flux, $d\Phi/dt$. Its EMF is integrated by an op-amp integrator to produce a voltage proportional to the flux $\Phi$, and hence to $B$. This integrated voltage is fed to the **Y-plate** of the CRO.
> 5. With the CRO's internal timebase disabled (X-Y mode), the spot traces the locus of (H, B) points, drawing the **closed hysteresis loop** on the screen.
> 6. By varying the variac voltage, different loop sizes are obtained; saturation and remanence are easily observed.
>
> *(Award 2 marks for the block diagram, 3 marks for the procedure, 1 mark for CRO X-Y mode mention, 1 mark for result interpretation.)*

> **Model Solution (b):**
>
> **Expression for H (X-axis):**
>
> The primary current is $i_p = I_m \sin(\omega t)$. The voltage across $R_s$ is $v_R = i_p R_s$. By Ampere's Law applied to the toroid:
>
> $$H(t) = \frac{N_1 i_p(t)}{l} = \frac{N_1 v_R(t)}{l \cdot R_s}$$
>
> Therefore, the X-deflection $x$ (in cm) maps to H as:
>
> $$H = k_x \cdot x, \quad \text{where} \quad k_x = \frac{N_1}{l \cdot R_s} \cdot (\text{CRO X-sensitivity in V/cm})$$
>
> **Expression for B (Y-axis):**
>
> The induced EMF in the secondary is $e(t) = -N_2 A \dfrac{dB}{dt}$. The op-amp integrator output is:
>
> $$v_o(t) = -\frac{1}{R_i C_f} \int e(t) \, dt = \frac{N_2 A}{R_i C_f} B(t)$$
>
> Therefore, the Y-deflection $y$ (in cm) maps to B as:
>
> $$B = k_y \cdot y, \quad \text{where} \quad k_y = \frac{1}{R_i C_f N_2 A} \cdot (\text{CRO Y-sensitivity in V/cm})$$
>
> *(Award 3 marks for H derivation with Ampere's Law, 3 marks for B derivation with Faraday's Law and integrator, 1 mark for calibration constants.)*

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024, Module 2 — Alternative Choice]**

**(a)** Draw and explain the **shape of the B–H hysteresis loop** for a typical **ferromagnetic material**. Clearly mark **Retentivity (B_r)**, **Coercivity (H_c)**, **Saturation point (S)**, and indicate the regions of the four quadrants. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A toroidal iron core has the following specifications: $N_1 = 300$, $N_2 = 300$, mean path length $l = 0.5$ m, cross-sectional area $A = 5 \times 10^{-4}$ m², primary current $I_m = 1.5$ A, supply frequency $f = 50$ Hz. The CRO's X-sensitivity is $0.2$ V/cm and Y-sensitivity is $0.5$ V/cm. The series resistor is $R_s = 10$ $\Omega$, and the integrator uses $R_i = 100$ k$\Omega$, $C_f = 1$ $\mu$F. The loop shows a peak horizontal extent of 4 cm and a peak vertical extent of 3 cm.
>
> Calculate:
> (i) $H_{\max}$ and $B_{\max}$ **(3 marks)**
> (ii) The **hysteresis loss per cycle per unit volume** using the **Steinmetz formula** (assume $\eta = 0.002$ for the material). **(4 marks)**

> **Model Solution (a):**
>
> **B–H Loop Diagram (description for KTU answer sheet):**
>
> The loop is a closed figure symmetric about the origin. Quadrant I (H > 0, B > 0): The initial magnetisation curve rises from origin, becomes concave, then approximately linear, and bends over into saturation. As H is reduced to 0, B falls along the upper branch to point **B_r** (Retentivity) on the Y-axis. Quadrant II (H < 0, B > 0): As H becomes negative, B continues to fall to zero at point **H_c** (Coercivity) on the X-axis. Quadrant III (H < 0, B < 0): The material reaches negative saturation at point **S'**. As H returns to zero, B follows to **-B_r**. Quadrant IV (H > 0, B < 0): B rises to zero at **+H_c**, then back to positive saturation at **S**, completing the loop.
>
> **Key Markings:**
> - **B_r** (Retentivity) — Y-intercepts, B value when H = 0.
> - **H_c** (Coercivity) — X-intercepts, H value when B = 0.
> - **S** (Saturation Point) — tips of the loop in Quadrants I and III.
> - **Enclosed Area** ∝ Hysteresis Loss per cycle.
>
> *(Award 2 marks for the diagram, 2 marks for markings, 2 marks for quadrant explanation, 1 mark for relating area to loss.)*

> **Model Solution (b):**
>
> **(i) Calculation of H_max and B_max (3 marks):**
>
> $$H_{\max} = \frac{N_1 \cdot I_m}{l} = \frac{300 \times 1.5}{0.5} = 900 \; \text{A/m}$$
>
> Calibration constant for Y-axis:
>
> $$\begin{aligned}
> k_y &= \frac{(\text{CRO Y-sensitivity})}{R_i \cdot C_f \cdot N_2 \cdot A} \\
> &= \frac{0.5 \; \text{V/cm}}{(10^5)(10^{-6})(300)(5 \times 10^{-4}) \; \text{· (V/T unit conversion)}} \\
> &= \frac{0.5}{15} \approx 0.0333 \; \text{T/cm}
> \end{aligned}$$
>
> Therefore:
>
> $$B_{\max} = k_y \cdot y_{\max} = 0.0333 \times 3 = 0.1 \; \text{T} = 100 \; \text{mT}$$
>
> **(ii) Hysteresis Loss (4 marks):**
>
> Volume of core $V = A \cdot l = 5 \times 10^{-4} \times 0.5 = 2.5 \times 10^{-4}$ m³
>
> Steinmetz formula:
>
> $$\begin{aligned}
> W_h &= \eta \cdot B_{\max}^{1.6} \cdot f \cdot V \\
> &= 0.002 \times (0.1)^{1.6} \times 50 \times 2.5 \times 10^{-4} \\
> &= 0.002 \times 0.0251 \times 50 \times 2.5 \times 10^{-4} \\
> &= 0.002 \times 0.0251 \times 0.0125 \\
> &\approx 6.28 \times 10^{-7} \; \text{W} = 0.628 \; \mu\text{W}
> \end{aligned}$$
>
> *(Award 1 mark for $H_{\max}$, 1 mark for $B_{\max}$ with calibration, 1 mark for Steinmetz formula setup, 1 mark for numerical substitution and final answer.)*

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — "What to Write in the Last 5 Minutes Before Submission"**

- ✅ **H is produced by the primary current** through $N_1$ turns; **B is sensed by the secondary** through $N_2$ turns.
- ✅ **H is proportional to $V_R$** (voltage across series resistor) → goes to **X-plate**.
- ✅ **B is proportional to the integral of secondary EMF** → goes to **Y-plate**.
- ✅ **H formula:** $H = N_1 I / l$  → unit **A/m**.
- ✅ **B formula (after integration):** $B = \dfrac{1}{N_2 A} \displaystyle\int e \, dt$  → unit **Tesla**.
- ✅ **Op-Amp Integrator output:** $V_o = -\dfrac{1}{RC} \displaystyle\int V_{in} \, dt$ — the negative sign matters for loop orientation.
- ✅ **CRO must be in X-Y mode** (timebase off) for hysteresis loop display.
- ✅ **Retentivity $B_r$** is the residual flux when $H = 0$ — Y-axis intercept of the loop.
- ✅ **Coercivity $H_c$** is the reverse field required to bring $B$ to zero — X-axis intercept of the loop.
- ✅ **Area enclosed by the loop** = Hysteresis energy loss per cycle per unit volume.
- ✅ **Steinmetz formula:** $W_h = \eta B_{\max}^{1.6} f V$ (use $B^{1.6}$, NOT $B^2$).
- ✅ **Soft magnetic materials** (e.g., silicon steel) have **narrow loops** — low loss; used in **transformers, motors**.
- ✅ **Hard magnetic materials** (e.g., AlNiCo, NdFeB) have **wide loops** — high $B_r$, high $H_c$; used in **permanent magnets**.
- ✅ **Larger applied voltage** (higher $I_m$) → **larger loop** → higher $B_{\max}$ and $H_{\max}$, but loop shape is the same.
- ✅ **Always calibrate** the CRO axes using $k_x$ and $k_y$ before reading values — examiners award full marks only with calibration shown.
- ✅ **Never connect the secondary directly to CRO Y-input** without an integrator — you'll get the derivative $dB/dt$, not $B$.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Measurement of power in an A.C. circuit by 3 ammeter and 3 voltmeter method
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCSFL408 - ELECTRICAL MACHINES LABORATORY
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Measurement of power in an A.C. circuit by 3 ammeter and 3 voltmeter method

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **3 Ammeter and 3 Voltmeter Methods** are *indirect techniques* used to measure the active power consumed by a single-phase AC load **without using a Wattmeter**. These methods are employed when a wattmeter is unavailable, defective, or when measurements must be performed at frequencies beyond the calibration range of the available wattmeter.

The **3 Ammeter Method** uses three ammeters and a known *non-inductive* (purely resistive) shunt resistor connected in parallel with the load, while the **3 Voltmeter Method** uses three voltmeters and a known *non-inductive* series resistor connected in series with the load. In both methods, the active power is computed indirectly from current, voltage, and resistance readings using the power factor relation derived from the phasor diagram of the circuit.

## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy: Weighing a Parcel Using Only a Ruler**
>
> Imagine you want to find the weight of a parcel but have **no weighing scale**. You know the density of cardboard and measure the parcel's dimensions with a ruler, then **compute** the weight mathematically. The ruler gives you "indirect" data, not the weight directly.
>
> Similarly, in the 3 ammeter / 3 voltmeter method, the **ammeters and voltmeters give us current and voltage readings**, not power directly. From these readings and a known resistor, we **compute the active power** using trigonometric relationships hidden in the phasor diagram — the "mathematical ruler" of AC circuits.

## 1.3 Key Terminology & Standard Metrics

- **Active Power (P):** Real power actually consumed by the load, measured in **Watts (W)** or **kilowatts (kW)**.
- **Reactive Power (Q):** Power exchanged between source and reactive elements, measured in **Volt-Ampere Reactive (VAR)**.
- **Apparent Power (S):** Product of RMS voltage and RMS current, measured in **Volt-Ampere (VA)**.
- **Power Factor (cos φ):** Ratio of active power to apparent power; dimensionless, ranges from **0 to 1**.
- **Non-Inductive Resistor:** A resistor with **negligible inductance**, typically a wire-wound or high-quality carbon resistor. Symbolically it draws current **in phase** with its terminal voltage.
- **Phasor Diagram:** A vector representation of sinusoidal AC quantities (voltage, current) showing their *magnitude* and *phase relationship*.

> [!TIP]
> The two methods are **dual of each other** — replacing ammeters with voltmeters and series with parallel gives a symmetric pair. Mastering one method makes the other intuitive.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The 3 Ammeter Method — Operational Logic

In this method, a **purely resistive (non-inductive) resistor R** is connected in **parallel** with the unknown load Z.

**Circuit Configuration Reasoning:**
1. The supply voltage V is applied across the parallel combination of the load and resistor R.
2. **Ammeter A** measures the **total supply current I** drawn from the source.
3. **Ammeter A1** measures the **load current I_L** flowing through the unknown impedance Z.
4. **Ammeter A2** measures the **current I_R** flowing through the known resistance R.

**Phasor Logic — Why does the power factor formula work?**

The supply current **I** is the phasor sum of **I_L** (current through load, lagging V by angle φ) and **I_R** (current through resistor, in phase with V).

Applying the **cosine rule of triangles** to the phasor triangle:

$$I^2 = I_L^2 + I_R^2 + 2 I_L I_R \cos\phi$$

Rearranging for cos φ:

$$\cos\phi = \frac{I^2 - I_L^2 - I_R^2}{2 I_L I_R}$$

**Power calculation steps:**
- Voltage across the load (and across R) = **V = I_R × R**
- Active power in resistance R = **P_R = I_R² × R** (but this is dissipated in R, NOT in the load)
- Active power consumed by the **load** = **P = V × I_L × cos φ**

> [!NOTE]
> The resistor R must have a value that draws a current comparable to the load current — neither too small (overloads R) nor too large (poor accuracy in I_R reading).

## 2.2 The 3 Voltmeter Method — Operational Logic

In this method, a **purely resistive (non-inductive) resistor R** is connected in **series** with the unknown load Z.

**Circuit Configuration Reasoning:**
1. A single current **I** flows through the series combination of R and the load Z.
2. **Voltmeter V** measures the **total supply voltage V** across (R + Z).
3. **Voltmeter V1** measures the **voltage V_L** across the load.
4. **Voltmeter V2** measures the **voltage V_R** across the known resistance R.

**Phasor Logic — Why does the power factor formula work?**

The supply voltage **V** is the phasor sum of **V_L** (voltage across load, leading I by angle φ) and **V_R** (voltage across resistor, in phase with I).

Applying the **cosine rule of triangles** to the phasor triangle:

$$V^2 = V_L^2 + V_R^2 + 2 V_L V_R \cos\phi$$

Rearranging for cos φ:

$$\cos\phi = \frac{V^2 - V_L^2 - V_R^2}{2 V_L V_R}$$

**Power calculation steps:**
- Current through the circuit = **I = V_R / R**
- Active power in resistance R = **P_R = V_R² / R**
- Active power consumed by the **load** = **P = V × I × cos φ = V_L × I × cos φ**

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| **Parameter** | **3 Ammeter Method** | **3 Voltmeter Method** | **Unit** |
|---|---|---|---|
| Known Quantity | Resistance R (parallel) | Resistance R (series) | Ω (ohms) |
| Total Quantity | I = I_L + I_R (phasor) | V = V_L + V_R (phasor) | A or V |
| Branch Quantity 1 | I_L (load current) | V_L (load voltage) | A or V |
| Branch Quantity 2 | I_R (current through R) | V_R (voltage across R) | A or V |
| Voltage across Load | V = I_R × R | V_L (directly read) | V |
| Current through Load | I_L (directly read) | I = V_R / R | A |
| Power Factor | cos φ = (I² − I_L² − I_R²) / (2 I_L I_R) | cos φ = (V² − V_L² − V_R²) / (2 V_L V_R) | dimensionless |
| Power in Resistor | P_R = I_R² × R | P_R = V_R² / R | W |
| Active Power of Load | P = V × I_L × cos φ | P = V × I × cos φ | W |
| Reactive Power | Q = V × I_L × sin φ | Q = V × I × sin φ | VAR |
| Apparent Power | S = V × I_L | S = V × I | VA |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The single most critical step in both methods is identifying the **cosine-rule sign convention** correctly. The "+" sign appears because the angle between I_L and I_R (or V_L and V_R) is **less than 90°**, not supplementary.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Calibration Standard:** These methods serve as a *reference technique* to cross-check wattmeter accuracy in **metrology laboratories** and **testing houses**.
- **High-Frequency Power Measurement:** Wattmeters are inaccurate above **1 kHz**; the 3 ammeter/voltmeter method works for **kHz to MHz** ranges.
- **High-Voltage / High-Current Setups:** Standard wattmeter current coils have limited ratings; these methods let engineers use *precision shunts* or *precision resistors* to extend measurement range.
- **Educational Value:** Found in **power systems, electrical machines, and measurements labs** across all engineering disciplines for teaching phasor analysis.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Power Factor Formula for 3 Ammeter Method

**Step 1 — Setup the phasor diagram:**
Let the supply voltage **V** be taken as the reference phasor along the positive x-axis.

- Current **I_R** through resistor R: in phase with V → lies along the reference axis.
- Current **I_L** through load Z: lags V by an angle **φ** (assuming inductive load) → lies at angle −φ from V.
- Total current **I**: phasor sum of I_L and I_R.

**Step 2 — Construct the current phasor triangle:**
The three phasors I, I_L, and I_R form a closed triangle.

**Step 3 — Apply the cosine rule of triangles:**
For any triangle with sides a, b, c and angle θ between sides a and b, the opposite side c satisfies:
$$c^2 = a^2 + b^2 - 2ab\cos(\pi - \theta)$$

But here the angle between I_L and I_R is **φ** (not π − φ) because I_R is along the reference and I_L is below it.

Direct application:
$$I^2 = I_L^2 + I_R^2 + 2 I_L I_R \cos\phi$$

> The "+" sign appears because the angle **inside** the phasor triangle at vertex I_R is **φ** (cos of φ is positive for φ < 90°).

**Step 4 — Solve for cos φ:**
$$I^2 = I_L^2 + I_R^2 + 2 I_L I_R \cos\phi$$

$$2 I_L I_R \cos\phi = I^2 - I_L^2 - I_R^2$$

$$\boxed{\cos\phi = \frac{I^2 - I_L^2 - I_R^2}{2 I_L I_R}}$$

**Step 5 — Compute the load power:**
Voltage across the load equals voltage across R (parallel connection):
$$V = I_R \times R$$

Active power consumed by the load:
$$\boxed{P = V \cdot I_L \cdot \cos\phi = I_R \cdot R \cdot I_L \cdot \cos\phi}$$

## 3.2 Derivation: Power Factor Formula for 3 Voltmeter Method

**Step 1 — Setup the phasor diagram:**
Let the circuit current **I** be the reference phasor (since it's a series circuit, the same current flows through R and Z).

- Voltage **V_R** across resistor R: in phase with I → lies along the reference axis.
- Voltage **V_L** across load Z: leads I by an angle **φ** (assuming inductive load) → lies at angle +φ from V_R.
- Total voltage **V**: phasor sum of V_L and V_R.

**Step 2 — Construct the voltage phasor triangle:**
The three phasors V, V_L, and V_R form a closed triangle.

**Step 3 — Apply the cosine rule of triangles:**
$$V^2 = V_L^2 + V_R^2 + 2 V_L V_R \cos\phi$$

**Step 4 — Solve for cos φ:**
$$\boxed{\cos\phi = \frac{V^2 - V_L^2 - V_R^2}{2 V_L V_R}}$$

**Step 5 — Compute the load power:**
Current through the circuit (and through the load):
$$I = \frac{V_R}{R}$$

Active power consumed by the load:
$$\boxed{P = V \cdot I \cdot \cos\phi = V \cdot \frac{V_R}{R} \cdot \cos\phi}$$

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example — 3 Ammeter Method

> **Problem Statement:**
> In a 3 ammeter test on a single-phase inductive load, the following readings were obtained:
> Total current I = **20 A**, Load current I_L = **15 A**, Current through resistor I_R = **12 A**, Resistor R = **10 Ω**.
> Calculate: (a) Power factor, (b) Power consumed by the load, (c) Reactive power.

**Solution:**

**(a) Power Factor:**

$$\cos\phi = \frac{I^2 - I_L^2 - I_R^2}{2 I_L I_R}$$

$$\cos\phi = \frac{(20)^2 - (15)^2 - (12)^2}{2 \times 15 \times 12}$$

$$\cos\phi = \frac{400 - 225 - 144}{360} = \frac{31}{360}$$

$$\cos\phi = 0.0861 \text{ (lagging)}$$

**Verification by sine rule (alternate method):**
The phasor triangle has sides I=20, I_L=15, I_R=12. Check triangle inequality: 12 + 15 = 27 > 20 ✓, 12 + 20 = 32 > 15 ✓, 15 + 20 = 35 > 12 ✓. Triangle is valid.

$$\phi = \cos^{-1}(0.0861) = 85.06°$$

**(b) Load Power:**

Voltage across load:
$$V = I_R \times R = 12 \times 10 = 120 \text{ V}$$

Active power of load:
$$P = V \times I_L \times \cos\phi = 120 \times 15 \times 0.0861$$

$$P = 1800 \times 0.0861 = 154.98 \text{ W} \approx 155 \text{ W}$$

**(c) Reactive Power:**

$$\sin\phi = \sin(85.06°) = 0.9963$$

$$Q = V \times I_L \times \sin\phi = 120 \times 15 \times 0.9963 = 1793.34 \text{ VAR} \approx 1.79 \text{ kVAR}$$

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Comparison Table

| **Aspect** | **3 Ammeter Method** | **3 Voltmeter Method** |
|---|---|---|
| Known Element | R in **parallel** with load | R in **series** with load |
| Instruments | 3 Ammeters (A, A1, A2) | 3 Voltmeters (V, V1, V2) |
| Direct Read Quantities | I, I_L, I_R | V, V_L, V_R |
| Computed V or I | V = I_R × R | I = V_R / R |
| Computed cos φ | (I² − I_L² − I_R²) / (2 I_L I_R) | (V² − V_L² − V_R²) / (2 V_L V_R) |
| Source Requirement | Low-voltage, high-current | High-voltage, low-current |
| Typical Application | Low-voltage inductive loads | High-voltage transformer testing |
| Accuracy | Limited by ammeter accuracy | Limited by voltmeter accuracy |
| Practical Issue | Risk of overloading R | Risk of overloading voltmeters |

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Sign Error in Cosine Rule:**
> Many students incorrectly write **I² = I_L² + I_R² − 2 I_L I_R cos φ** (with a minus sign) by treating the angle as π − φ. The correct sign is **+** because the phasor triangle is formed *externally* with angle φ at the resistor-current vertex, not supplementary.

## 3.5 Sensitivity & Error Analysis

**Sources of Error in 3 Ammeter Method:**
- Ammeter resistance is **non-zero** (typically 0.01–0.1 Ω), causing a small voltage drop in the ammeter coil that appears across R.
- The "non-inductive" resistor R may have a small **stray inductance** at high frequencies.

**Sources of Error in 3 Voltmeter Method:**
- Voltmeters are **non-ideal** (typically 10 kΩ/V), drawing a small current that disturbs the series circuit.
- Voltmeter loading effect is most severe when measuring V_L across a high-impedance load.

**Choosing the Resistor R:**
- R should be chosen so that I_R ≈ I_L (3 ammeter method) or V_R ≈ V_L (3 voltmeter method) for **maximum sensitivity** of the cos φ formula.
- A ratio of 1:1 gives the steepest gradient; ratios of 1:3 or 3:1 give acceptable but reduced sensitivity.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
