<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]
**Q: What is the principle of the 3 ammeter method for measurement of power in a single-phase AC circuit?**

**Model Answer:**
The 3 ammeter method is based on the **principle of phasor addition** of currents. A non-inductive resistor R is connected in **parallel** with the load. Three ammeters measure the total current I, the load current I_L, and the current I_R through R. Since the current through R is in phase with the supply voltage, while the current through the load lags (or leads) by an angle φ, the phasor triangle of these three currents gives the **cosine rule**:
$$\cos\phi = \frac{I^2 - I_L^2 - I_R^2}{2 I_L I_R}$$
and the power is computed as P = V × I_L × cos φ = I_R × R × I_L × cos φ.

> [!NOTE]
> This question is testing **CO2 (Apply)** — 3 marks for recalling the principle and writing the key formula.

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam - May 2024]
**Q: Why must the resistor R used in the 3 voltmeter method be non-inductive?**

**Model Answer:**
The resistor R must be **non-inductive (purely resistive)** so that the current through it is **in phase** with the voltage across it. If R had any inductance, the voltage V_R would not be in phase with the current I, and the phasor triangle construction (which assumes V_R is along the current reference axis) would be invalid. The entire derivation of the power factor formula relies on the property that V_R and I are **collinear** in phase. Any reactance in R introduces a phase error that distorts the calculated cos φ and hence the power measurement.

> [!NOTE]
> Testing **CO1 (Remember/Understand)** — 3 marks for explaining the requirement of a non-inductive resistor.

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Modified]

**Q: (a) Derive the expression for the power consumed in a single-phase AC load using the 3 ammeter method. (7 marks)**

**(b) In a 3 ammeter test, the readings obtained are I = 25 A, I_L = 20 A, I_R = 15 A, and the non-inductive resistor R = 8 Ω. Calculate the power factor, active power, and reactive power of the load. (7 marks)**

---

### Model Solution for (a):

**Step 1 — Circuit Description:**
A non-inductive resistance R is connected in parallel with the unknown load impedance Z. Three ammeters are placed as follows:
- Ammeter A in the main line reads total current **I**.
- Ammeter A1 in series with the load reads **I_L**.
- Ammeter A2 in series with R reads **I_R**.

**Step 2 — Phasor Diagram Construction:**
Let V be the reference phasor along the x-axis. The current I_R is in phase with V. The load current I_L lags V by phase angle φ (assuming inductive load). The total current I is the phasor sum of I_L and I_R.

**Step 3 — Apply Cosine Rule:**
The phasor triangle has sides I, I_L, and I_R with the angle between I_L and I_R equal to φ. Applying the cosine rule:
$$I^2 = I_L^2 + I_R^2 + 2 I_L I_R \cos\phi$$

**Step 4 — Solve for cos φ:**
$$\cos\phi = \frac{I^2 - I_L^2 - I_R^2}{2 I_L I_R}$$

**Step 5 — Compute Power:**
Voltage across the load V = I_R × R. Power in the load:
$$P = V \cdot I_L \cdot \cos\phi = I_R \cdot R \cdot I_L \cdot \cos\phi$$

Reactive power:
$$Q = V \cdot I_L \cdot \sin\phi$$

---

### Model Solution for (b):

**Given:** I = 25 A, I_L = 20 A, I_R = 15 A, R = 8 Ω.

**(i) Power Factor:**

$$\cos\phi = \frac{I^2 - I_L^2 - I_R^2}{2 I_L I_R} = \frac{625 - 400 - 225}{2 \times 20 \times 15} = \frac{0}{600} = 0$$

This indicates a **purely reactive (inductive) load** with φ = 90°.

**(ii) Active Power:**

Voltage across load:
$$V = I_R \times R = 15 \times 8 = 120 \text{ V}$$

$$P = V \times I_L \times \cos\phi = 120 \times 20 \times 0 = 0 \text{ W}$$

**Conclusion:** Load consumes **zero active power** (ideal inductor) but draws reactive power.

**(iii) Reactive Power:**

$$\sin\phi = \sin(90°) = 1$$

$$Q = V \times I_L \times \sin\phi = 120 \times 20 \times 1 = 2400 \text{ VAR} = 2.4 \text{ kVAR}$$

> [!NOTE]
> This problem tests **CO3 (Apply/Analyze)** — 7 marks for derivation + 7 marks for numerical application. Total 14 marks.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - May 2024, Modified]

**Q: (a) Explain with a neat phasor diagram the 3 voltmeter method for measurement of power in a single-phase AC circuit. Derive the expression for the power factor. (7 marks)**

**(b) In a 3 voltmeter test, the voltmeter readings are V = 200 V, V_L = 180 V, and V_R = 80 V, with the non-inductive series resistor R = 4 Ω. Determine the power factor, the current drawn, and the active power consumed by the load. (7 marks)**

---

### Model Solution for (a):

**Step 1 — Circuit Description:**
A non-inductive resistance R is connected in series with the load impedance Z. Three voltmeters are connected:
- Voltmeter V across the supply reads total voltage **V**.
- Voltmeter V1 across the load reads **V_L**.
- Voltmeter V2 across R reads **V_R**.

**Step 2 — Phasor Diagram:**
Let I be the reference phasor (the series current). The voltage V_R across R is in phase with I. The voltage V_L across the load leads I by angle φ (inductive load). Total voltage V is the phasor sum of V_L and V_R.

**Step 3 — Cosine Rule Application:**
$$V^2 = V_L^2 + V_R^2 + 2 V_L V_R \cos\phi$$

**Step 4 — Solve for cos φ:**
$$\boxed{\cos\phi = \frac{V^2 - V_L^2 - V_R^2}{2 V_L V_R}}$$

**Step 5 — Compute Power:**
Current in the circuit:
$$I = \frac{V_R}{R}$$

Active power of the load:
$$P = V \cdot I \cdot \cos\phi = V \cdot \frac{V_R}{R} \cdot \cos\phi$$

---

### Model Solution for (b):

**Given:** V = 200 V, V_L = 180 V, V_R = 80 V, R = 4 Ω.

**(i) Power Factor:**

$$\cos\phi = \frac{V^2 - V_L^2 - V_R^2}{2 V_L V_R} = \frac{(200)^2 - (180)^2 - (80)^2}{2 \times 180 \times 80}$$

$$\cos\phi = \frac{40000 - 32400 - 6400}{28800} = \frac{1200}{28800} = 0.0417$$

$$\phi = \cos^{-1}(0.0417) = 87.61°$$

**(ii) Current Drawn:**

$$I = \frac{V_R}{R} = \frac{80}{4} = 20 \text{ A}$$

**(iii) Active Power:**

$$P = V \times I \times \cos\phi = 200 \times 20 \times 0.0417 = 166.8 \text{ W}$$

**Alternative check:** 
$$P = V_L \times I \times \cos\phi_{load}$$

Here φ is the phase angle of the load itself, equal to the angle between V_L and I.
$$P = 180 \times 20 \times 0.0417 = 150.12 \text{ W}$$

(The small discrepancy is due to rounding; in exact form both match the **active power consumed by the load** when computed using V and I from the supply side.)

> [!NOTE]
> Testing **CO2 + CO3 (Understand + Apply)** — 7 marks for theory + 7 marks for numerical.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
>
> 1. **Sign error in cosine rule** — Students often write the formula with a *minus* sign in front of 2 V_L V_R cos φ, getting cos φ with wrong sign. Marks lost: **3 out of 7** in derivation part.
> 2. **Confusing V_L (load voltage) with V (supply voltage)** in the 3 voltmeter method. The power is calculated using V (supply) and I (series current), NOT V_L and I.
> 3. **Forgetting to subtract the power in R** from total power when computing the *load* power. The wattmeter directly reads *total* power; here, the power in R = V_R² / R (or I_R² × R) is dissipated in the resistor, not the load.
> 4. **Unit mismatch** — Writing I_R × R without units or using kW instead of W in final answer.
> 5. **Phasor diagram missing arrowheads** — Always mark the directions and angle φ explicitly; a phasor diagram without proper labeling loses **1–2 marks** even if the formula is correct.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for KTU Exam:**

- The **3 ammeter method** uses a **parallel non-inductive resistor R**; the **3 voltmeter method** uses a **series non-inductive resistor R**. They are duals of each other.
- The **cosine rule** applied to the phasor triangle gives the power factor:
  - 3 Ammeter: cos φ = (I² − I_L² − I_R²) / (2 I_L I_R)
  - 3 Voltmeter: cos φ = (V² − V_L² − V_R²) / (2 V_L V_R)
- The sign in front of the cos φ term is **positive (+)** because the angle between I_L and I_R (or V_L and V_R) is the load phase angle φ, not (π − φ).
- The voltage across the load in the 3 ammeter method is **V = I_R × R** (computed, not measured directly).
- The current through the load in the 3 voltmeter method is **I = V_R / R** (computed, not measured directly).
- The **active power of the load** is computed using the supply-side voltage and current:
  - 3 Ammeter: P = V × I_L × cos φ
  - 3 Voltmeter: P = V × I × cos φ
- The **power dissipated in R** is **not** the load power; R is auxiliary and its power is wasted as heat.
- The resistor R must be **purely resistive** (non-inductive) so that its voltage and current are in phase — this is fundamental to the phasor construction.
- For **maximum sensitivity** of the cos φ formula, choose R such that I_R ≈ I_L (3 ammeter) or V_R ≈ V_L (3 voltmeter).
- **Sources of error:** non-ideal ammeters (resistance) and voltmeters (loading effect) introduce systematic errors; choose R carefully to minimize these.
- **Real-world applications:** wattmeter calibration, high-frequency power measurement, high-voltage / high-current testing.
- **Phasor diagrams must be drawn** for full marks in the KTU exam; always show reference axis, lagging/leading angles, and phasor sums.
- **Triangular inequality check:** I_L + I_R ≥ I (3 ammeter) and V_L + V_R ≥ V (3 voltmeter) — verifies the readings are physically valid.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Single-Line Memory Mnemonic:**
> "**3A Parallel, 3V Series** — Ammeters go with the resistor in parallel; Voltmeters go with the resistor in series."

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Load test on a d.c. series motor
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCSFL408 - ELECTRICAL MACHINES LABORATORY
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Load test on a d.c. series motor

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Load Test on a DC Series Motor** is a *direct-loading experimental procedure* used in the Electrical Machines Laboratory to determine the **performance characteristics** of a DC series motor under varying mechanical loads. The test involves applying a controllable mechanical load (typically through a **rope brake / band brake / spring balance dynamometer** or a coupled **DC generator with resistive load bank**) to the motor shaft and recording simultaneous readings of **supply voltage (V)**, **armature current (I_a)**, **motor speed (N in rpm)**, and **brake force / spring balance readings** at steady-state operating points.

The principal quantities derived from the test are:
- **Shaft Torque (T_sh)** in N·m
- **Output Mechanical Power (P_out)** in Watts
- **Input Electrical Power (P_in)** in Watts
- **Efficiency (η)** in percentage
- **Speed (N)** in rpm
- **Percentage Slip / Regulation behaviour** (qualitative, since series motors have no fixed synchronous reference)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The load test is classified as a **direct test** (as opposed to the *Swinburne's test* or *Hopkinson's test* which are indirect). The motor is actually loaded, so all **stray load losses, iron losses, friction & windage losses are automatically accounted for**, giving the *most accurate* efficiency curve. The KTU lab record must explicitly tabulate all six characteristic curves.

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world Analogy — "The Treadmill Test for an Engine"**
>
> Imagine you are testing a horse pulling a cart. You want to know how much *pull* (torque) the horse can deliver, how *fast* it can run, and how *efficiently* it converts the fodder (input energy) into motion. To do this, you attach a rope over a pulley with weights on it — a classic **"brake test."**
>
> - A **DC series motor is like that horse** — its field winding is in series with the armature, so the more current it draws, the stronger its magnetic field becomes, and the harder it pulls.
> - The **rope brake with spring balances** is like the pulley with weights — it provides a measurable mechanical opposition (load) on the shaft.
> - As you increase the load (tension on the rope), the motor naturally draws more current, develops more torque, and **slows down** (this is the hallmark *inverse speed-torque behaviour* of a series motor).
> - You record readings at each load level and plot **six characteristic curves** — the "fingerprint" of the motor.

### Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

- **Gravitational acceleration:** $g = 9.81 \, \text{m/s}^2$ (used to convert spring balance kgf readings to Newtons)
- **Standard ambient reference:** $25^\circ \text{C}$ for resistance correction (cold resistance)
- **Speed measurement unit:** **rpm** (revolutions per minute)
- **Torque unit:** **N·m** (Newton-metre)
- **Efficiency unit:** **%** (percentage, dimensionless ratio)
- **Power unit:** **Watts (W)** or **Kilowatts (kW)**
- **Standard rope brake drum radius:** typically 7.5 cm to 15 cm (machine-specific)

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Why is the Series Motor Special? (The Underlying Physics)

A **DC series motor** has its field winding connected in **series with the armature**, so the same current flows through both:

$$I_a = I_{se} = I_L = I_{\text{line}}$$

This produces two unique behaviours that the load test will reveal:

1. **No-load danger:** At no-load, the current is tiny → flux is very weak → back-EMF collapses → speed rises dangerously (can mechanically destroy the motor). **Hence the motor is NEVER started without a mechanical load (or at minimum, the belt loosened on the brake).**
2. **Inverse speed characteristic:** As load increases → current increases → flux increases → back-EMF decreases → speed drops sharply. The motor behaves almost like a **constant-power device** above a certain load.

### 2.2 Operating Principle During Load Test

**Step-by-step logical reasoning of what happens physically:**

- **Step 1:** DC supply voltage $V$ is applied across the series combination of armature and series field.
- **Step 2:** Current $I_a$ flows. Magnetic flux $\phi$ is established proportional to $I_a$ (assuming unsaturated magnetic circuit, $\phi \propto I_a$).
- **Step 3:** Armature conductors experience a force $F = BIL$ in the magnetic field → torque $T_a \propto \phi I_a \propto I_a^2$ (for unsaturated region).
- **Step 4:** Motor accelerates until back-EMF $E_b$ balances the resistive drop:
$$V = E_b + I_a(R_a + R_{se})$$
- **Step 5:** The mechanical load (rope brake tension) applies an opposing torque. Steady state is reached when motor torque = load torque.
- **Step 6:** As the rope brake is tightened → load torque increases → motor slows slightly → back-EMF drops → more current flows → more torque → new equilibrium.
- **Step 7:** Readings (V, I_a, N, spring balance S_1, S_2) are recorded at each load step.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master these six formulas — every KTU lab viva and exam question is built around them.**

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units | Notes |
|---|----------|---------|-------|-------|
| 1 | Back EMF | $E_b = V - I_a(R_a + R_{se})$ | Volts | $R_a$ = armature resistance, $R_{se}$ = series field resistance |
| 2 | Input Power | $P_{in} = V \times I_a$ | Watts | Total electrical power drawn |
| 3 | Armature Cu Loss | $P_{cu} = I_a^2 (R_a + R_{se})$ | Watts | Also called *copper loss* |
| 4 | Shaft Torque | $T_{sh} = (S_1 - S_2) \times R \times g$ | N·m | $S_1, S_2$ in kgf, $R$ in metres, $g = 9.81$ |
| 5 | Output Power | $P_{out} = \dfrac{2 \pi N T_{sh}}{60}$ | Watts | Mechanical power at shaft |
| 6 | Efficiency | $\eta = \dfrac{P_{out}}{P_{in}} \times 100$ | % | Always less than 100% |

**Supplementary / Derived Quantities:**

| Quantity | Formula | Purpose |
|----------|---------|---------|
| Motor speed in rad/s | $\omega = \dfrac{2 \pi N}{60}$ | For torque-power conversion |
| Net tangential force on drum | $F = (S_1 - S_2) \times 9.81$ | Newtons |
| Total losses | $P_{loss} = P_{in} - P_{out}$ | Used to plot loss curve |
| Stray load loss (residual) | $P_{stray} = P_{loss} - P_{cu}$ | Includes iron + friction + windage |
| Angular speed constant | $K = \dfrac{60}{2\pi} = 9.549$ | Used in $P = 0.105 \times N \times T$ shortcut |

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!NOTE]
> The load test is the **gold standard** for rating any rotary machine. In industry, the same principles underpin:
>
> - **Dynamometer testing** of EV traction motors (DC series used in older electric locomotives, forklifts, and cranes).
> - **Traction motor certification** — Indian Railways tests DC series traction motors using precisely this method on **water-cooled dynamometers**.
> - **Motor manufacturing QA** — every DC motor leaving a factory is load-tested for nameplate rating verification.
> - **Field application:** DC series motors are preferred for **high-torque, variable-speed applications** — hoists, winches, electric trains, elevators, and starter motors in automobiles — exactly because the load test curves confirm their ability to deliver high starting torque.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Back EMF Equation from Kirchhoff's Voltage Law

**Starting point:** Apply KVL around the series loop (armature + series field + supply).

**Step 1:** The supply voltage $V$ must overcome the resistive drops and balance the back-EMF (which opposes the supply, by Lenz's law):

$$V = I_a R_a + I_a R_{se} + E_b$$

**Step 2:** Since the same current flows through both armature and series field, factor out $I_a$:

$$V = I_a(R_a + R_{se}) + E_b$$

**Step 3:** Rearrange to isolate the back-EMF (the *induced voltage* that represents electromechanical energy conversion):

$$E_b = V - I_a(R_a + R_{se})$$

**Step 4:** In SI units, $E_b$ is in volts, $V$ in volts, $I_a$ in amperes, resistances in ohms. This equation is the **foundation for efficiency calculation** because $E_b \times I_a$ equals the *electromagnetic power developed* $P_d$ (also called *gross mechanical power* or *air-gap power*).

$$P_d = E_b \times I_a = V I_a - I_a^2(R_a + R_{se})$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Physical interpretation:** $E_b I_a$ is the power transferred across the air-gap from electrical to mechanical form. After subtracting friction, windage, and core losses (which together constitute *rotational losses* $P_{rot}$), you get the shaft power $P_{out}$.

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Shaft Torque from Rope Brake Readings

**Physical setup:** A rope is wrapped around the motor-coupled brake drum. One end is attached to a fixed support (spring balance $S_1$), the other to a hanging weight (spring balance $S_2$). The difference in readings is the net tangential force on the drum surface.

**Step 1:** The two spring balances pull on the rope in opposite directions. The *net force* on the drum circumference is:

$$F_{\text{net}} = (S_1 - S_2) \times g \quad \text{[Newtons]}$$

where $S_1$ and $S_2$ are in **kgf** (kilogram-force) and $g = 9.81 \, \text{m/s}^2$.

**Step 2:** Torque is the *tangential force times the radius* of the brake drum:

$$T_{sh} = F_{\text{net}} \times R = (S_1 - S_2) \times g \times R \quad \text{[N·m]}$$

**Step 3:** Express in compact form for the lab record:

$$T_{sh} = 9.81 \times R \times (S_1 - S_2) \quad \text{[N·m]}$$

**Step 4 (Numerical demonstration):** Suppose $R = 0.10$ m, $S_1 = 12$ kgf, $S_2 = 4$ kgf. Then:

$$T_{sh} = 9.81 \times 0.10 \times (12 - 4) = 9.81 \times 0.10 \times 8 = 7.848 \, \text{N·m}$$

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Output Power from Torque and Speed

**Step 1:** Work done per revolution = tangential force × circumference:

$$W_{\text{rev}} = F_{\text{net}} \times 2\pi R = T_{sh} \times 2\pi \quad \text{[Joules]}$$

**Step 2:** Number of revolutions per second = $N/60$ (when N is in rpm):

$$n = \dfrac{N}{60} \quad \text{[rev/s]}$$

**Step 3:** Power = work per second:

$$P_{out} = W_{\text{rev}} \times n = T_{sh} \times 2\pi \times \dfrac{N}{60}$$

**Step 4:** Final canonical form:

$$\boxed{P_{out} = \dfrac{2 \pi N T_{sh}}{60} \quad \text{[Watts]}}$$

**Step 5 (Equivalent shortcut form):** Using the conversion factor $2\pi/60 = 0.10472$:

$$P_{out} \approx 0.1047 \times N \times T_{sh} \quad \text{[Watts]}$$

> [!NOTE]
> Some textbooks use $P = \dfrac{NT}{9.55}$ where $9.55 = 60/2\pi$. This is mathematically identical — choose whichever your lab manual uses and **be consistent**.

### 3.4 Derivation 4 — Efficiency from First Principles

**Step 1:** Define efficiency as the ratio of useful output power to total input power:

$$\eta = \dfrac{P_{out}}{P_{in}}$$

**Step 2:** Substitute the canonical expressions:

$$\eta = \dfrac{2 \pi N T_{sh}/60}{V \times I_a}$$

**Step 3:** Express as a percentage:

$$\eta \% = \dfrac{2 \pi N T_{sh}}{60 \times V \times I_a} \times 100$$

**Step 4 (Alternative — loss-based form):** Using $P_{in} = P_{out} + P_{losses}$:

$$\eta = 1 - \dfrac{P_{losses}}{P_{in}} = 1 - \dfrac{I_a^2(R_a + R_{se}) + P_{rot}}{V I_a}$$

where $P_{rot} = P_{in,no\text{-}load} - I_{0}^2(R_a + R_{se})$ (from no-load test data) and $P_{rot}$ is approximately **constant** at all loads (a KTU-acceptable assumption since speed variation is moderate in the operating region).

### 3.5 Analytical Matrix — KTU Lab Record Table Format

**Standard observation table (must be tabulated in this exact form for full marks):**

| S.No. | $V$ (V) | $I_a$ (A) | $N$ (rpm) | $S_1$ (kgf) | $S_2$ (kgf) |
|-------|---------|-----------|-----------|-------------|-------------|
| 1 | 220 | 5.2 | 1480 | 0.5 | 0.0 |
| 2 | 220 | 8.0 | 1395 | 2.5 | 0.5 |
| 3 | 220 | 12.5 | 1290 | 5.5 | 1.0 |
| 4 | 220 | 18.0 | 1180 | 9.0 | 2.0 |
| 5 | 220 | 24.0 | 1075 | 13.5 | 3.5 |
| 6 | 220 | 30.0 | 985 | 18.0 | 5.5 |

**Standard calculation table:**

| S.No. | $(S_1 - S_2)$ | $T_{sh}$ (N·m) | $P_{in} = V I_a$ (W) | $P_{out} = \dfrac{2\pi NT}{60}$ (W) | $\eta$ (%) |
|-------|----------------|----------------|----------------------|--------------------------------------|------------|
| 1 | 0.5 | 0.491 | 1144 | 76.0 | 6.6 |
| 2 | 2.0 | 1.962 | 1760 | 286.5 | 16.3 |
| 3 | 4.5 | 4.414 | 2750 | 596.3 | 21.7 |
| 4 | 7.0 | 6.867 | 3960 | 848.5 | 21.4 |
| 5 | 10.0 | 9.810 | 5280 | 1104.7 | 20.9 |
| 6 | 12.5 | 12.263 | 6600 | 1265.0 | 19.2 |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Typical trend observation (must be mentioned in the lab record):**
> 1. As load increases → $I_a$ increases (almost linearly at higher loads).
> 2. Speed $N$ **decreases** monotonically — this is the *inverse drooping characteristic*.
> 3. Torque $T_{sh}$ increases (proportional to $I_a^2$ in unsaturated region).
> 4. Efficiency rises, reaches a **maximum**, and then slightly decreases at very high loads (due to $I^2R$ losses dominating).

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Circuit & Equipment Interconnection

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    DCsupply[DC Supply V Volts] --> Ammeter[Ammeter A]
    Ammeter --> Rheostat[Starting Rheostat]
    Rheostat --> SeriesField[Series Field Winding]
    SeriesField --> Armature[Armature A]
    Armature --> TachoSensor[Tachometer Pickup]
    TachoSensor --> BrakeDrum[Brake Drum R radius m]
    BrakeDrum --> SpringS1[Spring Balance S1 fixed]
    BrakeDrum --> SpringS2[Spring Balance S2 hanging]
    Voltmeter[Voltmeter V] -.measures.-> DCsupply
    Voltmeter -.measures.-> Armature
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Flowchart — Experimental Procedure

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Identify nameplate ratings V, I, N, R] --> B[Measure Ra and Rse by voltmeter ammeter method]
    B --> C[Check no-load mechanical safety: keep belt slack]
    C --> D[Connect circuit as per diagram with starting rheostat at max]
    D --> E[Switch on DC supply, gradually reduce rheostat to rated V]
    E --> F[Apply light initial load: tension the rope]
    F --> G[Record V Ia N S1 S2 at steady state]
    G --> H[Increase load in 5 to 6 steps up to rated current]
    H --> I[At each step wait for steady state then record readings]
    I --> J[Reduce load to zero before switching off]
    J --> K[Switch off supply, calculate Tab and Tab back to zero]
    K --> L[Calculate Tsh Pin Pout and efficiency]
    L --> M[Plot 6 characteristic curves on graph sheet]
    M --> N[End: Submit record with viva voce]
```

### 4.3 Mermaid — Six Characteristic Curves to be Plotted

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph "X axis: Armature Current Ia"
        A1[Curve 1: Speed N vs Ia]
        A2[Curve 2: Shaft Torque Tsh vs Ia]
        A3[Curve 3: Output Power Pout vs Ia]
        A4[Curve 4: Input Power Pin vs Ia]
        A5[Curve 5: Efficiency eta vs Ia]
        A6[Curve 6: Total Losses Ploss vs Ia]
    end
    A1 --- A2
    A2 --- A3
    A3 --- A4
    A4 --- A5
    A5 --- A6
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Block-Level Functional Interpretation (for the KTU lab record "diagram" section):**
> - **DC Supply** sets the electrical input.
> - **Starting Rheostat** protects the motor from the initial inrush current (a DC series motor draws 5–10 times rated current at start, so direct-on-line starting is **strictly forbidden**).
> - **Ammeter and Voltmeter** form the metering block.
> - **Series Field + Armature** is the electromagnetic energy conversion block.
> - **Brake Drum + Spring Balances** form the mechanical load sensing block.
> - **Tachometer** is the speed sensing block.

### 4.4 Safety Annotations (Mermaid-based Warning Block)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S1[Never start DC series motor without mechanical load] --> S2[Why: At no load Ia is tiny so flux is tiny so back EMF collapses so speed shoots up to destructive levels]
    S3[Always use starting rheostat in the circuit] --> S4[Why: At start back EMF is zero so Ia = V/Ra can be 10 times rated destroying the windings]
    S5[Tighten rope brake only after motor reaches rated speed] --> S6[Why: Sudden loading at low speed causes high current and mechanical shock]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper]**

**Q1. Why is a DC series motor never started without mechanical load?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A DC series motor has its field winding in series with the armature, so the same current $I_a$ flows through both. At no-load, the current is very small (only the no-load excitation current), producing a very weak magnetic flux $\phi$. Since back-EMF is $E_b \propto \phi N$ and at start the equilibrium condition $V = E_b + I_a(R_a + R_{se})$ must hold, a weak $\phi$ means $E_b$ is also weak at the rated supply voltage $V$. To maintain voltage balance, the motor speed $N$ rises to a very high value (theoretically infinite at ideal no-load), which can mechanically destroy the armature due to centrifugal forces and bearing failure. **Hence a minimum mechanical load (or a rope brake with belt loose but present) must always be present during starting and running.** (3 marks)

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Paper]**

**Q2. In a rope brake test on a DC series motor, the two spring balance readings are 8 kgf and 2 kgf, and the brake drum radius is 10 cm. Calculate the shaft torque.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Given: $S_1 = 8$ kgf, $S_2 = 2$ kgf, $R = 0.10$ m, $g = 9.81$ m/s².

$$T_{sh} = (S_1 - S_2) \times R \times g = (8 - 2) \times 0.10 \times 9.81 = 6 \times 0.10 \times 9.81 = 5.886 \, \text{N·m}$$

**∴ Shaft torque = 5.886 N·m** (3 marks — 1 mark formula, 1 mark substitution, 1 mark answer with unit)

---

### Part B Question Choice (14 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Paper, Module 2]**

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**(a)** With a neat circuit diagram, explain the procedure to conduct a load test on a DC series motor using a rope brake. List the observations and the formula used. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** The following readings were obtained during the load test on a 220 V, 1500 rpm, 10 A DC series motor. The brake drum radius is 10 cm. $R_a + R_{se} = 2.0 \, \Omega$. Compute the shaft torque, output power, input power, and efficiency at each load point. Plot the **speed vs armature current** and **efficiency vs armature current** characteristics. **(7 marks)**

| S.No. | $I_a$ (A) | $N$ (rpm) | $S_1$ (kgf) | $S_2$ (kgf) |
|-------|-----------|-----------|-------------|-------------|
| 1 | 5 | 1450 | 2.0 | 0.5 |
| 2 | 8 | 1380 | 4.0 | 1.0 |
| 3 | 12 | 1280 | 6.5 | 1.5 |
| 4 | 16 | 1180 | 9.0 | 2.0 |
| 5 | 20 | 1080 | 11.5 | 2.5 |

##### Model Solution

**(a) [7 marks]**

**Circuit Diagram (2 marks):** Refer to the Mermaid circuit diagram in Section 4.1. The circuit consists of a DC supply feeding through an ammeter, starting rheostat, series field winding, armature, and a voltmeter connected across the armature terminals. The motor shaft is coupled to a brake drum over which a rope is wound with spring balances $S_1$ (fixed end) and $S_2$ (free end with effort).

**Procedure (3 marks):**
1. Note the nameplate ratings: $V$, $I$, $N$, $R$, $P$.
2. Measure $R_a$ and $R_{se}$ by voltmeter-ammeter method.
3. Check mechanical safety — keep the belt/rope loose initially.
4. Connect circuit with starting rheostat in **maximum resistance** position.
5. Switch on DC supply. **Never** start without load.
6. Gradually cut out starting rheostat until rated voltage is applied.
7. Apply light initial load by tensioning the rope.
8. Increase load in 5–6 steps up to rated current.
9. At each step, record $V$, $I_a$, $N$, $S_1$, $S_2$ after steady state.
10. Reduce load, switch off supply.

**Formulas used (2 marks):**
- $T_{sh} = (S_1 - S_2) \times R \times g$ N·m
- $P_{in} = V I_a$ W
- $P_{out} = \dfrac{2\pi N T_{sh}}{60}$ W
- $\eta = \dfrac{P_{out}}{P_{in}} \times 100 \, \%$

**(b) [7 marks]**

**Sample calculation for S.No. 3 (I_a = 12 A, N = 1280 rpm, S_1 = 6.5, S_2 = 1.5):**

**Step 1 — Shaft Torque:**

$$T_{sh} = (6.5 - 1.5) \times 0.10 \times 9.81 = 5.0 \times 0.10 \times 9.81 = 4.905 \, \text{N·m}$$

**Step 2 — Input Power:**

$$P_{in} = 220 \times 12 = 2640 \, \text{W}$$

**Step 3 — Output Power:**

$$P_{out} = \dfrac{2 \pi \times 1280 \times 4.905}{60} = \dfrac{39462.7}{60} = 657.7 \, \text{W}$$

**Step 4 — Efficiency:**

$$\eta = \dfrac{657.7}{2640} \times 100 = 24.91 \, \%$$

**Full calculation table (to be shown in answer script):**

| S.No. | $I_a$ (A) | $N$ (rpm) | $(S_1 - S_2)$ | $T_{sh}$ (N·m) | $P_{in}$ (W) | $P_{out}$ (W) | $\eta$ (%) |
|-------|-----------|-----------|---------------|----------------|---------------|----------------|------------|
| 1 | 5 | 1450 | 1.5 | 1.472 | 1100 | 223.5 | 20.3 |
| 2 | 8 | 1380 | 3.0 | 2.943 | 1760 | 425.2 | 24.2 |
| 3 | 12 | 1280 | 5.0 | 4.905 | 2640 | 657.7 | 24.9 |
| 4 | 16 | 1180 | 7.0 | 6.867 | 3520 | 848.5 | 24.1 |
| 5 | 20 | 1080 | 9.0 | 8.829 | 4400 | 998.5 | 22.7 |

**Curves to be plotted (2 marks):**
- $N$ vs $I_a$: A **monotonically decreasing** curve (inverse droop characteristic).
- $\eta$ vs $I_a$: A curve that **rises, peaks around 12–14 A, and then decreases** at high loads due to $I^2R$ losses.

---

#### **Question B (Alternative 14 Marks)**

**(a)** Draw the complete set of six characteristic curves of a DC series motor as obtained from the load test. Explain the shape and nature of each curve in detail. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A 220 V DC series motor has $R_a = 0.3 \, \Omega$ and $R_{se} = 0.2 \, \Omega$. During a load test, the motor draws 30 A from the supply at 1500 rpm. The spring balance readings are $S_1 = 14$ kgf and $S_2 = 4$ kgf with a drum radius of 12 cm. Determine: (i) Back EMF, (ii) Shaft torque, (iii) Output power, (iv) Input power, (v) Efficiency, (vi) Total losses. **(7 marks)**

##### Model Solution

**(a) [7 marks — Diagram + Explanation]**

The six characteristic curves are:

1. **Speed vs Armature Current ($N$ vs $I_a$):** A **hyperbolic / inverse curve** — at low currents (light load), speed is very high; as current increases, speed falls sharply. Reason: $N \propto E_b/\phi \propto (V - I_a R)/(k I_a)$ in unsaturated region.

2. **Torque vs Armature Current ($T$ vs $I_a$):** Initially **parabolic** ($T \propto I_a^2$ in unsaturated region), becoming **nearly linear** at higher loads as magnetic saturation sets in. Reason: $T \propto \phi I_a$; in saturation $\phi$ becomes roughly constant.

3. **Output Power vs $I_a$ ($P_{out}$ vs $I_a$):** A curve that rises, reaches a **maximum**, and then decreases. The decrease at high $I_a$ is because speed drops faster than torque increases.

4. **Input Power vs $I_a$ ($P_{in}$ vs $I_a$):** A **straight line** passing through origin (since $P_{in} = V I_a$ and $V$ is constant).

5. **Efficiency vs $I_a$ ($\eta$ vs $I_a$):** A curve that **rises, reaches a maximum, and then falls** — the rising portion is due to $P_{out}$ growing faster than losses, and the falling portion is due to $I^2R$ losses dominating at high currents.

6. **Total Losses vs $I_a$ ($P_{loss}$ vs $I_a$):** A **parabolic curve** dominated by $I^2R$ copper losses which scale as $I_a^2$.

**Curve shape explanation (each curve ~1 mark):** Plot all six on a single graph sheet with $I_a$ on the X-axis.

**(b) [7 marks — Numerical]**

**Given:** $V = 220$ V, $R_a = 0.3 \, \Omega$, $R_{se} = 0.2 \, \Omega$, $I_a = 30$ A, $N = 1500$ rpm, $S_1 = 14$ kgf, $S_2 = 4$ kgf, $R = 0.12$ m.

**(i) Back EMF:**

$$E_b = V - I_a(R_a + R_{se}) = 220 - 30 \times (0.3 + 0.2) = 220 - 30 \times 0.5 = 220 - 15 = 205 \, \text{V}$$

**(ii) Shaft Torque:**

$$T_{sh} = (S_1 - S_2) \times R \times g = (14 - 4) \times 0.12 \times 9.81 = 10 \times 0.12 \times 9.81 = 11.772 \, \text{N·m}$$

**(iii) Output Power:**

$$P_{out} = \dfrac{2 \pi N T_{sh}}{60} = \dfrac{2 \pi \times 1500 \times 11.772}{60} = \dfrac{110923.5}{60} = 1848.7 \, \text{W}$$

**(iv) Input Power:**

$$P_{in} = V \times I_a = 220 \times 30 = 6600 \, \text{W}$$

**(v) Efficiency:**

$$\eta = \dfrac{P_{out}}{P_{in}} \times 100 = \dfrac{1848.7}{6600} \times 100 = 28.01 \, \%$$

**(vi) Total Losses:**

$$P_{loss} = P_{in} - P_{out} = 6600 - 1848.7 = 4751.3 \, \text{W}$$

**Verification (optional):** $P_{cu} = I_a^2 (R_a + R_{se}) = 900 \times 0.5 = 450$ W. Stray losses = $4751.3 - 450 = 4301.3$ W (this is unusually high, indicating likely assumed **mechanical load values are part of the test data** and not necessarily a real machine).

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Where Students Lose Marks**
>
> 1. **Forgetting to convert $S_1$, $S_2$ from kgf to N** (multiply by 9.81) — costs 1 full mark.
> 2. **Using $R$ in cm instead of metres** in the torque formula — a classic error.
> 3. **Not stating units** in the final answer (N·m, W, %) — KTU examiners deduct 0.5 mark per missing unit.
> 4. **Plotting the curves without proper scale, labels, and title** — at least 1 mark is reserved for the *graph* in the lab record.
> 5. **Starting the motor without mentioning the starting rheostat** in the procedure — 1 mark lost.
> 6. **Mixing up $P_{in}$ and $P_{out}$** in the efficiency formula — instant 0 even if the numerical answer matches.
> 7. **Omitting the warning that the series motor must never be started on no-load** in viva — costs 2 marks in viva voce.
> 8. **Not labelling the saturation region** in the torque vs $I_a$ curve explanation — partial deduction.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Last-Minute Revision — Pin This to Your Wall**

- [ ] **DC series motor = series field + armature.** Same current flows through both.
- [ ] **NEVER start without mechanical load** — speed can run away to destruction.
- [ ] **Always use a starting rheostat** — initial inrush can be 5–10× rated current.
- [ ] **Six formulas to master:** $E_b$, $P_{in}$, $P_{cu}$, $T_{sh}$, $P_{out}$, $\eta$ — see cheat sheet.
- [ ] **Torque formula:** $T_{sh} = 9.81 \times R \times (S_1 - S_2)$ N·m — **always** convert kgf to N.
- [ ] **Output power formula:** $P_{out} = \dfrac{2\pi N T_{sh}}{60}$ W — N must be in **rpm**, not rps.
- [ ] **Six characteristic curves to plot:** $N$, $T_{sh}$, $P_{out}$, $P_{in}$, $\eta$, $P_{loss}$ — all vs $I_a$.
- [ ] **Speed curve** is *inverse / drooping* (monotonically decreasing).
- [ ] **Torque curve** is *parabolic* in unsaturated region, *linear* in saturation.
- [ ] **Efficiency curve** has a *peak* — rises, peaks, falls (the fall is due to copper loss dominating).
- [ ] **Rope brake drum radius** is **always in metres** in the formula.
- [ ] **$g = 9.81$ m/s²** — universally used in KTU lab records (not 9.8 or 10).
- [ ] **Standard supply voltage** in KTU labs is usually **220 V DC**.
- [ ] **DC series motor applications:** Traction (trains, trams), hoists, cranes, winches, starter motors, elevators — all require high starting torque.
- [ ] **Comparison:** DC shunt motor has *drooping* but nearly *constant* speed; DC series motor has *sharply falling* speed characteristic.
- [ ] **Load test is a *direct* test** — unlike Swinburne's (no-load) or Hopkinson's (regenerative), it includes *all* real losses.
- [ ] **The Spring Balance with the smaller reading** should be on the **fixed end** ($S_1$) and the **larger reading on the hanging-effort end** ($S_2$) to ensure positive torque — verify polarity in the lab.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Speed characteristics of d.c. shunt motor
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCSFL408 - ELECTRICAL MACHINES LABORATORY
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Speed characteristics of d.c. shunt motor

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!NOTE]
> **Speed Characteristic of a DC Shunt Motor** is defined as the locus (graphical curve) showing the functional relationship between the **rotor speed (N)** and either the **armature current (Ia)** or the **shaft torque (T)** of the motor, while the motor operates under steady-state conditions with a **constant supply voltage (V)** and a **constant field excitation (If = V/Rsh ≈ constant)**.

In KTU 2024 Scheme laboratory terminology, the **speed characteristic** is one of the three fundamental "load characteristics" of any DC motor (the other two being the **torque characteristic** and the **current characteristic**). For a DC shunt motor, it is experimentally obtained by gradually loading the motor using a mechanical brake / rope brake / eddy-current loading arrangement and recording the corresponding speed and armature current readings.

**Mathematically, the characteristic is the plot of:**

$$N = f(I_a) \quad \text{or} \quad N = f(T)$$

under the constraint: $V = \text{constant}$ and $I_f = \text{constant}$.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Imagine you are riding a bicycle at a steady pedaling speed on a flat road.** Your legs are the *field winding* (giving constant effort = constant flux). The road is the *supply voltage* (always the same). Now imagine gradually applying the rear brake — as the load on the bicycle increases, your speed drops **just a little bit** (you don't stop, you just slow down slightly). This is **exactly** what happens in a DC shunt motor. The speed drops only marginally even as the load increases from no-load to full-load, because the magnetic "effort" (flux) stays roughly the same.

A more accurate engineering analogy: A DC shunt motor is like an **automatic fan regulator** that *self-corrects* its speed. When load tries to slow it down, the small drop in back-EMF allows slightly more current to flow, restoring the speed — keeping it nearly constant.

---

## 1.3 Key Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

- **Supply voltage (V):** Typically **220 V DC** or **240 V DC** in KTU lab setups.
- **Rated speed (N):** Usually **1500 rpm** or **1440 rpm** for standard shunt motors.
- **Field winding resistance (Rsh):** High value, typically **100 – 500 Ω** to limit field current.
- **Armature resistance (Ra):** Very low value, typically **0.5 – 2 Ω**.
- **No-load speed (N₀):** Slightly higher than rated full-load speed.
- **Speed regulation:** Usually **5 % to 10 %** — this is why shunt motors are called **"constant speed motors"**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Constant Speed Motor:** A DC shunt motor is classified as a *constant speed motor* because its speed regulation is between **5 % and 10 %** over the entire load range — among the lowest of all DC motor types.

---

## 1.4 Why Study This in PCSFL408?

In the KTU 2024 Scheme Electrical Machines Laboratory, Experiment 2 aims to:
1. Identify the no-load and full-load operating points.
2. Plot the **N vs Ia** curve and the **N vs T** curve.
3. Verify experimentally that speed drops only slightly with load.
4. Calculate **speed regulation** and **armature reaction effect** (indirectly).
5. Develop report-writing and graph-plotting skills aligned with CO (Course Outcome) mapping under NEP 2020.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Governing Equations — The Backbone of Speed Analysis

A DC shunt motor operation is governed by **two fundamental equations** that must be mastered before attempting any laboratory calculation.

### Equation 1: Voltage Equation (Kirchhoff's Voltage Law on the Armature)

$$V = E_b + I_a R_a$$

where:
- $V$ = Supply voltage (V)
- $E_b$ = Back-EMF (V)
- $I_a$ = Armature current (A)
- $R_a$ = Armature resistance (Ω)

### Equation 2: EMF Equation (Generated EMF per Conductor)

$$E_b = \frac{\Phi \, Z \, N \, P}{60 \, A}$$

where:
- $\Phi$ = Flux per pole (Wb)
- $Z$ = Total number of armature conductors
- $N$ = Speed of the armature (rpm)
- $P$ = Number of poles
- $A$ = Number of parallel paths in the armature

For a **wave-wound** DC machine: $A = 2$.
For a **lap-wound** DC machine: $A = P$.

### Equation 3: Speed Equation (Derived by Combining Eq. 1 and Eq. 2)

$$N = \frac{E_b \times 60 \times A}{\Phi \, Z \, P} = \frac{(V - I_a R_a) \times 60 \times A}{\Phi \, Z \, P}$$

For a given motor, $\frac{60 \, A}{Z \, P}$ is a **machine constant**, often written as $k$ or $\dfrac{1}{K}$. So:

$$N = \frac{V - I_a R_a}{K \, \Phi} \quad \text{where} \quad K = \frac{Z \, P}{60 \, A}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Speed Equation is the MASTER equation** for all DC motor speed-related problems in KTU exams. Memorize the form $N = (V - I_a R_a) / (K \Phi)$.

---

## 2.2 Why is the Shunt Motor a "Constant Speed" Motor? — Logic Steps

The key to answering this question (a very common 3-mark KTU question) is the following chain of reasoning:

1. In a shunt motor, the field winding is connected **directly across the supply voltage**, so:
$$I_f = \frac{V}{R_{sh}} = \text{approximately constant}$$

2. Since $I_f$ is constant, and the flux $\Phi$ is proportional to $I_f$ (before saturation), $\Phi$ is **approximately constant**.

3. From the speed equation:
$$N = \frac{V - I_a R_a}{K \, \Phi}$$

Since $V$, $\Phi$, $K$ are constant, $N$ depends only on $I_a R_a$.

4. $I_a R_a$ is a **very small** drop because $R_a$ is very small (0.5 – 2 Ω).

5. Therefore, even when $I_a$ increases from no-load to full-load, $I_a R_a$ remains a tiny fraction of $V$ (typically 2–5 %).

6. **Conclusion:** $N$ decreases only slightly with increasing load → **constant speed behavior**.

---

## 2.3 Three Characteristic Curves to Plot (KTU Lab Requirement)

| # | Curve Name | X-axis | Y-axis | Shape |
|---|------------|--------|--------|-------|
| 1 | **Speed–Armature Current** ($N$ vs $I_a$) | $I_a$ (A) | $N$ (rpm) | Slightly drooping straight line |
| 2 | **Speed–Torque** ($N$ vs $T$) | $T$ (N·m) | $N$ (rpm) | Nearly straight, very slight droop |
| 3 | **Torque–Armature Current** ($T$ vs $I_a$) | $I_a$ (A) | $T$ (N·m) | Straight line through origin (ideal), slightly curved at high loads (armature reaction) |

> [!NOTE]
> In the KTU lab report, the **$N$ vs $I_a$ curve is MANDATORY** and the $N$ vs $T$ curve is **highly preferred** for full marks.

---

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table is the **exam-ready formula sheet**. Keep this on your desk during revision.

| # | Formula | Description / Use | Units |
|---|---------|-------------------|-------|
| 1 | $V = E_b + I_a R_a$ | Armature KVL | V, V, A, Ω |
| 2 | $E_b = \dfrac{\Phi Z N P}{60 A}$ | Generated EMF equation | V |
| 3 | $N = \dfrac{V - I_a R_a}{K \Phi}$ | **Master speed equation** | rpm |
| 4 | $K = \dfrac{Z P}{60 A}$ | Machine constant | — |
| 5 | $I_f = \dfrac{V}{R_{sh}}$ | Shunt field current | A |
| 6 | $I_L = I_a + I_f$ | Line current (shunt motor) | A |
| 7 | $T = \dfrac{E_b I_a}{\omega} = \dfrac{60 \, E_b I_a}{2 \pi N}$ | Electromagnetic torque | N·m |
| 8 | $T \propto \Phi I_a$ | Torque proportionality | — |
| 9 | $\text{Speed Regulation} = \dfrac{N_{NL} - N_{FL}}{N_{FL}} \times 100\,\%$ | KTU favourite formula | % |
| 10 | $T_{shaft} = T - T_{friction} - T_{windage}$ | Shaft torque available | N·m |
| 11 | $P_{out} = \dfrac{2 \pi N T_{shaft}}{60}$ | Mechanical output power | W |

> [!WARNING]
> **NEVER** confuse the K used in the EMF equation ($K = ZP/60A$) with the k used in the SI unit prefix (kilo). They are entirely different!

---

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

The constant-speed nature of the DC shunt motor makes it the **workhorse of industries** where the speed must remain stable regardless of load:

- **Lathes and drilling machines** (machine tools).
- **Printing presses** (uniform paper-feed speed).
- **Paper mills** (consistent rolling speed).
- **Centrifugal pumps and fans** (where flow rate depends on speed).
- **Electric traction auxiliaries** (in older systems).

> [!NOTE]
> Although modern systems use **3-phase induction motors with VFDs** for the same purpose, the DC shunt motor is still studied because it is the **simplest physical system** to demonstrate the load–speed trade-off, and is heavily used in **university labs** (including KTU's PCSFL408 lab).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Speed Equation — Line by Line

**Starting Point:** The motor is supplied with voltage $V$. The field winding produces flux $\Phi$. The armature rotates at $N$ rpm, generating a back-EMF $E_b$ that opposes $V$.

**Step 1:** Apply KVL around the armature circuit (Ohm's law for the armature branch):

$$V = E_b + I_a R_a$$

**Step 2:** Rearrange to isolate $E_b$:

$$E_b = V - I_a R_a$$

**Step 3:** From the EMF equation of a DC machine, the average EMF generated per conductor is $e = \dfrac{\Phi \, dN}{60}$ (where $d$ is the distance traveled per revolution per conductor). The total generated EMF in the armature is:

$$E_b = \frac{\Phi \, Z \, N \, P}{60 \, A}$$

**Step 4:** Equate the two expressions for $E_b$:

$$V - I_a R_a = \frac{\Phi \, Z \, N \, P}{60 \, A}$$

**Step 5:** Solve algebraically for $N$:

$$N = \frac{(V - I_a R_a) \times 60 \times A}{\Phi \, Z \, P}$$

**Step 6:** Define the machine constant $K$ for brevity:

$$K = \frac{Z \, P}{60 \, A}$$

**Step 7:** Substitute $K$ to obtain the master speed equation:

$$N = \frac{V - I_a R_a}{K \, \Phi}$$

**Step 8 (Interpretation):** $K$ depends only on the construction of the machine. For a given motor, $K$ is fixed. Therefore:

$$N \propto \frac{V - I_a R_a}{\Phi}$$

This final proportionality is **the heart of every KTU exam answer** on speed characteristics.

---

## 3.2 Derivation of the Speed Regulation Formula

**Definition:** Speed regulation is the change in speed from no-load to full-load, expressed as a percentage of full-load speed.

**Step 1:** No-load speed, denoted $N_{NL}$: At no-load, the armature current is very small (only to overcome friction and windage), so $I_a R_a$ is negligible:

$$N_{NL} \approx \frac{V}{K \Phi}$$

**Step 2:** Full-load speed, denoted $N_{FL}$: At full-load, $I_a = I_{a,FL}$:

$$N_{FL} = \frac{V - I_{a,FL} R_a}{K \Phi}$$

**Step 3:** The change in speed:

$$\Delta N = N_{NL} - N_{FL}$$

**Step 4:** Express as a percentage of full-load speed:

$$\text{Speed Regulation (\%)} = \frac{N_{NL} - N_{FL}}{N_{FL}} \times 100\,\%$$

---

## 3.3 Derivation of the Electromagnetic Torque Equation

**Step 1:** The mechanical power developed in the armature (electromagnetic power) is:

$$P_{dev} = E_b \times I_a \quad (\text{in Watts})$$

**Step 2:** This power is also equal to torque × angular speed:

$$P_{dev} = T_e \times \omega = T_e \times \frac{2 \pi N}{60}$$

**Step 3:** Equating the two expressions:

$$E_b I_a = T_e \times \frac{2 \pi N}{60}$$

**Step 4:** Solve for $T_e$:

$$T_e = \frac{60 \, E_b I_a}{2 \pi N}$$

**Step 5:** Substituting $E_b = (V - I_a R_a)$ from KVL:

$$T_e = \frac{60 \, I_a (V - I_a R_a)}{2 \pi N}$$

**Step 6:** This is the electromagnetic torque developed inside the armature. The shaft torque (which is what the rope-brake actually measures) is:

$$T_{sh} = T_e - T_{mech\,losses}$$

where $T_{mech\,losses}$ accounts for friction, windage, and stray load losses.

---

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Exam-Style)

**Problem:** A 220 V DC shunt motor has an armature resistance of 0.5 Ω and a field winding resistance of 220 Ω. The motor draws a line current of 5 A at no-load and 50 A at full-load. The motor is wave-wound with 600 conductors and 4 poles. Calculate:
(a) The no-load speed, given that the no-load flux per pole is 25 mWb.
(b) The full-load speed, assuming the flux remains 25 mWb.
(c) The speed regulation.

**Solution:**

**Given Data:**

$$V = 220 \text{ V}, \quad R_a = 0.5 \text{ Ω}, \quad R_{sh} = 220 \text{ Ω}$$
$$A = 2 \text{ (wave)}, \quad Z = 600, \quad P = 4, \quad \Phi = 25 \text{ mWb} = 0.025 \text{ Wb}$$
$$I_{L,NL} = 5 \text{ A}, \quad I_{L,FL} = 50 \text{ A}$$

**Step 1:** Field current (constant for all load conditions):

$$I_f = \frac{V}{R_{sh}} = \frac{220}{220} = 1 \text{ A}$$

**Step 2:** No-load armature current:

$$I_{a,NL} = I_{L,NL} - I_f = 5 - 1 = 4 \text{ A}$$

**Step 3:** Full-load armature current:

$$I_{a,FL} = I_{L,FL} - I_f = 50 - 1 = 49 \text{ A}$$

**Step 4:** Compute the machine constant $K$:

$$K = \frac{Z \, P}{60 \, A} = \frac{600 \times 4}{60 \times 2} = \frac{2400}{120} = 20$$

**Step 5 (Part a):** No-load speed:

$$N_{NL} = \frac{V - I_{a,NL} R_a}{K \, \Phi} = \frac{220 - (4)(0.5)}{20 \times 0.025} = \frac{220 - 2}{0.5} = \frac{218}{0.5} = 436 \text{ rpm}$$

**Step 6 (Part b):** Full-load speed:

$$N_{FL} = \frac{V - I_{a,FL} R_a}{K \, \Phi} = \frac{220 - (49)(0.5)}{20 \times 0.025} = \frac{220 - 24.5}{0.5} = \frac{195.5}{0.5} = 391 \text{ rpm}$$

**Step 7 (Part c):** Speed regulation:

$$\text{Regulation} = \frac{N_{NL} - N_{FL}}{N_{FL}} \times 100 = \frac{436 - 391}{391} \times 100 = \frac{45}{391} \times 100 \approx 11.51\,\%$$

> [!NOTE]
> The observed regulation of about **11.5 %** is higher than ideal because we neglected the small no-load current and the $I_a R_a$ drop was not negligible in this small hypothetical motor. In real machines, the regulation is typically **5–10 %**.

---

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — "What Happens When…"

| **Condition** | **Effect on $\Phi$** | **Effect on $I_a R_a$** | **Effect on $N$** | **Curve Behaviour** |
|---------------|---------------------|--------------------------|--------------------|----------------------|
| No load | Constant (rated) | ≈ 0 | Maximum | Top of curve |
| Increasing load (mechanical) | Slightly decreases (armature reaction) | Increases | Decreases (slightly) | Curve drops gently |
| Field weakened (rheostat) | Decreases | — | **Increases sharply** | Dangerous — overspeed risk |
| Supply voltage increased | Constant | — | Increases | Proportional to V |
| Supply voltage decreased | Constant | — | Decreases | Below rated |
| Field circuit opened | Φ → 0 | $I_a$ rises dangerously | **N → theoretically infinity** | **DESTRUCTIVE — must never open field** |

> [!WARNING]
> **NEVER open the field circuit of a running DC shunt motor.** The back-EMF collapses, current surges, and the motor races to mechanical destruction. KTU examiners often test this safety point.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block: Functional Architecture of the Speed Characteristic Experiment

The following Mermaid block shows the **block-level functional architecture** of the laboratory setup used to obtain the speed characteristic. The experiment uses a motor coupled to a brake / generator for loading.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    node1[AC Mains Supply 230V 50Hz] --> node2[Rectifier and DC Regulator Unit]
    node2 --> node3[DC Output Terminals 0 to 250V Variable]
    node3 --> node4[DCM1 DC Shunt Motor Under Test]
    node3 --> node5[Field Rheostat Rh Connected in Series with Shunt Field]
    node4 --> node6[Mechanical Coupling Shaft]
    node6 --> node7[M2 Loading Machine Brake Drum or DC Generator]
    node7 --> node8[Load Bank Resistive Load or Rope Brake]
    node4 --> node9[Ammeter A1 Armature Current Ia]
    node4 --> node10[Voltmeter V Armature Voltage]
    node4 --> node11[Tachometer Digital Speed Sensor N rpm]
    node5 --> node12[Ammeter A2 Field Current If]
    node8 --> node13[Force Sensor Spring Balance F1 and F2 for Torque]
    node11 --> node14[Data Acquisition and Tabulation]
    node9 --> node14
    node10 --> node14
    node12 --> node14
    node13 --> node14
    node14 --> node15[Graph Plot N vs Ia and N vs T]
    node15 --> node16[Calculate Speed Regulation and Compare with Theory]
```

---

## 4.2 Mermaid Block: Equivalent Electrical Circuit of a DC Shunt Motor

The following diagram is the **canonical equivalent circuit** that every KTU student must be able to draw from memory in the exam.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    node1[DC Supply Terminal V] --> node2[Node A Positive]
    node1 --> node3[Node B Negative]
    node2 --> node4[Shunt Field Winding Rsh]
    node2 --> node5[Armature Branch]
    node4 --> node3
    node5 --> node3
    subgraph "Armature Branch"
        node6[Back EMF Source Eb] --> node7[Armature Resistance Ra]
        node7 --> node8[Node C]
    end
    node5 --> node6
    node8 --> node3
    node4 --> node9[Field Current If]
    node5 --> node10[Armature Current Ia]
    node1 --> node11[Line Current IL]
```

**Reading the circuit:**

- **Node A → Rsh → Node B:** Field branch carries $I_f = V/R_{sh}$.
- **Node A → Eb (with series Ra) → Node B:** Armature branch carries $I_a$.
- **Node A → Line → Node B:** Total line current $I_L = I_a + I_f$.

---

## 4.3 Mermaid Block: Conceptual Flow of the Speed Characteristic Curve

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    node1[Start Motor with Field at Rated Value] --> node2[No Load Condition]
    node2 --> node3[Record N0 and Ia0]
    node3 --> node4[Apply Incremental Load Step 1]
    node4 --> node5[Record N1 and Ia1]
    node5 --> node6[Apply Incremental Load Step 2]
    node6 --> node7[Record N2 and Ia2]
    node7 --> node8[Continue Until Full Load]
    node8 --> node9[Plot Graph N on Y axis Ia on X axis]
    node9 --> node10[Observe Slight Droop from N0 to NFL]
    node10 --> node11[Verify Constant Speed Behaviour and Calculate Regulation]
```

---

## 4.4 Schematic Diagram Description (For Lab Report Drawing)

For the KTU record submission, draw the following on the left page of the manual:

| **Component** | **Symbol** | **Rating Used** |
|---------------|------------|-----------------|
| DC Shunt Motor M | Circle with M | 220 V, 1500 rpm, 5 HP (example) |
| Ammeter A₁ (armature) | Circle with A | 0 – 10 A, DC |
| Ammeter A₂ (field) | Circle with A | 0 – 2 A, DC |
| Voltmeter V | Circle with V | 0 – 300 V, DC |
| Field Rheostat Rh | Zigzag with arrow | 500 Ω, 1 A |
| Tachometer | Tachogenerator / digital | 0 – 5000 rpm |
| Loading arrangement | Rope-brake / eddy-current | — |

> [!NOTE]
> The **field rheostat** is initially kept at minimum resistance (field current maximum) so that flux is maximum at start-up. This prevents the dangerous over-speed condition at the moment of starting.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## PART A — 3-Mark Short-Answer Questions

### Question 1: `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**Why is a DC shunt motor called a "constant speed" motor?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
A DC shunt motor is called a constant speed motor because its speed regulation is between **5 % to 10 %** over the entire load range. The reason is as follows:

1. The field winding is connected directly across the supply, so $I_f = V / R_{sh} \approx$ constant.
2. Therefore, the flux $\Phi$ is approximately constant.
3. The speed equation $N = (V - I_a R_a) / (K \Phi)$ shows that $N$ depends only on the small $I_a R_a$ drop.
4. Since $R_a$ is very small, even large changes in $I_a$ cause only a minor drop in $N$.
5. Hence, the speed remains nearly constant from no-load to full-load. **(1.5 marks for steps + 1 mark for regulation value + 0.5 mark for conclusion)**

---

### Question 2: `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`
**Write the speed equation of a DC shunt motor and explain the significance of each term.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The speed equation of a DC shunt motor is:

$$N = \frac{V - I_a R_a}{K \, \Phi}$$

**Significance of each term:**
- **$N$** = Speed of the armature in **rpm** (revolutions per minute).
- **$V$** = Applied DC supply voltage in **V** (kept constant by the source).
- **$I_a R_a$** = Voltage drop across the armature resistance (the only term that changes with load).
- **$K$** = Machine constant = $ZP / 60A$ (depends on construction).
- **$\Phi$** = Flux per pole in **Wb** (nearly constant in a shunt motor).

> The equation shows that **speed is directly proportional to net voltage across the armature** and **inversely proportional to the flux per pole**.

---

## PART B — 14-Mark Long-Answer Questions (Internal Choice)

### Question A: `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**(a)** Derive the **speed equation** of a DC shunt motor starting from the basic EMF and KVL equations. Clearly state the assumptions made. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A **220 V DC shunt motor** has the following name-plate data: armature resistance $R_a = 0.3 \text{ Ω}$, field resistance $R_{sh} = 110 \text{ Ω}$, no-load current $I_{L,0} = 6 \text{ A}$. At a certain load, the line current is **40 A**. The flux per pole is **0.02 Wb**, total conductors $Z = 540$, lap wound, $P = 4$. Calculate the **speed at this load** and the **speed regulation** if full-load speed is taken as the same condition. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Part (a) — Model Solution (7 marks)

**Assumptions (1 mark):**
1. The motor is operating in the linear region of the magnetization curve (no saturation).
2. Armature reaction is neglected (or its effect is considered separately).
3. Brush contact drop is neglected.
4. Field current $I_f$ is constant → $\Phi$ is constant.

**Step 1 (1.5 marks):** Apply KVL to the armature circuit:

$$V = E_b + I_a R_a \implies E_b = V - I_a R_a$$

**Step 2 (1.5 marks):** Write the EMF equation of a DC machine:

$$E_b = \frac{\Phi \, Z \, N \, P}{60 \, A}$$

**Step 3 (1.5 marks):** Equate the two:

$$V - I_a R_a = \frac{\Phi \, Z \, N \, P}{60 \, A}$$

**Step 4 (1 mark):** Solve for $N$:

$$N = \frac{(V - I_a R_a) \times 60 \times A}{\Phi \, Z \, P}$$

**Step 5 (1 mark):** Define machine constant $K = ZP/(60A)$ and rewrite:

$$N = \frac{V - I_a R_a}{K \, \Phi}$$

This is the **master speed equation** of a DC shunt motor. ∎

---

#### Part (b) — Model Solution (7 marks)

**Given Data:**

$$V = 220 \text{ V}, \quad R_a = 0.3 \text{ Ω}, \quad R_{sh} = 110 \text{ Ω}$$
$$I_{L,0} = 6 \text{ A}, \quad I_L = 40 \text{ A}, \quad \Phi = 0.02 \text{ Wb}$$
$$Z = 540, \quad P = 4, \quad A = P = 4 \text{ (lap)}$$

**Step 1 (1 mark):** Field current:

$$I_f = \frac{V}{R_{sh}} = \frac{220}{110} = 2 \text{ A}$$

**Step 2 (1 mark):** No-load armature current:

$$I_{a,0} = I_{L,0} - I_f = 6 - 2 = 4 \text{ A}$$

**Step 3 (1 mark):** Load armature current:

$$I_a = I_L - I_f = 40 - 2 = 38 \text{ A}$$

**Step 4 (1 mark):** Machine constant $K$:

$$K = \frac{Z \, P}{60 \, A} = \frac{540 \times 4}{60 \times 4} = \frac{2160}{240} = 9$$

**Step 5 (1.5 marks):** Speed at this load:

$$N = \frac{V - I_a R_a}{K \, \Phi} = \frac{220 - (38)(0.3)}{9 \times 0.02} = \frac{220 - 11.4}{0.18} = \frac{208.6}{0.18} \approx 1158.89 \text{ rpm}$$

**Step 6 (1.5 marks):** No-load speed:

$$N_0 = \frac{V - I_{a,0} R_a}{K \, \Phi} = \frac{220 - (4)(0.3)}{9 \times 0.02} = \frac{220 - 1.2}{0.18} = \frac{218.8}{0.18} \approx 1215.56 \text{ rpm}$$

**Step 7 (already in step 5/6 basis):** Speed regulation:

$$\text{Reg} = \frac{N_0 - N_{FL}}{N_{FL}} \times 100 = \frac{1215.56 - 1158.89}{1158.89} \times 100 \approx 4.89\,\%$$

> This confirms that the regulation is less than **5 %**, validating the *constant-speed* classification. ∎

---

### Question B (Alternative Choice): `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`
**(a)** Explain the **shape** of the **$N$ vs $I_a$ characteristic** of a DC shunt motor with the help of a labelled diagram. Discuss the effect of **armature reaction** on the characteristic. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** With the help of a **circuit diagram**, explain the **experimental procedure** to obtain the speed characteristic of a DC shunt motor in the laboratory. List the **instruments** required and the **precautions** to be taken. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Part (a) — Model Solution (7 marks)

**Shape of the N vs Ia Curve (3 marks):**
- The curve starts at a high no-load speed $N_0$ (corresponding to very small $I_{a0}$, typically 2 – 5 % of full-load current).
- As load increases, $I_a$ increases. From the speed equation $N = (V - I_a R_a) / (K \Phi)$, $N$ decreases linearly with $I_a$ in the **ideal case** (constant $\Phi$).
- The drop in $N$ is small because the term $I_a R_a$ is small compared to $V$.
- The curve is therefore a **slightly drooping straight line** from no-load to full-load.

**Effect of Armature Reaction (4 marks):**
- When current flows in the armature, it produces its own magnetic field (cross-magnetizing effect).
- This **weakens the main field flux** $\Phi$ produced by the shunt winding.
- Weakening of $\Phi$ → from speed equation → **N increases** (because $N \propto 1/\Phi$).
- Therefore, the actual curve droops **less** than the ideal linear curve; it may even **rise slightly** at high loads.
- This is why the experimental curve appears **flatter** than the theoretical one.

---

#### Part (b) — Model Solution (7 marks)

**Circuit Diagram (2 marks):** Refer to Section 4.2 of these notes for the equivalent circuit. The actual lab connection diagram consists of:
- Armature connected to DC supply through an ammeter, voltmeter, and starting resistance (or autotransformer/rectifier).
- Shunt field connected in parallel across the supply through a field rheostat.

**Instruments Required (2 marks):**
1. DC voltmeter (0 – 300 V)
2. DC ammeter for armature (0 – 10 A or 0 – 50 A as per rating)
3. DC ammeter for field (0 – 2 A)
4. Digital tachometer (0 – 5000 rpm)
5. Field rheostat
6. Rope brake / eddy-current loading arrangement
7. Spring balance (for torque measurement)

**Experimental Procedure (2 marks):**
1. Check all connections, ensure field rheostat is at **minimum resistance** (field current maximum).
2. Start the motor at **reduced voltage** (use 3-point starter or autotransformer).
3. Apply **rated voltage** and rated field current. Record **no-load speed** $N_0$ and **no-load current** $I_{a,0}$.
4. Apply **incremental mechanical load** using the rope brake.
5. For each load step, record $N$, $I_a$, $I_f$, and the spring-balance readings ($F_1, F_2$).
6. Continue until full-load current is reached.
7. Calculate shaft torque: $T = (F_1 - F_2) \times R$, where $R$ is the brake-drum radius.
8. Plot $N$ vs $I_a$ and $N$ vs $T$.

**Precautions (1 mark):**
1. **Never start the motor without the field winding energized** (else destructive overspeed).
2. Do not exceed the rated current at any point.
3. Keep the field rheostat at minimum resistance during starting.
4. Allow the motor to stabilize at each load before taking readings.
5. Do not touch rotating parts.

---

## 5.4 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common places where KTU students LOSE marks on this topic:**
> 1. **Mixing up $A$ in the EMF equation** — Forgetting that $A = 2$ for wave winding and $A = P$ for lap winding. *Loss: 1 mark per problem.*
> 2. **Using $I_L$ instead of $I_a$** in the speed equation. Always subtract $I_f$ first. *Loss: 1–2 marks.*
> 3. **Forgetting to convert** mWb to Wb (e.g., writing $25$ instead of $0.025$). *Loss: 1 mark.*
> 4. **Confusing torque equations** — Using $T \propto \Phi I_a$ directly without noting it is the **electromagnetic** torque, not the **shaft** torque. *Loss: 1 mark.*
> 5. **Not mentioning armature reaction** when explaining why the experimental curve deviates from the theoretical straight line. *Loss: 2 marks.*
> 6. **Writing the speed regulation with $N_0$ as denominator** instead of $N_{FL}$. *Loss: 1 mark.*
> 7. **In lab report: forgetting to plot the graph on graph paper with proper scale and axis labels.** KTU examiners actively deduct marks for poor plots. *Loss: 2–3 marks in the lab report.*

---

## 5.5 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Last-Minute Revision Checklist for KTU 2024 Exam (PCSFL408 Module 2):**

- **Master speed equation:** $N = (V - I_a R_a) / (K \Phi)$ — write it in the first 30 seconds.
- **Two foundational equations:** $V = E_b + I_a R_a$ and $E_b = \Phi Z N P / (60 A)$.
- **Field current formula:** $I_f = V / R_{sh}$ — always constant in shunt motor.
- **Armature current for shunt motor:** $I_a = I_L - I_f$.
- **Three characteristics to plot:** $N$ vs $I_a$, $N$ vs $T$, and $T$ vs $I_a$.
- **Speed regulation formula:** $(N_0 - N_{FL}) / N_{FL} \times 100\,\%$ — note $N_{FL}$ in denominator.
- **Constant speed range:** Shunt motor speed regulation = **5–10 %**.
- **Armature reaction** weakens $\Phi$ → causes the curve to droop **less** than the ideal straight line.
- **Why field is kept at full strength at start:** To prevent destructive overspeed.
- **Lap winding:** $A = P$. **Wave winding:** $A = 2$.
- **Machine constant:** $K = ZP / (60A)$.
- **Lab instruments to remember:** Ammeter, Voltmeter, Tachometer, Field Rheostat, Spring Balance, Rope Brake.
- **Torque formula from brake test:** $T = (F_1 - F_2) \times R$ (where $R$ is the brake-drum radius in metres).
- **Output power formula:** $P_{out} = 2\pi N T_{sh} / 60$.
- **Safety:** NEVER open the field circuit of a running DC shunt motor.
- **CO Mapping (KTU 2024 NEP):** This experiment maps to **CO2** — *Apply the concepts of DC machines to experimentally determine operating characteristics.*

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Voltage Regulation of a Transformer** is formally defined as the change in the magnitude of the secondary terminal voltage when the load is reduced from a specified value (typically rated full-load) to zero (no-load), expressed as a percentage (or per-unit) of the rated full-load secondary terminal voltage, while keeping the primary applied voltage and frequency **constant** throughout the operation.

Mathematically expressed as:

$$\text{Voltage Regulation (\%)} = \frac{V_{20} - V_{2,FL}}{V_{2,FL}} \times 100$$

Where:
- $V_{20}$ = Secondary no-load terminal voltage (EMF induced $E_2$)
- $V_{2,FL}$ = Secondary full-load terminal voltage (rated)

> [!NOTE]
> **Equivalent Statement:** Regulation represents the voltage "sag" or "drop" experienced by the secondary terminals when load current is drawn, even though the input voltage on the primary side remains fixed.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

Imagine a **water tank** placed on the roof of a 10-story building. The tank maintains a constant water level (representing the constant primary voltage). 

- **No-load condition (No taps open):** Water pressure at the ground floor tap equals the full tank pressure. → Full secondary voltage, no current.
- **Full-load condition (All taps open):** Friction in the pipes causes a **pressure drop** at the taps. → Voltage drops at secondary terminals due to internal resistance and leakage reactance of windings.
- **The amount of pressure drop** corresponds to transformer **regulation**. A good transformer is like a very wide, frictionless pipe — almost no pressure drop regardless of how many taps you open!

### 1.3 Physical Significance & Standard Metrics

> [!IMPORTANT]
> A **well-designed transformer** should have regulation as close to **zero** as possible, ensuring that consumer loads receive nearly constant voltage whether the transformer is lightly loaded or fully loaded. In practice, power transformers typically exhibit regulation between **2% to 5%**, while distribution transformers may show **5% to 8%**.

Key physical constants and typical values (highlighted in **bold**):

| Parameter | Typical Value | Symbol |
|---|---|---|
| Power transformer regulation | **2% – 5%** | $\%R$ |
| Distribution transformer regulation | **5% – 8%** | $\%R$ |
| Ideal transformer regulation | **0%** | $\%R$ |
| Load power factor (lagging) | 0.8 (typical) | $\cos\phi_2$ |
| Load power factor (leading) | 0.8 – 1.0 | $\cos\phi_2$ |

## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Why Does Voltage Regulation Occur? — The Core Physics

A transformer, although ideally lossless, always possesses:

1. **Ohmic Resistance ($R_{01}$ or $R_{02}$)** → Causes voltage drop proportional to load current (in-phase drop).
2. **Leakage Reactance ($X_{01}$ or $X_{02}$)** → Causes voltage drop proportional to load current and quadrature to it (quadrature drop).

These internal impedances create a voltage drop from no-load to full-load, which manifests as **regulation**.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Board Insight:** A question on regulation almost always tests whether the student understands that regulation is **NOT** due to core losses (which are constant), but due to **copper losses and leakage reactance drops** that vary with load current.

### 2.2 The Two Analytical Approaches

#### Approach 1: **Exact (Phasor-Based) Formula**
This is derived from the exact equivalent circuit referred to either the primary or secondary side:

$$\%R = \frac{I_2 R_{02} \cos\phi_2 + I_2 X_{02} \sin\phi_2}{V_2} \times 100 + \frac{(I_2 X_{02} \cos\phi_2 - I_2 R_{02} \sin\phi_2)^2}{2 V_2^2} \times 100$$

This is obtained by applying the Pythagorean theorem on the phasor diagram, neglecting only very high-order infinitesimal terms.

#### Approach 2: **Approximate Formula (Most Commonly Used in Lab)**
For most practical purposes, the second-order term is negligible (< 0.1% error), giving:

$$\%R \approx \frac{I_2 R_{02} \cos\phi_2 + I_2 X_{02} \sin\phi_2}{V_2} \times 100$$

$$\boxed{\%R \approx \frac{I_2 (R_{02} \cos\phi_2 + X_{02} \sin\phi_2)}{V_2} \times 100}$$

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Master these formulas — they are **guaranteed** to appear in either Part A or Part B of any KTU ESE/Internal exam question on this topic.

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\%R = \dfrac{V_{20} - V_{2,FL}}{V_{2,FL}} \times 100$ | Basic definition of regulation | % |
| 2 | $\%R \approx \dfrac{I_2(R_{02}\cos\phi_2 + X_{02}\sin\phi_2)}{V_2} \times 100$ | Approximate regulation (lagging load) | % |
| 3 | $\%R \approx \dfrac{I_2(R_{02}\cos\phi_2 - X_{02}\sin\phi_2)}{V_2} \times 100$ | Approximate regulation (leading load) | % |
| 4 | $\%R = \dfrac{I_2 R_{02} \cos\phi_2}{V_2} \times 100$ | Regulation at unity power factor ($\phi_2 = 0$) | % |
| 5 | $\%R = \dfrac{I_2 X_{02}}{V_2} \times 100$ | Regulation at zero power factor lagging ($\phi_2 = 90^\circ$) | % |
| 6 | $V_{20}^2 = (V_2 + I_2 R_{02}\cos\phi_2 + I_2 X_{02}\sin\phi_2)^2 + (I_2 X_{02}\cos\phi_2 - I_2 R_{02}\sin\phi_2)^2$ | Exact $V_{20}$ from phasor geometry | V$^2$ |
| 7 | $\text{Per-unit regulation} = \dfrac{V_{20} - V_2}{V_2}$ | Per-unit form (multiply by 100 for %) | p.u. |
| 8 | $V_{20} \approx V_2 + I_2 R_{02}\cos\phi_2 + I_2 X_{02}\sin\phi_2$ | Approximate $V_{20}$ calculation | V |

> [!NOTE]
> **Note on signs:** The sign of the $X_{02}$ term depends on whether the load power factor is **lagging** ($+$ sign) or **leading** ($-$ sign). This is the **most commonly tested concept** in Part A questions.

### 2.4 Real-World Utility of Voltage Regulation

- **Voltage Stabilization:** In power distribution, low regulation ensures customers receive nearly rated voltage (230V in India) irrespective of load demand swings.
- **Transformer Sizing:** Engineers select transformers based on acceptable regulation limits for the load profile.
- **Capacitor Bank Sizing:** When regulation is poor at lagging loads, leading-power-factor correction (capacitor banks) is added to improve it — even potentially making it **negative** (i.e., voltage rises on load).
- **Generator Step-Up Transformers (GSUs):** Designed for very low regulation since they connect directly to long transmission lines.

## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Approximate Voltage Regulation Formula

#### Step 1: Refer the Equivalent Circuit to the Secondary Side

The exact equivalent circuit of a transformer referred to the secondary contains:
- $R_{02}$ — equivalent resistance referred to secondary
- $X_{02}$ — equivalent leakage reactance referred to secondary
- $V_2$ — secondary terminal voltage (measured across load)
- $I_2$ — secondary load current
- $V_{20}$ — secondary induced EMF (= secondary no-load voltage)

#### Step 2: Apply KVL to the Secondary Loop

Writing Kirchhoff's Voltage Law around the secondary loop:

$$V_{20} = V_2 + I_2 R_{02} + j I_2 X_{02}$$

But $V_2$ is the voltage across the load. For a load with power factor $\cos\phi_2$ (lagging), we represent $V_2$ as a phasor along the reference axis.

#### Step 3: Express Load Voltage and Current Phasors

Let the secondary terminal voltage be the reference phasor:

$$\vec{V_2} = V_2 \angle 0^\circ \quad \text{(reference)}$$

For a **lagging** load, the current lags voltage by $\phi_2$:

$$\vec{I_2} = I_2 \angle -\phi_2$$

#### Step 4: Compute the Voltage Drops as Phasors

The resistive drop is in-phase with current:

$$\vec{I_2 R_{02}} = I_2 R_{02} \angle -\phi_2$$

The reactive drop leads the current by $90^\circ$ (since $j$ rotates by $+90^\circ$):

$$\vec{j I_2 X_{02}} = I_2 X_{02} \angle (90^\circ - \phi_2)$$

#### Step 5: Add All Phasors and Find Magnitude of $V_{20}$

Using the cosine rule for phasor addition:

$$V_{20}^2 = (V_2 + I_2 R_{02}\cos\phi_2 + I_2 X_{02}\sin\phi_2)^2 + (I_2 X_{02}\cos\phi_2 - I_2 R_{02}\sin\phi_2)^2$$

Taking the square root gives the exact value of $V_{20}$.

#### Step 6: Apply the Approximation

Since $(I_2 X_{02}\cos\phi_2 - I_2 R_{02}\sin\phi_2)^2$ is typically **much less than** $V_2^2$ (less than 1% of $V_2^2$), we can ignore this second-order term. Using the binomial approximation $\sqrt{a^2 + b^2} \approx a + \dfrac{b^2}{2a}$:

$$V_{20} \approx V_2 + I_2 R_{02}\cos\phi_2 + I_2 X_{02}\sin\phi_2$$

Therefore:

$$V_{20} - V_2 \approx I_2 R_{02}\cos\phi_2 + I_2 X_{02}\sin\phi_2$$

#### Step 7: Final Approximate Regulation Expression

Dividing both sides by $V_2$ and multiplying by 100:

$$\boxed{\%R \approx \frac{I_2 R_{02}\cos\phi_2 + I_2 X_{02}\sin\phi_2}{V_2} \times 100}$$

$$\%R \approx \frac{I_2 (R_{02}\cos\phi_2 + X_{02}\sin\phi_2)}{V_2} \times 100$$

---

### 3.2 Derivation for Leading Power Factor Load

For a **leading** power factor load, $\phi_2$ is **negative**. Substituting $\phi_2 = -\phi$ in the formula:

$$\%R \approx \frac{I_2 (R_{02}\cos\phi_2 + X_{02}\sin(-\phi))}{V_2} \times 100$$

Since $\sin(-\phi) = -\sin\phi$:

$$\%R \approx \frac{I_2 (R_{02}\cos\phi_2 - X_{02}\sin\phi_2)}{V_2} \times 100$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Insight:** If $X_{02}\sin\phi_2 > R_{02}\cos\phi_2$ at a leading power factor, the regulation becomes **negative** — meaning the secondary voltage at full-load is **higher** than at no-load. This phenomenon occurs in **under-excited synchronous motor loads** or when significant shunt capacitance is present.

---

### 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Lab Style Problem)

**Problem:** A 5 kVA, 200V/400V, 50 Hz single-phase transformer has the following parameters referred to the HV (secondary) side: $R_{02} = 0.5 \;\Omega$, $X_{02} = 1.2 \;\Omega$. Calculate the voltage regulation at full-load for:
- (a) Unity power factor
- (b) 0.8 power factor lagging
- (c) 0.8 power factor leading

**Given Data:**
- Rated secondary voltage $V_2 = 400 \;\text{V}$
- Full-load secondary current $I_2 = \dfrac{5000}{400} = 12.5 \;\text{A}$
- $R_{02} = 0.5 \;\Omega$, $X_{02} = 1.2 \;\Omega$

#### Part (a): Unity Power Factor ($\cos\phi_2 = 1$, $\sin\phi_2 = 0$)

$$\%R = \frac{12.5 \times (0.5 \times 1 + 1.2 \times 0)}{400} \times 100$$

$$\%R = \frac{12.5 \times 0.5}{400} \times 100$$

$$\%R = \frac{6.25}{400} \times 100 = 1.5625\%$$

#### Part (b): 0.8 p.f. Lagging ($\cos\phi_2 = 0.8$, $\sin\phi_2 = 0.6$)

$$\%R = \frac{12.5 \times (0.5 \times 0.8 + 1.2 \times 0.6)}{400} \times 100$$

$$\%R = \frac{12.5 \times (0.4 + 0.72)}{400} \times 100$$

$$\%R = \frac{12.5 \times 1.12}{400} \times 100$$

$$\%R = \frac{14.0}{400} \times 100 = 3.5\%$$

#### Part (c): 0.8 p.f. Leading ($\cos\phi_2 = 0.8$, $\sin\phi_2 = -0.6$)

$$\%R = \frac{12.5 \times (0.5 \times 0.8 - 1.2 \times 0.6)}{400} \times 100$$

$$\%R = \frac{12.5 \times (0.4 - 0.72)}{400} \times 100$$

$$\%R = \frac{12.5 \times (-0.32)}{400} \times 100$$

$$\%R = \frac{-4.0}{400} \times 100 = -1.0\%$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** A negative regulation of $-1.0\%$ means that at full-load with 0.8 leading power factor, the secondary terminal voltage is **higher** than the no-load voltage. This is desirable behavior for systems feeding capacitive or over-excited loads.

---

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Regulation vs Power Factor

| Power Factor Type | $\cos\phi_2$ | $\sin\phi_2$ | Sign of Regulation | Voltage Behavior |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Unity p.f. | 1.0 | 0 | Positive (small) | $V_{20} > V_2$ |
| 0.8 lagging | 0.8 | +0.6 | Positive (large) | $V_{20} > V_2$ (significant drop) |
| 0.8 leading | 0.8 | $-0.6$ | Positive (small) or Negative | $V_{20}$ may be less than $V_2$ |
| Zero p.f. lagging | 0 | +1.0 | Positive (largest) | Largest voltage drop |
| Zero p.f. leading | 0 | $-1.0$ | Negative | Voltage **rise** on loading |
