<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**  
Differentiate between a fuse and a circuit breaker. Why is a circuit breaker preferred in modern power systems even though fuses are cheaper?

**Model Answer:**

A fuse is a **single-use** protective device that melts a calibrated element when current exceeds a safe limit, permanently breaking the circuit. A circuit breaker is a **reusable** mechanical switching device that interrupts the circuit via separable contacts and extinguishes the resulting arc; it can be reclosed manually or automatically after the fault is cleared.

A circuit breaker is preferred because:

1. **Reusability** — No replacement needed after every trip; simply reclose.
2. **Auto-reclosing** — Can be coordinated with auto-reclosers for transient line faults.
3. **Better discrimination** — Adjustable time–current characteristics allow precise grading with other breakers.
4. **Higher breaking capacities** — Fuses are limited in MVA rating, especially at HV.
5. **Indications & auxiliaries** — Provides trip indication, fault recording, and SCADA compatibility.

> **Examiner's note:** A common error is writing "fuses are slow" — fuses are actually *faster* for high currents, but their *single-use* nature and inability to coordinate make them inferior in coordinated systems. (3 marks)

---

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam – June 2024]**  
What is a **Plug Setting Multiplier (PSM)** in an IDMT relay, and what does it physically represent?

**Model Answer:**

The **Plug Setting Multiplier (PSM)** is defined as the ratio of the actual secondary fault current flowing through the relay coil to the pickup current setting of the relay:

$$
\text{PSM} = \frac{I_{f,\text{secondary}}}{I_{\text{pickup,relay}}}
$$

Physically, PSM represents **how many times above pickup the fault current is**. A PSM of 1 means the fault is at the pickup threshold; PSM of 10 means the fault is 10 times the pickup value. It is the *key input* to the IDMT equation that determines the relay's operating time. A higher PSM produces a faster operation because the inverse term $(\text{PSM})^{0.02} - 1$ grows larger. (3 marks)

---

## PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

### Question A (Choice 1)

**[KTU University Exam – May 2024]**  
**(a)** With the help of a neat block diagram, explain the functional blocks of a complete protection scheme. Mention the role of CTs and PTs. **(7 marks)**  
**(b)** A 5 A, 2.5 s IDMT overcurrent relay is connected through a 200/5 CT. The plug setting is 150% and TMS is 0.6. Compute the operating time for a fault current of 5000 A. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution for (a) — 7 Marks

The complete protection scheme consists of the following functional blocks:

1. **Transducer Block (CTs and PTs):**  
   CTs step down the high primary current (hundreds/thousands of A) to a standardized secondary value of **1 A or 5 A**. PTs (Voltage Transformers) step down primary voltage to **110 V secondary**. This isolation protects the relay and standardizes the input range.

2. **Relay Decision Block:**  
   The relay receives the secondary current/voltage and compares it with the pickup setting. It uses electromagnetic induction (electromechanical relay) or DSP-based phasor estimation (numerical relay) to decide whether a fault exists.

3. **Tripping Battery / DC Supply Block:**  
   A separate 110 V DC battery supplies the trip coil. This ensures the breaker can be tripped even when the AC system is dead.

4. **Trip Coil of Circuit Breaker:**  
   When the relay closes its trip contact, current flows from the DC battery through the trip coil, generating magnetic force on the breaker latch.

5. **Circuit Breaker Mechanism:**  
   The latch releases; the stored energy in a spring opens the contacts within 2–3 cycles.

6. **Arc Quenching Chamber:**  
   The arc drawn between separating contacts is extinguished by oil, air, SF₆, or vacuum.

7. **Indications and Annunciations:**  
   A flag, semaphore, lamp, or contact provides visual/SCADA indication of the trip.

8. **Backup / Secondary Protection:**  
   In case the primary relay or breaker fails, a backup relay (typically with a longer time delay) operates.

**Block diagram (already covered in SECTION 4.1) — full credit requires the student to reproduce it in the answer sheet with each block labelled.** (7 marks distribution: 1 mark for each major block, 1 mark for explaining the role of CT/PT explicitly)

#### Model Solution for (b) — 7 Marks

**Step 1 — Relay pickup current at the chosen plug setting:**

$$
I_{pickup,relay} = I_{rated,relay} \times \text{Plug Setting} = 5 \times 1.5 = 7.5 \text{ A (secondary)}
$$

**Step 2 — Fault current referred to CT secondary:**

$$
I_{f,sec} = \frac{5000}{200/5} = \frac{5000}{40} = 125 \text{ A}
$$

**Step 3 — Plug Setting Multiplier:**

$$
\text{PSM} = \frac{I_{f,sec}}{I_{pickup,relay}} = \frac{125}{7.5} = 16.67
$$

**Step 4 — Compute $(\text{PSM})^{0.02}$:**

$$
(16.67)^{0.02} = e^{0.02 \times \ln(16.67)} = e^{0.02 \times 2.8134} = e^{0.05627} \approx 1.0579
$$

**Step 5 — Apply the IDMT operating time equation:**

$$
t_{op} = \frac{0.14 \times \text{TMS}}{(\text{PSM})^{0.02} - 1} = \frac{0.14 \times 0.6}{1.0579 - 1} = \frac{0.084}{0.0579} \approx 1.45 \text{ s}
$$

**Result:** The relay operates in approximately **1.45 seconds**.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. Students often forget to convert fault current to CT secondary first; this yields an erroneously high PSM and very small operating time. **Loss: 2 marks.**
> 2. Plug setting is sometimes applied to *primary* current by mistake. It must be applied to the **relay's rated secondary current** (here 5 A).
> 3. The constant 0.14 is for the *standard inverse* characteristic. If the question specifies *very inverse* or *extremely inverse*, the constant changes (0.06 and 0.02 respectively). Always read the characteristic type.

---

### Question B (Choice 2 — Alternative to Question A)

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**  
**(a)** Explain the construction and working of a **Miniature Circuit Breaker (MCB)**. Mention the two protection mechanisms it uses. **(7 marks)**  
**(b)** With a labelled diagram, explain the construction and arc-quenching process in a **Vacuum Circuit Breaker (VCB)**. List two advantages of VCB over oil circuit breakers. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution for (a) — 7 Marks

**Construction of MCB:**

A Miniature Circuit Breaker is a compact, moulded insulating case (typically bakelite or a thermoset plastic) housing the following components:

1. **Arc chutes / Arc splitter plates** — Steel plates that split and lengthen the arc, cooling it.
2. **Bimetallic strip** — Two metals of different expansion coefficients welded together. On sustained overcurrent, the strip bends and trips the latch.
3. **Magnetic trip coil / solenoid** — A coil carrying the line current; on a short circuit, the large current creates a strong magnetic pull that rapidly opens the contacts.
4. **Operating handle** — Externally accessible lever for manual ON/OFF.
5. **Latch mechanism** — Holds the contacts in the closed position; releases on trip.
6. **Line and load terminals** — For connection to the circuit.

**Working — Two Protection Mechanisms:**

- **Thermal (Inverse-Time) Protection via Bimetallic Strip:** For *sustained overloads* (1.1× to 10× rated current), the bimetallic strip heats up gradually, bends, and mechanically trips the latch. The trip time is **inversely proportional** to the square of the overcurrent.

- **Magnetic (Instantaneous) Protection via Solenoid:** For *short circuits* (typically 5× to 10× rated current and above), the magnetic field produced by the high current pulls a plunger instantly, releasing the latch. Trip time is **< 10 ms**.

This dual mechanism provides both **inverse-time** (for overloads) and **instantaneous** (for short circuits) protection in a single device. (7 marks: 3 for construction, 4 for working of the two mechanisms)

#### Model Solution for (b) — 7 Marks

**Construction of Vacuum Circuit Breaker:**

The VCB has a **vacuum interrupter** (also called a vacuum bottle) as the arc-quenching chamber. The construction is:

1. **Glass or ceramic insulating envelope** — Sealed and evacuated to a pressure of $10^{-6}$ to $10^{-8}$ torr (extremely high vacuum).
2. **Fixed contact** — Attached to one end of the envelope, connected to one terminal.
3. **Moving contact** — Attached to a movable stem, connected to the other terminal, operated by the mechanism through a bellows seal.
4. **Metal vapour condensing shield** — Surrounds the contacts inside the bottle; condenses the metal vapour released during arcing back to the contacts, preventing contamination of the envelope walls.
5. **External operating mechanism** — Spring-operated, providing the energy to separate the contacts in ≈ 1 cycle.

**Arc-Quenching Process:**

When the contacts separate under load, the small contact area results in high current density. Metal vapour is emitted from the contact surface, sustaining a metallic-vapour arc. Because the surrounding medium is a *high vacuum* (extremely low gas density), the arc's ionized metal vapour rapidly **diffuses** outward to the surrounding shield. Within the first **half-cycle** (≈ 10 ms for 50 Hz), the arc extinguishes at the **current zero** because there is no ionized medium to re-strike it. The contacts are then separated enough to withstand the recovery voltage.

**Diagram (refer SECTION 4.3 node D1 — VCB):** A neat, labelled diagram showing the two contacts inside the vacuum bottle, the metal shield, the bellows, and the external mechanism. A cross-sectional view earns full marks.

**Two Advantages of VCB over OCB:**

1. **No Fire Risk / Explosion Hazard:** Oil is flammable and can cause a fire/explosion on internal arc; vacuum has nothing to burn. VCB is intrinsically safe.
2. **Zero Maintenance on the Quenching Medium:** Oil degrades, requires periodic filtration and testing; vacuum is sealed for life (≈ 20–30 years). Operating costs are far lower.
3. *(Bonus, if time permits)* **Silent operation, low contact erosion, fast operation, and no atmospheric pollution** from oil or SF₆.

(7 marks: 3 for construction, 2 for arc quenching, 2 for advantages)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — MCB & VCB Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. Students frequently confuse the *bimetallic* strip with a *solenoid* — remember, **thermal = bimetallic**, **magnetic = solenoid**. Mark loss: 1.
> 2. In VCB, students write "arc is quenched by oil" — that is a circuit breaker error of the highest order. **Always mention the high vacuum** and metal-vapour diffusion.
> 3. The vacuum level ($10^{-6}$ torr) is a frequently asked viva question. Remember it.

---

## TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Print this checklist and keep it for last-minute revision before the lab exam and theory KTU exam.**

- [ ] **Relay vs. Breaker:** Relay *decides*; Breaker *acts*. A relay does NOT interrupt current directly.
- [ ] **CT ratio** standard secondary is **1 A or 5 A**. Pickup current is always referred to the *relay secondary*, not the primary.
- [ ] **PSM = (secondary fault current) / (relay pickup current).** Do NOT skip the secondary conversion.
- [ ] **IDMT Equation:** $t = 0.14 \times \text{TMS} \div [(\text{PSM})^{0.02} - 1]$. Constant 0.14 is for *standard inverse*; use 0.06 for *very inverse*.
- [ ] **As PSM ↑, operating time ↓** (inverse relationship).
- [ ] **TMS** is the *time dial*; it linearly scales the operating time without changing the curve shape.
- [ ] **MCB** has two protection mechanisms: **bimetallic strip (thermal/inverse-time)** for overloads, **solenoid (magnetic/instantaneous)** for short circuits.
- [ ] **VCB** uses **vacuum (≈ 10⁻⁶ torr)** to quench the arc via **metal-vapour diffusion** at current zero. **No oil, no SF₆, no fire risk.**
- [ ] **SF₆ CB** is the workhorse of EHV (66 kV – 1200 kV) due to SF₆'s extraordinary dielectric strength (~3× air) and arc-quenching ability.
- [ ] **Breaking Capacity (MVA) = √3 × V_L × I_brk × 10⁻⁶.**  
- [ ] **Making Capacity = 2.55 × Symmetric Breaking Capacity** (factor 2.55 = √2 × 1.8 for peak and DC offset).
- [ ] **RRRV (Rate of Rise of Restriking Voltage)** is critical for arc re-strike; SF₆ and vacuum have the *lowest* RRRV tolerances.
- [ ] **Numerical Relays** use **Fourier or phasor algorithms** to extract the fundamental component from the fault waveform before decision-making.
- [ ] **Lab observation table must include:** rated current, CT ratio, plug setting, TMS, fault current, observed operating time, calculated operating time, and a graph of TCC (Time–Current Characteristic).
- [ ] **Differential protection** compares $I_1$ vs $I_2$ at the two ends of a protected zone; works for transformers, generators, and busbars.
- [ ] **Distance relay** operates on apparent impedance $Z = V/I$; reach is set as a percentage of line impedance.
- [ ] **Standard frequency** for KTU numericals is **50 Hz** unless stated otherwise.
- [ ] **Viva-favourite question:** "What happens if the CT secondary is left open-circuited?" — Answer: The CT saturates and develops dangerously high voltage on the secondary, endangering personnel and insulation. Always short the CT secondary before disconnecting a relay.
- [ ] **Viva-favourite question:** "Why is SF₆ used in EHV breakers despite being a greenhouse gas?" — Answer: Because no other gas has comparable dielectric strength and arc-quenching ability; its use is strictly controlled and recycled, and the leakage rates are mandated to be below 0.5% per year by IEC 60376.

> **End of Notes — All the best for your KTU 2024 Scheme examinations!**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Study of insulation testing and ground testing
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCSFL408 - ELECTRICAL MACHINES LABORATORY
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Study of insulation testing and ground testing

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Insulation Testing — Formal Definition

**Insulation Testing** is a non-destructive diagnostic procedure used in electrical machines and power systems to evaluate the condition of the dielectric (insulating) materials that separate live conductors from earthed parts and from each other. It is performed by applying a known high DC voltage across the insulation and measuring the very small leakage current that flows through it. The result is expressed as **Insulation Resistance (IR)** in **megaohms (MΩ)** or **gigaohms (GΩ)**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition:** Insulation testing determines the ability of the insulating material (enamel, varnish, mica, paper, epoxy resin, rubber, XLPE) of a winding or cable to withstand the rated voltage without allowing excessive leakage current. It is the **first test** performed on any rewound motor, transformer, or newly installed cable before energisation.

The standard instrument used is a **Megohmmeter**, commonly called a **"Megger"** (a registered trademark of Megger Group, now used generically).

## 1.2 Earth / Ground Testing — Formal Definition

**Earth Testing** (or **Ground Testing**) is the measurement of the resistance offered by an **earth electrode** (a metallic rod, plate, or pipe buried in soil) to the flow of fault current into the general mass of earth. The measured value, called **Earth Electrode Resistance** ($R_e$), is typically required to be **less than 1 Ω** for large power stations and substations, **less than 5 Ω** for industrial installations, and **less than 8 Ω** for domestic installations as per **IS 3043:1987 (Reaffirmed 2018)**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why is low earth resistance critical?** When a live conductor accidentally touches the metal body of an appliance (an earth fault), the fault current must flow safely through the earth electrode and trip the protective device (MCB/RCD). If $R_e$ is too high, the fault current will be too small to trip the breaker, leaving the body **dangerously live** at mains voltage.

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — "The Leaky Bucket" 🪣

Imagine a water tank (the conductor) connected to a tap (the load). Between the tank and the ground, there is a thick rubber sheet (the insulation).

- **Insulation Test** = Sealing the tap, filling the tank, and checking how fast water **leaks through the rubber** to the ground. If the rubber is cracked or old, water leaks quickly → **low IR → bad insulation**. If the rubber is intact, water stays inside for hours → **high IR → good insulation**.

- **Earth Test** = Checking the pipe (earth electrode) that carries this leak-water safely away into the ground. If the pipe is clogged or narrow, water backs up and floods the floor (the appliance body becomes live). A **wide, short, moist pipe** gives **low $R_e$**, draining fault current instantly.

## 1.4 Physical Constants & Standards (Highlighted)

| Quantity | Standard Value | Unit |
|---|---|---|
| Megger test voltage for LV machines | **500 V DC** | Volts |
| Megger test voltage for HV machines/cables | **1000 / 2500 / 5000 V DC** | Volts |
| Minimum acceptable IR (KTU rule of thumb) | **1 MΩ per kV** of rated voltage + 1 MΩ | MΩ |
| Polarization Index (PI) — minimum | **2.0** | ratio |
| Maximum permissible $R_e$ (substation) | **< 1 Ω** | Ohm |
| Maximum permissible $R_e$ (domestic) | **< 8 Ω** | Ohm |
| Reference soil resistivity $\rho$ | **100 (moist) to 1000 (dry) Ω·m** | Ω·m |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Highlight:** The mnemonic **"1 MΩ per kV + 1"** is the most-tested single line on insulation testing. For a 415 V motor: minimum IR = 1 × 0.415 + 1 ≈ **1.4 MΩ**. For an 11 kV transformer: 1 × 11 + 1 = **12 MΩ**.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Megohmmeter (Megger) — Construction & Working

A modern **electronic/digital Megger** consists of four functional blocks:

1. **High-Voltage DC Source:** Generates a stable 500 V / 1000 V / 2500 V / 5000 V DC from internal batteries (hand-cranked in old analog models, electronic in modern ones).
2. **Current-Sensing Amplifier:** Detects the **leakage current** (in nano-amperes to milli-amperes) flowing through the insulation.
3. **Ohmmeter Display:** Computes $R = V/I$ and shows it on an analog scale or digital LCD.
4. **Three Terminals:** **Line (L)**, **Earth (E)**, and **Guard (G)** — the guard terminal bypasses **surface leakage** across the insulation surface so that only **volume leakage** through the body of the dielectric is measured.

> [!NOTE]
> **The Guard Terminal (G):** Surface leakage current (e.g., moisture, dirt film on bushing surface) flows from L → surface → E. By connecting G to a guard electrode around L, this surface current is shunted away and excluded from the measurement, giving a **true** insulation reading. This is critical for transformer bushings and cable terminations.

## 2.2 Types of Leakage Currents in an Insulation

When a DC test voltage is applied, **three types of current** flow through the insulation:

| Current Type | Symbol | Cause | Time Behavior |
|---|---|---|---|
| Capacitive charging current | $I_c$ | Geometric capacitance of winding | Decays in < 1 second |
| Conduction / volume leakage current | $I_v$ | Current through bulk dielectric | **Steady, persists** |
| Absorption current | $I_a$ | Polarization of dielectric molecules | Decays slowly over minutes |

Total current: $I_{total} = I_c + I_v + I_a$

> **Insulation Resistance at any instant:** $R(t) = \dfrac{V_{test}}{I_{total}(t)}$

Because $I_c$ and $I_a$ die away with time, the **measured IR rises** as the test progresses and stabilizes after about **1 minute** — which is why KTU specifies a **1-minute reading** as the standard.

## 2.3 Polarization Index (PI) and Dielectric Absorption Ratio (DAR)

These time-dependent ratios reveal insulation condition far better than a single IR reading:

$$\text{Polarization Index (PI)} = \frac{R_{10\,min}}{R_{1\,min}}$$

$$\text{Dielectric Absorption Ratio (DAR)} = \frac{R_{60\,sec}}{R_{30\,sec}}$$

| PI Value | Insulation Condition |
|---|---|
| **> 4.0** | Excellent |
| **2.0 – 4.0** | Good (KTU minimum acceptable = **2.0**) |
| **1.0 – 2.0** | Questionable / drying needed |
| **< 1.0** | Failed / contaminated / wet |

A PI below 1.0 means IR is *falling* with time → moisture or severe contamination. A high PI (>2) means a healthy, dry, well-polarized dielectric.

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

> [!NOTE]
> This is the **must-memorize** formula set for KTU 2024 Scheme ESE and lab viva.

| # | Formula | Meaning | Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $R = \dfrac{V}{I_L}$ | Ohm's law for insulation (V = test DC voltage, $I_L$ = leakage current) | MΩ, V, µA |
| 2 | $R_{min} = \dfrac{V_{rated\,(kV)}}{1} + 1$ | KTU minimum IR rule (1 MΩ per kV + 1) | MΩ |
| 3 | $PI = \dfrac{R_{10\,min}}{R_{1\,min}}$ | Polarization Index | ratio |
| 4 | $DAR = \dfrac{R_{60s}}{R_{30s}}$ | Dielectric Absorption Ratio | ratio |
| 5 | $R_e = \dfrac{\rho}{2\pi L}\left[\ln\!\left(\dfrac{4L}{d}\right) - 1\right]$ | Resistance of a single **rod electrode** of length $L$, diameter $d$, in soil of resistivity $\rho$ | Ω |
| 6 | $R_e = \dfrac{\rho}{2\pi L}\left[\ln\!\left(\dfrac{8L}{D}\right) - 1\right]$ | Resistance of a **pipe/rod** electrode, $D$ = outer diameter | Ω |
| 7 | $R_e = \dfrac{\rho}{2\pi d}\!\left[\ln\!\left(\dfrac{4d}{a}\right) + \dfrac{a}{4d} - 1\right]$ | Resistance of a **plate electrode**, side $a$ buried at depth $d$ | Ω |
| 8 | $R_e = \dfrac{\rho}{2\pi}\left[\dfrac{1}{L_1} + \dfrac{1}{L_2} + \cdots - \sum \dfrac{1}{d_{ij}}\right]$ | Multiple electrodes in parallel (mutual resistance) | Ω |
| 9 | $\rho = 2\pi a \, R$ | Apparent soil resistivity from **Wenner 4-pin method**, $a$ = probe spacing | Ω·m |
| 10 | $R_{63.2\%}$ = time constant $\tau$ of $R_e$ vs distance curve | Used to identify the true $R_e$ on flat part of Fall-of-Potential curve | Ω |

> [!WARNING]
> **Do not use the vertical bar `\|` symbol** in KTU answer sheets for absolute value. Use parentheses or write "mod". This avoids confusion with stroke currents.

## 2.5 The Fall of Potential Method (Wenner's 3-Pin Earth Test)

This is the **official IS 3043 / IEEE 81** method for measuring $R_e$ of an existing earth electrode (e.g., a substation ground mat).

**Setup:**
- **E** = Earth electrode under test (the one whose $R_e$ we want).
- **P** = Potential probe (auxiliary current return point), driven into soil.
- **C** = Current probe (auxiliary current injection point), driven into soil.
- Typical spacing: **E–P = 20 m, E–C = 40 m** (or any constant ratio, e.g., 61.8 % which gives the **61.8 % Rule**).

**Procedure:** Pass a known AC test current $I$ between E and C using a hand-cranked earth tester (or a digital earth resistance meter). Measure the voltage $V$ between E and P. Then:

$$R_e = \dfrac{V_{EP}}{I_{EC}}$$

The reading on the earth tester is **directly calibrated** to show $R_e$ in ohms.

**61.8 % Rule:** If the distance $X$ from E to P is varied while E–C is held fixed, the measured $R_e$ varies and forms an "S-curve". The true $R_e$ is read where the curve **flattens (plateaus)** — typically at $X = 0.618 \times D_{EC}$ (the **61.8 % principle**), which corresponds to the **62 % simplified rule** of IEEE 80.

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Insulation testing** prevents in-service **phase-to-ground faults**, **winding burnouts**, and **arc-flash incidents** in motors, transformers, generators, and cables. It is mandated by **IS 900, IS 2026, IEC 60076-3** before energisation.
- **Earth testing** ensures protective device coordination, prevents **step-and-touch potentials** from becoming lethal, and is a statutory requirement under the **Central Electricity Authority (CEA) Safety Regulations 2010** and **Indian Electricity Rules 1956**.

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Resistance of a Hemispherical Earth Electrode

Consider a **hemispherical electrode of radius $r_0$** buried flush with the ground surface. Soil resistivity is $\rho$ (Ω·m). Current $I$ leaks radially outward into the infinite soil mass.

**Step 1 — Current density at radius $r$:**

The current spreads over the surface area of a hemisphere of radius $r$, which is $2\pi r^2$.

$$J(r) = \dfrac{I}{2\pi r^{2}}$$

**Step 2 — Electric field intensity at radius $r$ (using $J = E / \rho$):**

$$E(r) = \rho \cdot J(r) = \dfrac{\rho \, I}{2\pi r^{2}}$$

**Step 3 — Voltage between the electrode surface ($r_0$) and infinity:**

$$V = \int_{r_0}^{\infty} E(r)\,dr = \int_{r_0}^{\infty} \dfrac{\rho \, I}{2\pi r^{2}}\,dr$$

**Step 4 — Evaluate the integral:**

$$V = \dfrac{\rho \, I}{2\pi}\int_{r_0}^{\infty} r^{-2}\,dr = \dfrac{\rho \, I}{2\pi}\left[-\dfrac{1}{r}\right]_{r_0}^{\infty}$$

$$V = \dfrac{\rho \, I}{2\pi}\left(0 - \left(-\dfrac{1}{r_0}\right)\right) = \dfrac{\rho \, I}{2\pi r_0}$$

**Step 5 — Apply Ohm's Law, $R_e = V / I$:**

$$R_e = \dfrac{\rho}{2\pi r_0}$$

> **Result:** The resistance to remote earth of a **hemispherical electrode** of radius $r_0$ in soil of resistivity $\rho$ is:

$$\boxed{R_e = \dfrac{\rho}{2\pi r_0} \;\; \Omega}$$

> This shows that **doubling the radius halves the resistance** — hence larger plates give lower $R_e$.

## 3.2 Derivation: Resistance of a Driven Rod Electrode (Babar's Formula)

A vertical rod of length $L$ and diameter $d$ is driven into the soil. Consider the rod as a series of thin hemispherical shells.

**Step 1 — Consider a thin disc of thickness $dx$ at depth $x$.**

For a long thin rod, the resistance contribution of a small element is modeled by the formula for a hemispherical shell of radius $x$:

$$dR = \dfrac{\rho}{2\pi x}\,dx$$

**Step 2 — Integrate from the surface to the tip ($x = L$):**

$$R_e = \int_{0}^{L} \dfrac{\rho}{2\pi x}\,dx = \dfrac{\rho}{2\pi}\left[\ln x\right]_{0}^{L}$$

**Step 3 — Bound the integral** to avoid singularity at $x = 0$ (the rod has finite radius $r_0 = d/2$):

$$R_e = \dfrac{\rho}{2\pi}\int_{r_0}^{L} \dfrac{dx}{x} = \dfrac{\rho}{2\pi}\ln\!\left(\dfrac{L}{r_0}\right)$$

**Step 4 — Substitute $r_0 = d/2$:**

$$R_e = \dfrac{\rho}{2\pi}\ln\!\left(\dfrac{2L}{d}\right) = \dfrac{\rho}{2\pi}\left[\ln\!\left(\dfrac{4L}{d}\right) - 1\right]$$

> **Result (Schwarz's formula for a driven rod):**

$$\boxed{R_e = \dfrac{\rho}{2\pi L}\left[\ln\!\left(\dfrac{4L}{d}\right) - 1\right] \;\; \Omega}$$

**Numerical check:** For $L = 3$ m, $d = 0.025$ m, $\rho = 100$ Ω·m:

$$R_e = \dfrac{100}{2\pi \times 3}\left[\ln\!\left(\dfrac{4 \times 3}{0.025}\right) - 1\right] = 5.305 \times \left[\ln(480) - 1\right] = 5.305 \times (6.173 - 1) \approx 27.4 \;\; \Omega$$

Adding a second identical rod **6 m away** typically reduces the combined resistance to **≈ 15 Ω** (parallel + mutual resistance effects).

## 3.3 Derivation: Wenner's 4-Pin Soil Resistivity Formula

Four equally-spaced probes are driven in a straight line, with spacing $a$ metres between adjacent probes. A current $I$ is passed between the **outer** two probes (1 and 4), and the voltage $V$ is measured between the **inner** two probes (2 and 3).

**Step 1 — Voltage due to probe 1 (current source) at point 2:**

The potential at a point distance $r$ from a hemispherical current source of current $I$ in a soil of resistivity $\rho$ is:

$$V(r) = \dfrac{\rho \, I}{2\pi r}$$

**Step 2 — Total voltage between probes 2 and 3:**

Probe 1 at distance $a$ from P2 injects current $+I$; probe 4 at distance $a$ from P3 injects current $-I$. The voltage at P2 (due to P1) and P3 (due to P4) is:

$$V_2 = +\dfrac{\rho I}{2\pi a}, \qquad V_3 = -\dfrac{\rho I}{2\pi a}$$

**Step 3 — Cross-contributions:** P1 is at distance $2a$ from P3, and P4 is at distance $2a$ from P2. These add:

$$V_{23} = \dfrac{\rho I}{2\pi}\left[\dfrac{1}{a} + \dfrac{1}{2a} - \dfrac{1}{a} - \dfrac{1}{2a}\right]\text{ (after sign consideration)}$$

After careful sign analysis:

$$V_{23} = \dfrac{\rho I}{2\pi}\left[\dfrac{1}{a} + \dfrac{1}{a} - \dfrac{1}{2a} - \dfrac{1}{2a}\right] = \dfrac{\rho I}{2\pi a}$$

**Step 4 — Solve for $\rho$:**

$$\rho = 2\pi a \cdot \dfrac{V_{23}}{I} = 2\pi a R$$

> **Result (Wenner, 1915):**

$$\boxed{\rho = 2\pi a R \;\; \Omega \cdot m}$$

> This formula is valid for $a \ll$ depth of the soil layer being characterized (typically $a = 1, 2, 5$ m).

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Typical KTU-Style Worked Problem

**Problem (KTU Dec 2023 style):** A 3-phase, 11 kV, 500 kVA alternator has its windings tested with a 1000 V Megger. The 1-minute reading is 200 MΩ and the 10-minute reading is 600 MΩ. Comment on the condition of the insulation.

**Solution:**

**Step 1 — Compute minimum acceptable IR using the KTU rule:**

$$R_{min} = \dfrac{V_{rated\,(kV)}}{1} + 1 = 11 + 1 = 12 \;\; M\Omega$$

Measured 1-minute IR = 200 MΩ ≫ 12 MΩ ✓ (passes minimum criterion)

**Step 2 — Compute the Polarization Index:**

$$PI = \dfrac{R_{10\,min}}{R_{1\,min}} = \dfrac{600}{200} = 3.0$$

**Step 3 — Interpret PI:**

PI = 3.0 lies in the range **2.0 – 4.0 → "Good"** insulation, healthy and dry.

**Step 4 — Conclude:** The insulation is in **good working condition** and the alternator may be safely energized.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common student mistakes:** (1) Confusing **PI** with **DAR**; (2) Using *rated voltage* instead of *test voltage* in $R = V/I$; (3) Forgetting the "+1" in the IR rule, which costs **2 marks** in the KTU valuation key.

## 3.5 Worked Earth-Resistance Problem

**Problem:** The resistance of an earth electrode was measured by the Fall of Potential method. Current injected: 5 A. Voltage between E and P: 25 V. Distance E–C = 60 m, E–P = 37 m (≈ 61.6 % of E–C). Find $R_e$ and comment.

**Solution:**

$$R_e = \dfrac{V_{EP}}{I_{EC}} = \dfrac{25}{5} = 5 \;\; \Omega$$

Since the probe spacing is ≈ 61.6 %, the reading lies on the **plateau of the fall-of-potential curve** and is the **true $R_e$** of the electrode.

The value (5 Ω) is acceptable for an **industrial installation** (limit 5 Ω per IS 3043) but **not** for a substation (limit 1 Ω). **Recommendation:** add a second parallel rod electrode and re-test.

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram of a Modern Digital Megohmmeter

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[DC-DC Boost Converter 12V to 5000V] --> B[HV DC Output Terminal L]
    A --> C[Sensing Resistor Rs 100k]
    D[Insulation Under Test] -->|Leakage Current IL| C
    C --> E[Guard Terminal G bypasses surface leakage]
    D --> F[Earth Terminal E of Megger]
    F --> G[Soil Mass Return]
    C --> H[Operational Amplifier I to V]
    H --> I[Microcontroller ADC]
    I --> J[Digital LCD Display MΩ]
    K[Range Selector 500V 1000V 2500V 5000V] --> A
    I --> L[PI Computation Engine R10min divided by R1min]
```

**Figure 4.1** — Functional architecture of a digital insulation tester. The leakage current $I_L$ through the insulation develops a voltage across sensing resistor $R_s$, which is amplified, digitised, and displayed as megohms. The guard terminal G shunts surface leakage current $I_s$ directly to earth terminal E, isolating only the **volume leakage** for true reading.

## 4.2 Insulation Test Connections on a 3-Phase Motor

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph Motor_3phase [Three Phase Induction Motor]
        U1[Terminal U1 Phase R]
        V1[Terminal V1 Phase Y]
        W1[Terminal W1 Phase B]
        Frame[Motor Frame Earthed Body]
    end
    Megger[Megger 500V DC]:::megger
    U1 --> Megger
    V1 --> Megger
    W1 --> Megger
    Frame --> Megger
    classDef megger fill:#fff4cc,stroke:#b8860b,stroke-width:2px
```

**Test Matrix (3 readings recommended by KTU):**

| Test | Line terminal connected to L | Earth terminal connected to E | Reading Name |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | All three phases shorted (U-V-W) | Motor frame | **IR to earth** (most critical) |
| 2 | Phase R only | Phases Y, B shorted to frame | **Phase-to-phase IR** |
| 3 | Phase Y only | Phases R, B shorted to frame | **Phase-to-phase IR** |

## 4.3 Fall-of-Potential Earth Test Layout

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    Earth[Earth Electrode E Under Test]:::earth -->|distance X| Potential[Potential Probe P]
    Earth -->|distance D| Current[Current Probe C]
    Tester[Earth Tester V and I source]:::tester
    Tester -->|Inject I| Earth
    Tester -->|Inject I| Current
    Tester -->|Measure V| Potential
    classDef earth fill:#d4f4dd,stroke:#2e7d32,stroke-width:2px
    classDef tester fill:#cce0ff,stroke:#003366,stroke-width:2px
```

**Figure 4.3** — 3-pin Fall of Potential (Wenner 3-point) earth testing. Variable $X$ is plotted to find the plateau at $X/D \approx 0.618$.

## 4.4 Wenner 4-Pin Soil Resistivity Test Layout

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    C1[Probe 1 Current Plus]:::curr -->|spacing a| P1[Probe 2 Voltage Plus]
    P1 -->|spacing a| P2[Probe 3 Voltage Minus]
    P2 -->|spacing a| C2[Probe 4 Current Minus]:::curr
    Meter[Soil Resistivity Meter]:::meter
    Meter --> C1
    Meter --> C2
    Meter --> P1
    Meter --> P2
    classDef curr fill:#ffd6d6,stroke:#c00,stroke-width:2px
    classDef meter fill:#e1d5ff,stroke:#4a148c,stroke-width:2px
```

**Figure 4.4** — Wenner 4-pin configuration. Outer probes carry test current, inner probes measure voltage. Spacing $a$ should be **≥ 20 × burial depth** of the rod whose resistance you are studying.

## 4.5 Conceptual Map of the Experiment Workflow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start([Begin Experiment]) --> A[Isolate Machine from Supply]
    A --> B[Discharge Windings to Earth for 1 minute]
    B --> C[Select 500V or 1000V Range on Megger]
    C --> D[Connect L to Winding and E to Frame]
    D --> E[Start Megger Crank or Press GO]
    E --> F[Read IR at 30 sec 60 sec 10 min]
    F --> G{PI and IR Acceptable?}
    G -->|Yes| H[Record and Pass]
    G -->|No| I[Troubleshoot Dry Bake or Replace]
    H --> J[Now Move to Earth Test]
    J --> K[Identify Earth Electrode E]
    K --> L[Drive Auxiliary Probes P and C]
    L --> M[Use Earth Tester Fall of Potential]
    M --> N[Plot V versus X curve]
    N --> O[Read Re at Plateau 61.8 percent]
    O --> End([Report Re and Comment])
    I --> End
```

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**Define Insulation Resistance. What is the significance of the "1 MΩ + 1 MΩ/kV" rule?**

**Model Answer:**
Insulation Resistance (IR) is the ohmic resistance offered by the dielectric material of a winding or cable to the flow of leakage current when a DC test voltage is applied.

The **1 MΩ + 1 MΩ/kV** rule states that the **minimum acceptable IR** of any electrical equipment is given by:

$$R_{IR(min)} = \dfrac{V_{rated}}{1000} + 1 \;\; M\Omega$$

where $V_{rated}$ is in volts. It is used as the acceptance threshold during pre-commissioning insulation tests. *(3 marks: definition 1, formula 1, significance 1)*

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**What is the Polarization Index? Why is it a more reliable indicator of insulation health than a single IR reading?**

**Model Answer:**
The Polarization Index (PI) is the **ratio of the 10-minute insulation resistance to the 1-minute insulation resistance**:

$$PI = \dfrac{R_{10\,min}}{R_{1\,min}}$$

A single IR reading is dominated by capacitive charging current and absorption current, which decay over time. PI **eliminates** these transient effects and is therefore a more reliable indicator of the **true volume leakage** through the dielectric. A PI ≥ 2.0 indicates dry, healthy insulation; PI < 1.0 indicates wet or contaminated insulation. *(3 marks: definition 1, formula 1, justification 1)*

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning:** Students commonly lose marks by: (a) forgetting to **discharge windings** before/after the test, (b) using **AC megger** instead of **DC**, (c) omitting the **guard terminal** discussion, and (d) not mentioning **temperature correction** (IR drops by half for every 10 °C rise). Always write the **working principle** of the megger for 3 marks before solving the numerical.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022, Modified]*

**(a)** Explain with a neat block diagram the **construction and working of an electronic megohmmeter**. Discuss the role of the **guard terminal** in eliminating surface leakage. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** The insulation resistance of a 6.6 kV alternator winding is measured as 80 MΩ at the end of 1 minute and 220 MΩ at the end of 10 minutes. Determine the Polarization Index and comment on the condition of the insulation. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Construction & Working (7 marks):**
- **HV DC Source:** A DC-DC boost converter (or hand-cranked generator in analog megger) produces a stable 500/1000/2500/5000 V DC.
- **Three terminals: L (Line), E (Earth), G (Guard).** L is connected to the conductor, E to the body/earth, G to a guard electrode.
- **Working:** When the test voltage is applied, three currents flow — capacitive ($I_c$, dies in ms), absorption ($I_a$, dies in minutes), and conduction ($I_v$, steady). The steady $I_v$ corresponds to the **true** IR.
- **Guard terminal:** Surface leakage $I_s$ flows from L across the insulation surface to E. By connecting G to a guard ring around L, $I_s$ is **shunted directly to E** through the guard lead, **bypassing** the measuring circuit. The measured current is therefore only the **volume leakage** $I_v$, giving a true reading.
- **Display:** $R = V/I$ is computed and shown on the analog scale or digital LCD.

*(Block diagram carries 2 marks; explanation 3 marks; guard terminal 2 marks.)*

**(b) Numerical (7 marks):**

$$PI = \dfrac{R_{10\,min}}{R_{1\,min}} = \dfrac{220}{80} = 2.75$$

PI = 2.75 lies in the range **2.0 – 4.0 → "Good"** insulation.

Check the minimum IR criterion:
$$R_{min} = \dfrac{6600}{1000} + 1 = 7.6 \;\; M\Omega$$
Measured 1-min IR = 80 MΩ ≫ 7.6 MΩ ✓

**Conclusion:** The alternator insulation is in **good condition** and may be safely energized for service. *(Calculation 3 marks, interpretation 2 marks, IR rule 2 marks.)*

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice** — *[KTU University Exam – May 2023, Modified]*

**(a)** Derive an expression for the **earth resistance of a driven rod electrode** of length $L$ and diameter $d$ in soil of resistivity $\rho$. State the assumptions used. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** The earth resistance of a substation earth electrode was measured by the **Fall of Potential method**. The distances E–C = 50 m, E–P = 30 m, current injected = 4 A, voltage measured = 8 V. Calculate $R_e$ and comment on its suitability. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Derivation (7 marks):** *(as in Section 3.2)*

Assumptions:
1. Soil is **homogeneous and isotropic** with constant $\rho$.
2. The electrode is a **thin vertical cylinder**.
3. Current radiates **uniformly** into the infinite soil mass.
4. End (tip) effects are **neglected** compared to the cylindrical surface.

Final result:
$$R_e = \dfrac{\rho}{2\pi L}\left[\ln\!\left(\dfrac{4L}{d}\right) - 1\right] \;\; \Omega$$

*(Integrand setup 2 marks, integration 2 marks, logarithmic simplification 2 marks, assumptions 1 mark.)*

**(b) Numerical (7 marks):**

$$R_e = \dfrac{V}{I} = \dfrac{8}{4} = 2 \;\; \Omega$$

Probe ratio: $\dfrac{X}{D} = \dfrac{30}{50} = 0.60 \approx 61.8 \;\%$

Since the probe spacing is at the **61.8 % plateau**, the reading represents the **true** $R_e$ of the electrode.

**Comment:** For a substation, IS 3043 requires $R_e < 1$ Ω. The measured value of 2 Ω is **NOT acceptable**. **Recommendation:** Drive two more parallel rod electrodes (≥ 6 m apart) to bring combined resistance below 1 Ω.

*(Calculation 2 marks, plateau justification 2 marks, IS standard 2 marks, recommendation 1 mark.)*

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember 🚀

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Last-Minute KTU Revision Checklist — print this and tape to your lab record!**

- ☐ **1 MΩ/kV + 1 MΩ** rule → minimum IR. *Memorise the +1.*
- ☐ Megger always uses **DC** voltage (500 V for LV, 1000 V+ for HV).
- ☐ Always **discharge** the winding for ≥ 1 minute **before and after** the test.
- ☐ **PI = R₁₀ₘᵢₙ / R₁ₘᵢₙ** → ≥ 2.0 is good, < 1.0 is failed.
- ☐ **DAR = R₆₀ₛ / R₃₀ₛ** → ≥ 1.6 is good, < 1.0 is failed.
- ☐ Guard terminal G **bypasses surface leakage**, isolating volume leakage.
- ☐ Rod electrode resistance: $R_e = \dfrac{\rho}{2\pi L}\!\left[\ln\!\left(\dfrac{4L}{d}\right) - 1\right]$.
- ☐ Hemispherical electrode: $R_e = \dfrac{\rho}{2\pi r_0}$.
- ☐ Wenner 4-pin: $\rho = 2\pi a R$, with probe spacing $a$.
- ☐ Fall of Potential: 3 pins E, P, C; $R_e = V_{EP}/I_{EC}$.
- ☐ **61.8 % Rule:** true $R_e$ is read at the plateau, i.e., E–P = 0.618 × E–C.
- ☐ IS 3043 limits: < 1 Ω (substation), < 5 Ω (industrial), < 8 Ω (domestic).
- ☐ Temperature correction: IR is **halved** for every **10 °C rise** in temperature.
- ☐ **NEVER** perform megger test on electronic circuits, surge arresters, or capacitors (they will be destroyed by the DC test voltage).
- ☐ Use **AC earth tester** for ground resistance (DC would cause polarization errors), and **DC megger** for insulation (DC avoids capacitive reactance issues).
